Avoid repeated high-output attempts. Expected outcome: The unit should accept reasonable technique settings and proceed to prep/exposure. If the issue only occurs at high technique settings, document the specific settings and escalate for generator/tube evaluation.

steps.title: Allow Tube Cooling Time
steps.instructions: Tube heat protection can inhibit exposure until the tube cools. Ask whether the unit was used for repeated exposures or high-output exams. Check for tube heat warnings, cooling messages, or exposure delay indicators. Allow the unit to sit idle for an appropriate cooling period. Do not force repeated exposure attempts. Expected outcome: If tube heat was the cause, exposure readiness should return after cooling. If cooling clears the issue, document tube heat/excessive exposure demand and return the unit only after confirming normal operation.

steps.title: Listen for Rotor Prep Behavior
steps.instructions: A rotor-related fault may occur when the tube rotor does not reach proper speed or the prep sequence fails. In a safe service area, initiate prep according to site policy. Listen for normal rotor spin-up sound from the tube housing. Note whether the fault occurs during prep before exposure is allowed. Listen for abnormal grinding, buzzing, weak spin-up, repeated cycling, or no rotor sound. Expected outcome: Rotor prep should sound consistent and the system should reach ready state. If rotor sound is abnormal or absent, stop testing and remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Inspect the Tube Head and Collimator Externally
steps.instructions: External damage can point toward a deeper tube, cable, or mechanical issue. Inspect the tube head, collimator, handles, arm, and cable routing. Look for evidence of impact, loose covers, damaged cable jackets, oil leakage, unusual smell, heat, or physical misalignment. Check that the tube head moves normally and is not binding or stressing cables. Expected outcome: Tube head and cable areas should be physically intact with no signs of overheating, leakage, or damage. If damage, oil, burning smell, or unusual heat is found, remove the unit from service immediately.

steps.title: Check for Error Pattern After Reboot
steps.instructions: A reboot may clear a temporary software or readiness state, but recurring generator or tube faults should not be ignored. Power the unit down normally. Wait briefly, then restart the system. Observe whether the same fault returns at boot, during prep, or only during exposure. Do not repeatedly cycle power if the same high-voltage, generator, rotor, or tube fault returns. Expected outcome: A temporary inhibit may clear and remain cleared. If the fault returns, treat it as a persistent equipment problem requiring repair evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Functional Check Only If Safe
steps.instructions: Only perform a test exposure if the unit is away from patient care, shielding requirements are met, and local radiation safety procedure allows it. Use approved test setup and technique. Verify normal prep, exposure, audible/visual indicators, and image acquisition behavior if applicable. Confirm no repeat fault occurs. Expected outcome: The unit completes prep and exposure normally without generator, rotor, or tube fault. If the fault reappears, stop testing and remove the unit from service.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the GE AMX mobile X-ray system would prep but exposure was inhibited with a generator/rotor/tube fault during portable exams.
CCR.Cause: Verified the unit powered on, AC charging was normal, technique settings were reasonable, and the fault returned during rotor prep, indicating a likely internal tube rotor or generator readiness issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the unit from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the fault behavior, and routed the system for bench/vendor repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Battery charge level at time of failure
Whether the fault occurred on AC, battery, or both
Exact fault message or code displayed
Whether the unit failed during prep or exposure
Technique settings used when fault occurred
Tube heat or cooling messages observed
Rotor sound normal, abnormal, or absent
Any unusual sound, smell, heat, oil, or physical damage
Whether reboot temporarily cleared the issue

title: GE Healthcare AMX Series Mobile X-Ray System - Drive assist, brake, or steering problem
description: Drive assist, brake, or steering issues caused by power, battery, wheel, floor, control, or transport-related problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot movement problems while the mobile X-ray system is near a patient, bed, stretcher, or crowded clinical area. Move patients, staff, and obstacles away from the unit. If the unit cannot be moved safely, secure the area and notify the department. The unit is stable, patient care is not affected, and there is no risk of unintended movement. If this resolves the immediate safety concern, stop and document the condition.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is with drive assist, braking, steering, or general movement resistance. Ask staff what happened: drive assist not engaging, unit hard to push, brake will not release, brake will not hold, steering feels locked, loose, or uneven, unit pulls to one side, or problem occurs only on ramps, thresholds, or certain floors. The symptom is clearly identified before parts or internal faults are suspected.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge and Power Status
steps.instructions: Many mobile X-ray drive systems rely on adequate battery charge and proper system power. Check the battery level, power indicators, and whether the unit is fully booted. Plug the unit into a known-good outlet if charge is low. The unit has enough power to support drive assist, brake release, and control functions. If low battery caused the drive or brake issue, charge the unit and verify normal movement before returning it to service.

steps.title: Verify the Unit Is Fully Powered On
steps.instructions: Some movement functions may be disabled if the system is off, partially booted, or in a standby condition. Power the unit on fully and allow it to complete startup. Check for warning messages, inhibit messages, or drive-related alerts. The unit reaches a normal ready state without drive, brake, or motion warnings.

steps.title: Inspect the Area Around the Wheels
steps.instructions: Physical obstruction is a common external cause of movement problems. Check all wheels and casters for debris, tape, cable wrap, linen, syringe caps, floor mats, damaged tread, and objects wedged near the wheel assembly. Wheels rotate freely with no visible obstruction. If debris is found and removed, retest movement in a safe open area. If operation is normal, stop and document.

steps.title: Check for Brake Engagement or Parking Brake Issues
steps.instructions: A brake that is partially engaged can make the unit feel like the drive system or steering has failed. Confirm the brake control is fully released. Listen or feel for brake release if the model provides noticeable feedback. Attempt gentle movement only in a safe area. The brake releases fully and the unit moves normally. If the brake remains engaged or will not hold when applied, remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Test Drive Assist in an Open, Level Area
steps.instructions: Drive assist problems should be tested away from patients and obstacles. Move the unit to a clear, level hallway or service area. Test forward and reverse drive assist at low speed. Drive assist engages smoothly, responds consistently, and stops when the control is released. If drive assist is intermittent, delayed, surging, or not responding, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Handle, Deadman, or Drive Control Operation
steps.instructions: Drive assist may not activate if the handle switch, trigger, grip sensor, or control input is not being recognized. Inspect the control handle for obvious damage, sticking, looseness, cracked plastics, liquid residue, or signs of impact. Controls move normally and return to their neutral positions. If a control is physically damaged or sticking, remove the unit from service for repair.

steps.title: Verify Neutral Position and Control Release
steps.instructions: A drive control that does not return to neutral can create unsafe movement behavior or drive inhibition. Release the drive controls and confirm they return fully to the rest position. Power cycle the unit only if safe and allowed by site procedure. Controls return to neutral and any motion inhibit clears. If the unit continues to show abnormal drive behavior, do not keep testing in a clinical area.

steps.title: Inspect Steering Movement
steps.instructions: Steering issues may be mechanical, wheel-related, or caused by uneven floor contact. Slowly move the unit in a safe area and observe whether steering is excessively stiff, loose or wandering, pulling to one side, binding at certain angles, or uneven over thresholds. Steering is smooth and predictable. If steering binds, pulls heavily, or feels unstable, remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Check Floor and Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: The unit may behave differently on ramps, damaged flooring, elevator thresholds, or wet surfaces. Compare movement on a clean, dry, level floor versus the area where the problem was reported. Movement is consistent across normal flooring. If the issue only occurs in one location, document the floor condition and notify facilities if needed.

steps.title: Look for Error Messages or Indicator Lights
steps.instructions: Drive, brake, and steering problems may be associated with system warnings. Check the operator display for active alerts, fault codes, inhibit messages, or service indicators. Record the exact message before power cycling. Any displayed fault is documented clearly for repair escalation.

steps.title: Check for Recent Impact or Transport Damage
steps.instructions: Mobile X-ray systems are frequently moved through tight spaces and may be damaged by door frames, elevators, beds, or wall impacts. Inspect the base, wheel area, handle, column, covers, and lower chassis for fresh damage. No signs of impact, cracked covers, bent components, or loose assemblies are present. If damage is found, remove the unit from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Movement Check
steps.instructions: Only continue if the unit appears mechanically safe and no severe fault is active. In a safe open area, verify forward movement, reverse movement, turning left and right, brake release, brake hold, and stop response when controls are released. The AMX moves, steers, brakes, and stops predictably. If the unit passes testing, return it to service and document the checks performed.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the GE AMX mobile X-ray system was difficult to steer and the drive assist would not consistently engage during transport.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found debris wrapped around the front caster and the unit had low battery charge, causing inconsistent movement assistance.
CCR.Resolution: Removed debris, charged the unit, verified brake release, steering, forward and reverse drive assist, and returned the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Battery charge level at time of inspection
Whether the unit was plugged in or running on battery
Exact drive, brake, or steering symptom reported
Whether the issue occurred on level floor, ramp, elevator, or threshold
Brake release and brake hold behavior
Any displayed fault or warning message
Any burning smell, heat, or visible damage
Whether the unit pulled to one side
Environmental factors such as wet floors, damaged flooring, ramps, or elevator thresholds
Indicator lights, power indicators, or service warnings observed

title: GE Healthcare AMX Series Mobile X-Ray System - Image Transfer, Cassette/Detector, or Workstation Communication Failure
description: Image transfer, cassette/detector recognition, or workstation communication issues preventing images from reaching review or PACS systems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot image transfer or detector communication problems during an active patient exam if repeat exposure risk is present. Remove the mobile X-ray unit from patient use if images are not transferring, the detector is not recognized, or exposures may need to be repeated. Use another mobile X-ray system if imaging is clinically urgent. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without unnecessary delay or avoidable repeat radiation exposure. Why it matters: Communication failures can lead to missing images, repeated exposures, delayed diagnosis, or incomplete exam documentation.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly where the failure occurs. Ask clinical staff what happened: Did the detector fail to connect? Did the exposure complete but no image appeared? Did the image appear locally but fail to send? Did the image reach the workstation but not PACS? Did an error message appear? Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to detector connection, image acquisition, workstation transfer, or network/PACS communication. If the problem is only with PACS transfer but images are stored locally, do not assume the detector or generator failed.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Detector or Cassette Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Verify that the detector, cassette, or image receptor belongs with the unit and is approved for that system configuration. Check the detector ID, cassette labeling, and assigned room/unit if applicable. Confirm staff did not swap detectors between mobile units. Expected outcome: The AMX system is being used with the correct detector or cassette. If the wrong detector was used, return the correct detector to the unit, repeat detector recognition if required, and stop if communication is restored.

steps.title: Check Detector Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: For wireless detector configurations, confirm the detector has adequate battery charge. Check detector battery indicators or workstation detector status. Install a known-charged detector battery if available. Seat the battery fully and inspect for obvious latch or contact issues. Expected outcome: The detector powers on and becomes available to the mobile unit. If the detector reconnects after battery replacement or reseating, document the battery condition and stop.

steps.title: Inspect Detector or Cassette for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Check for obvious external damage before continuing. Inspect the detector/cassette for cracks, bent corners, loose covers, damaged handles, fluid exposure, or signs of impact. Check connector areas if a tethered or docking connection is used. Expected outcome: No visible physical damage is present. If damage is found, remove the detector from service and send it for repair evaluation. Do not continue using a damaged detector for patient imaging.

steps.title: Verify Detector Seating, Docking, or Cable Connection
steps.instructions: If the AMX configuration uses a docking bay, tether, reader, or cassette interface, check all external connections. Reseat the detector or cassette. Confirm the detector is fully docked or inserted. Inspect external cables for loose connections, bent pins, damaged strain reliefs, or broken latch points. Swap with a known-good external cable if available and appropriate. Expected outcome: The detector/cassette is recognized by the system. If reseating or cable replacement resolves the issue, document the failed connection or cable and stop.

steps.title: Restart the Image Acquisition Application
steps.instructions: A software session may be frozen even when the mobile unit itself is powered. Close and reopen the image acquisition software if accessible. Log out and back in if the workflow allows. Avoid deleting exams or images unless following site policy. Expected outcome: The acquisition workstation reconnects to the detector and resumes normal workflow. If the detector or image queue returns after restarting the application, verify image transfer with a test or non-patient workflow and stop.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Mobile X-Ray Unit Safely
steps.instructions: If the detector and connections appear normal, perform a controlled restart. Confirm no patient exam is in progress. Shut down the AMX system through the normal shutdown process. Wait briefly, then power the system back on. Observe startup for detector, workstation, or network errors. Expected outcome: The unit boots normally and communication is restored. If the issue clears after restart, document it as an intermittent communication/software lockup and monitor for recurrence.

steps.title: Check Local Image Storage or Exam Queue
steps.instructions: Determine whether images are being captured but not transferred. Check whether the completed images are visible locally on the acquisition workstation. Review the send queue, pending queue, failed queue, or exam list if available. Look for failed sends, retry prompts, or unsent studies. Expected outcome: You can tell whether acquisition failed or transfer failed after acquisition. If images are stored locally but not sending, focus on network, destination, AE title, worklist, or PACS communication rather than detector hardware.

steps.title: Verify Network Connection
steps.instructions: Mobile X-ray units often depend on wireless or wired network connection for worklist and image transfer. Check wireless signal status or wired network connection if docked. Confirm the unit is in an area with known wireless coverage. Move the unit closer to a known-good wireless area if appropriate. Check whether other mobile X-ray units are transferring images successfully. Expected outcome: The AMX has active network connectivity. If transfer works in another area, document possible wireless coverage or network access issue and notify IT/PACS support as needed.

steps.title: Confirm Worklist and Patient Data Communication
steps.instructions: If the issue involves missing patients or failed worklist lookup, separate worklist communication from image transfer. Check whether the unit can query the modality worklist. Confirm date, time, accession number, patient ID, and exam selection are correct. Compare with another working mobile unit if available. Expected outcome: Worklist communication is confirmed or identified as failed. If worklist is unavailable but image transfer still works manually, document the issue as a worklist communication problem rather than detector failure.

steps.title: Check Destination or PACS Transfer Status
steps.instructions: If images acquire locally but do not reach PACS, verify destination communication. Check send status on the workstation. Retry sending the study if site policy allows. Confirm the correct destination is selected. Ask PACS/IT whether PACS, DICOM routing, wireless network, or storage destination issues are occurring. Expected outcome: The failure is identified as local device communication or downstream PACS/network issue. If multiple imaging devices are affected, escalate to IT/PACS rather than treating the AMX as the primary failure.

steps.title: Check for Date, Time, or System Identification Issues
steps.instructions: Incorrect system time or identification can interfere with worklist matching, image routing, and exam reconciliation. Check date and time displayed on the unit. Confirm the unit identity, location, or configured destination has not changed unexpectedly. Do not alter network or DICOM configuration unless authorized. Expected outcome: The unit has correct time, identity, and exam routing behavior. If configuration appears incorrect, escalate to imaging IT, PACS administrator, or service support.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Detector or Workflow
steps.instructions: If available, use a known-good detector, cassette, battery, or test workflow to isolate the fault. Use only approved site procedures and compatible components. Compare behavior using: Known-good detector battery, known-good detector/cassette, known-good network location, known-good test exam workflow. Expected outcome: The issue follows either the detector/cassette, the mobile unit, or the network/workstation path. If the fault follows the detector, remove the detector from service. If the fault stays with the AMX unit, remove the unit from service for bench evaluation. If the fault follows network location or routing, escalate to IT/PACS.

steps.title: Review Error Messages and Logs Without Deep Service Action
steps.instructions: Record any displayed communication, detector, workstation, image transfer, or DICOM errors. Photograph or write down the exact error message. Record when the error occurs: At startup, during detector connection, after exposure, during image processing, during send to PACS, during worklist query. Expected outcome: Repair, PACS, or vendor support has useful details for troubleshooting. Do not perform deep internal service, board replacement, or configuration changes unless trained and authorized for that GE AMX configuration.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the GE AMX mobile X-ray system completed exposure but images did not transfer to the workstation/PACS.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the detector was recognized locally, but completed studies remained in the failed send queue due to network/PACS communication failure.
CCR.Resolution: Verified detector battery, reseated external connections, restarted the acquisition workstation, confirmed local image storage, documented failed transfer behavior, and escalated to PACS/IT while removing the unit from service pending verification.

helpfulDetails: Docking bay or tether cable reseated
Image appeared locally or did not appear locally
Whether other mobile units were affected

title: GE Healthcare Discovery XR656 Radiographic System - Detector Not Connecting or Not Detected
description: Detector not connecting, not detected, or unavailable for image acquisition due to power, seating, connection, battery, or communication issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the detector during active patient positioning or when the room is being used for an exam. Stop the exam if imaging cannot continue safely. Move the patient away from the table, wall stand, or detector area if needed. Use another room or detector if the exam is urgent. Do not force the detector into the table or wall bucky. Expected outcome: The patient is safe, and troubleshooting is performed without delaying care or risking detector damage.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Detector Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the system truly does not detect the detector or if the detector is only unavailable for a specific exam setup. Ask staff what message appeared and when it occurred. Confirm whether the issue happens at system startup, when selecting the detector, when placing the detector in the table bucky, when placing the detector in the wall stand, during portable/free-detector use, or during image acquisition. Check whether the console shows the detector as unavailable, disconnected, offline, or not ready. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before parts or accessories are blamed.

steps.title: Check Detector Power or Battery Status
steps.instructions: A wireless or removable digital detector may not be detected if the battery is discharged or not fully seated. Remove the detector from the bucky or holder if safe. Check for detector indicator lights. Confirm the detector battery is installed correctly. Replace the detector battery with a known charged battery if available. Allow the detector a moment to power up and reconnect. Expected outcome: The detector powers on and appears available at the system console. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the discharged or improperly seated battery.

steps.title: Reseat the Detector
steps.instructions: A detector may not be recognized if it is not fully seated in the table bucky, wall stand, or charging/connection position. Remove the detector carefully. Inspect for obvious obstruction, debris, misalignment, or improper orientation. Reinsert the detector fully into the intended position. Confirm it latches or seats normally. Check the console to see if the detector status changes to ready or available. Expected outcome: The system detects the detector after proper seating. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document that the detector was not fully seated.

steps.title: Inspect the Detector and Bucky Contacts
steps.instructions: Poor contact at the detector interface can prevent charging, communication, or detector recognition. Inspect the detector contact area for visible contamination, damage, bent pins, debris, or fluid residue. Inspect the table or wall stand detector interface for obvious damage. Do not scrape contacts or use unapproved cleaners. If cleaning is allowed by local procedure, clean only external accessible surfaces using approved methods. Expected outcome: No obvious physical damage or contamination is present. If contact damage, fluid intrusion, or bent pins are found, remove the detector or system from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Detector Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Some rooms may have multiple detectors, and the system may be configured for specific detector IDs. Confirm the detector belongs to the Discovery XR656 room. Check the detector label, asset tag, or assigned room marking. Ask staff whether detectors were recently swapped between rooms. Try the correct assigned detector if another detector was mistakenly used. Expected outcome: The room is using the correct detector assigned or configured for that system. If this resolves the issue, stop and document that the incorrect detector was being used.

steps.title: Check Detector Charging Dock or Storage Location
steps.instructions: If the detector battery is not charging correctly, the detector may appear dead or fail to connect. Verify the detector charging station or storage slot has power. Check for charge indicator lights. Try a known working battery if available. Confirm the detector or battery was not left out of the charger for an extended time. Inspect the charging contacts for obvious damage or debris. Expected outcome: The detector battery is confirmed charged or the charging issue is identified.

steps.title: Restart the Detector Connection Workflow
steps.instructions: The detector may need to be reselected or refreshed at the workstation after a temporary communication drop. Exit the current exam only if clinically appropriate and local workflow allows. Reopen the exam or select the detector again. Confirm the proper receptor is selected at the console. Wait for the detector status to update. Expected outcome: The detector reconnects and becomes available without deeper service intervention.

steps.title: Power Cycle the System Safely
steps.instructions: A controlled restart can clear temporary workstation or detector communication issues. Confirm no patient exam is active. Follow the normal shutdown process for the operator console/system. Wait for the system to fully power down. Restart the system. Confirm the detector status after startup. Expected outcome: The detector is detected after a clean system restart. If this resolves the issue, document the temporary communication failure and successful restart.

steps.title: Check for Network or Communication Clues
steps.instructions: Detector detection may depend on internal room communication, wireless association, or system-side communication paths. Check whether the issue affects only the detector or the entire imaging workstation. Confirm the console is operating normally. Check for network or communication warnings on the system. Ask whether any network, room, or IT changes occurred recently. Confirm other room functions are normal, such as exam selection, image review, and worklist access. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to the detector path, detector battery/interface, or broader system communication.

steps.title: Try a Known Working Detector or Battery Only If Approved
steps.instructions: Do not randomly swap detectors unless the department’s configuration and policy allow it. Use a known good battery if compatible and available. Use another detector only if it is assigned, compatible, and approved for that system. Do not force pairing or configuration changes without proper service authorization. Record which detector and battery were tested. Expected outcome: The problem follows either the detector/battery or remains with the room/system. If the issue follows the detector, remove the detector from service. If the issue remains with the room, escalate the radiographic system for service.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: A dropped or damaged detector may fail to connect even if the battery and system appear normal. Inspect the detector housing for cracks, dents, separation, loose parts, or signs of impact. Check for unusual heat, odor, fluid exposure, or rattling. Ask staff whether the detector was dropped, pinched, struck, or exposed to fluid. Do not continue using a detector with suspected physical damage. Expected outcome: Damaged detector hardware is identified before further clinical use. If damage is found, label the detector Out of Service and send it for evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the GE Discovery XR656 detector was not detected at the console and could not be used for imaging.
CCR.Cause: Detector battery was discharged and the detector did not power on when inserted into the table bucky.
CCR.Resolution: Installed a known charged detector battery, reseated the detector, confirmed detector ready status at the console, and returned the room to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact detector message shown at the console
Detector location tested: table, wall stand, or free detector use
Whether detector indicator lights were present
Whether the detector was fully seated
Contact condition at detector and bucky interface
Whether another battery or approved detector was tested
Any recent drop, impact, fluid exposure, or physical damage
Whether a full system restart was performed
Any communication warnings, network changes, or workstation behavior

title: GE Healthcare Discovery XR656 Radiographic System - Image Not Sending to PACS or Worklist Not Updating
description: Troubleshooting PACS send failures, missing studies, delayed image transfer, or worklist refresh issues caused by network, configuration, or workflow problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient exam is complete or that imaging can safely continue before troubleshooting the Discovery XR656. Do not troubleshoot network or image-transfer issues during active patient positioning if it delays care. Confirm images are available locally at the acquisition workstation. If the system cannot send images but imaging is clinically urgent, notify radiology staff and follow downtime workflow. Use another room or portable system if patient care would be delayed. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely while image transfer or worklist issues are investigated. Why it matters: PACS and worklist problems can delay reporting, but troubleshooting should not interrupt safe patient imaging.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is with image sending, worklist updating, or both. Ask staff what they are seeing: Images stuck in queue. Send failed message. Study not visible in PACS. Modality worklist not refreshing. Patient not appearing on the worklist. Wrong patient or accession information. Check whether the problem affects one exam, multiple exams, or all exams. Confirm if other radiographic rooms are sending normally. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to PACS transfer, worklist communication, patient/exam data, or a larger network problem. If the issue is isolated to one patient or one accession, focus on order/data issues first.

steps.title: Confirm Images Are Stored Locally
steps.instructions: Verify the acquired images are still present on the Discovery XR656 workstation. Open the local study list or image review area. Confirm the completed exam and images are visible. Check for unsent, pending, failed, or queued images. Do not delete or reprocess images unless the department’s workflow allows it. Expected outcome: Images are confirmed to be locally available and can potentially be resent. If the images are missing locally, stop and escalate according to imaging downtime or data recovery policy.

steps.title: Check the Send Queue or Network Queue
steps.instructions: Look for failed, pending, or stuck DICOM transfers. Open the system’s send queue, transfer queue, or network status area if accessible. Look for failed destinations, repeated retries, or stuck studies. Note the PACS destination name and any displayed error text. Attempt a resend only if local workflow permits and patient/study information is correct. Expected outcome: Failed studies either resend successfully or provide useful error behavior for documentation. If resend works, confirm image receipt in PACS and stop.

steps.title: Verify Network Connection at the Room
steps.instructions: Check the easiest physical network causes before assuming a software or PACS issue. Inspect the network cable at the acquisition workstation, wall jack, and network drop. Confirm the cable is fully seated and not damaged. Check for link lights at the network port if visible. Verify no one moved the workstation, unplugged a cable, or connected it to the wrong jack. Do not randomly move network cables between ports without knowing the assigned network drop. Expected outcome: The workstation has a stable physical network connection. If a loose cable or disconnected jack is found and corrected, retry worklist refresh or PACS send.

steps.title: Check Power and Status of Related Network Equipment
steps.instructions: Confirm nearby network-dependent components are powered and normal. Check the acquisition workstation, system cabinet, monitor, and any visible network hardware in the room. Look for abnormal power loss, error indicators, or recently restarted components. Confirm the workstation is not frozen or partially booted. Ask staff if there was a recent power outage, room shutdown, or network maintenance. Expected outcome: No obvious power or startup issue is affecting communication. If the workstation is frozen or partially responsive, follow approved restart workflow or escalate before forcing shutdown.

steps.title: Verify Modality Worklist Search Criteria
steps.instructions: A worklist may appear “down” when the search filters are too narrow or incorrect. Check date range, modality, location, scheduled station, patient name, and accession filters. Refresh the worklist using a broad search if allowed. Confirm the patient order exists in the RIS/EHR. Compare the patient’s scheduled location to the XR656 room location. Expected outcome: Valid orders appear once correct filters and location criteria are used. If the order appears after correcting filters, the device is functioning and the issue is workflow/search-related.

steps.title: Confirm Patient and Accession Data
steps.instructions: Incorrect or incomplete exam data can prevent successful PACS association or routing. Review patient name, MRN, accession number, exam type, and date of birth. Confirm the exam was started from the correct worklist entry when possible. Check whether the exam was manually entered instead of selected from the worklist. Ask imaging staff whether the order was changed, cancelled, merged, or reordered. Expected outcome: Patient and accession data match the active order. If the image was acquired under incorrect data, follow radiology/PACS correction workflow. Do not guess or manually alter patient data outside approved policy.

steps.title: Compare With Other Modalities or Rooms
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is local to the Discovery XR656 or part of a larger system problem. Ask if other x-ray rooms, CT, MRI, or portable units are sending to PACS. Ask if other rooms are receiving worklist. Contact radiology IT/PACS support if multiple systems are affected. Check whether there is a known PACS, RIS, interface, or network outage. Expected outcome: The problem is identified as either device-specific or enterprise/network-wide. If multiple systems are affected, escalation should go to PACS/RIS/network support rather than treating the XR656 as failed.

steps.title: Check Destination Selection
steps.instructions: Images may fail to send if routed to the wrong destination or if the expected destination is disabled. Confirm the selected send destination is the correct PACS/archive destination. Check whether the exam was sent to a secondary destination, local storage, CD/export queue, or QA destination instead of PACS. Review any available destination status indicators. Avoid changing DICOM destination settings unless authorized. Expected outcome: Images are routed to the correct PACS destination. If the wrong destination was selected and the correct destination resend succeeds, confirm image receipt and stop.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Worklist Refresh
steps.instructions: Refresh the worklist without disrupting active patient workflow. Confirm no active acquisition is in progress. Refresh the worklist from the normal user interface. Search by broad date range or specific patient if allowed. If the order still does not appear, confirm the order exists in RIS/EHR and is assigned to the correct modality/location. Expected outcome: Worklist updates with expected scheduled exams. If worklist remains unavailable but other network functions work, suspect RIS/worklist server, AE title, scheduled station mapping, or interface issue.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Resend Test
steps.instructions: Use an existing completed study or approved test workflow to confirm PACS communication. Select a completed study that is allowed to be resent. Send to the correct PACS destination. Watch for immediate failure, pending status, or successful completion. Have PACS/radiology staff confirm whether the study arrived. Expected outcome: The study transfers successfully and appears in PACS. If the queue shows success but PACS cannot find the image, suspect routing, reconciliation, patient matching, or PACS-side issue.

steps.title: Restart Only When Appropriate
steps.instructions: A restart may clear a software communication hang, but it should be done carefully. Confirm the room is not actively imaging a patient. Verify all acquired images are saved locally. Notify radiology staff before restarting the acquisition workstation or system. Use the approved shutdown/restart process. After restart, recheck worklist and PACS send. Expected outcome: Network services reload and normal communication returns. If restart resolves the issue, document the behavior and final successful send/worklist update.

steps.title: Escalate Configuration or DICOM Issues
steps.instructions: If basic checks pass, the issue may involve DICOM configuration, AE title, IP address, port, routing rules, firewall rules, RIS mapping, or PACS interface behavior. Do not randomly change AE titles, IP addresses, ports, or destination settings. Capture the exact error message and destination name. Record whether worklist, send, or both are affected. Escalate to PACS/radiology IT, network support, or GE service as appropriate. Expected outcome: The issue is escalated with enough information to avoid unnecessary troubleshooting delays.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the GE Discovery XR656 was not sending completed images to PACS and the modality worklist was not updating.
CCR.Cause: Images were present locally, but the DICOM send queue showed failed transfers and the room network connection was found loose at the workstation.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the network cable, confirmed link status, refreshed the worklist, resent the pending study to PACS, and verified image receipt with radiology staff.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue affected PACS send, worklist, or both
Exact error message or queue status
Network cable and wall jack condition
Whether the order existed in RIS/EHR
Alarm behavior or displayed system communication messages
Accessories or cables swapped during troubleshooting
Power behavior, restart behavior, or frozen workstation behavior
Environmental factors such as recent power outage, room shutdown, or network maintenance
Indicator lights, link lights, queue status, or destination status indicators
Whether PACS/radiology IT or GE service was contacted

title: GE Healthcare Discovery XR656 Radiographic System - Table, Wall Stand, or Tube Stand Positioning Fault
description: Troubleshooting positioning faults involving table movement, wall stand alignment, tube stand travel, locks, sensors, obstruction, or system readiness on a GE Healthcare Discovery XR656 radiographic system.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop all table, wall stand, and tube stand movement before troubleshooting. Do not troubleshoot while a patient is on the table unless movement has stopped and the patient is safe. If positioning is unreliable, transfer the patient to another room or complete the exam using another system if clinically necessary. Watch for pinch points, collision risk, unexpected motion, and patient fall risk. Expected outcome: The patient and staff are protected before mechanical or positioning checks begin.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Positioning Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm which part of the system is affected. Ask staff whether the issue involves patient table, wall stand / bucky, tube stand / overhead tube crane, auto-positioning, manual movement, lock release, detent positioning, tracking or alignment. Confirm whether the fault happens during startup, positioning, exposure setup, or after a collision/obstruction. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to the affected section instead of treating it as a general room failure.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstruction or Collision Risk
steps.instructions: Radiographic room positioning faults are often caused by something blocking normal movement. Inspect around the table base, tabletop path, wall stand travel path, and tube stand rail path. Remove stools, step platforms, cables, detector holders, positioning aids, linens, trash cans, or other items that may block movement. Check whether the tube, collimator, table, wall stand, or detector housing is close to a collision point. Expected outcome: If an obstruction was preventing travel, remove it, reset the system if needed, and retest movement. If resolved, return the system to service.

steps.title: Verify Table Locks and Movement Controls
steps.instructions: A table may appear faulty if brakes, locks, or positioning controls are not releasing correctly. Confirm the table is powered and responsive. Check that tabletop locks release when the proper control is activated. Verify longitudinal, lateral, vertical, and float movements as applicable. Listen for abnormal grinding, clicking, or motor strain. Stop testing immediately if movement is jerky, uncontrolled, or unsafe. Expected outcome: The table should move smoothly within normal limits and lock securely when released.

steps.title: Check Wall Stand / Bucky Positioning
steps.instructions: Wall stand faults may be caused by lock issues, detector seating, travel obstruction, or failure to reach a recognized position. Confirm the wall stand moves through its expected range without obstruction. Check that the bucky or detector tray is fully seated. Confirm locks engage and release correctly. Verify the wall stand reaches expected detent or alignment positions. Look for loose accessories, cassette/detector tray interference, or foreign objects in the travel path. Expected outcome: The wall stand should move smoothly, lock securely, and be recognized in position by the system.

steps.title: Check Tube Stand / Overhead Tube Crane Movement
steps.instructions: Tube stand positioning faults can occur when travel locks, detents, rails, column rotation, or tube angulation are not recognized. Verify the tube stand moves freely along the ceiling rails or floor track as designed. Check transverse and longitudinal travel. Check tube angulation and rotation locks. Confirm the tube stand locks and unlocks normally. Inspect for obvious rail obstruction, loose cable drape, or accessory interference. Do not force movement if the stand binds or will not release. Expected outcome: The tube stand should move smoothly, stop securely, and report a valid position to the system.

steps.title: Confirm Detector and Bucky Selection
steps.instructions: Some positioning or alignment faults can occur when the selected image receptor does not match the physical setup. Confirm the correct receptor is selected: table bucky, wall stand, wireless detector, free exposure mode, if applicable. Verify the detector is properly inserted, docked, or selected. Check that the tube is aligned to the selected receptor. Confirm SID and centering are reasonable for the exam setup. Expected outcome: The selected receptor should match the physical position of the tube and patient setup.

steps.title: Check Auto-Positioning or Tracking Mode
steps.instructions: Auto-positioning can fail if one section of the system does not know its current position or if a safety condition is active. Disable auto-positioning if safe and try manual positioning. Confirm whether the fault occurs only in auto-positioning or also during manual movement. Check for messages related to tracking, detent, SID, collision avoidance, or position not reached. Do not repeatedly command auto-movement if the system stops due to a safety warning. Expected outcome: If manual movement works but auto-positioning fails, document that distinction for service evaluation.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Workstation and Room System Only If Safe
steps.instructions: A position fault may clear after system reset if caused by a software state or temporary communication issue. Make sure no patient is on the table and all moving parts are in a safe position. Follow the site-approved shutdown and startup process. Allow the system to fully initialize. Watch for startup errors related to table, wall stand, tube stand, detector, or position sensing. Expected outcome: If the system initializes normally and positioning functions return, perform a safe functional check before returning to service.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: Before returning the room to use, confirm normal operation of the affected positioning function. Test table movement and locks. Test wall stand travel and lock engagement. Test tube stand travel, angulation, and lock release. Confirm the system recognizes valid positions. Confirm no abnormal sound, smell, heat, binding, or intermittent fault is present. Expected outcome: The system should position smoothly, lock securely, and show no recurring positioning fault.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology staff reported that the GE Discovery XR656 tube stand would not move into position and displayed a positioning fault during exam setup.
CCR.Cause: Checked room for obstructions, verified detector selection, table and wall stand movement, and confirmed the tube stand lock released but the positioning fault returned during travel.
CCR.Resolution: Removed system from service, labeled room Out of Service, documented repeated tube stand positioning fault, and escalated to imaging service for further evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Which component was affected: table, wall stand, tube stand, or auto-positioning
Whether the fault occurred during startup, manual movement, auto-positioning, or exposure setup
Any displayed error message or fault code
Whether the room was occupied or patient in use
Whether movement was smooth, slow, jerky, or completely inhibited
Whether detector/bucky selection matched the exam setup
Whether power cycle cleared or did not clear the fault
Any unusual sounds, heat, smell, binding, or collision concerns

title: GE Healthcare Discovery XR656 Radiographic System - Exposure Inhibited Due to Generator, Tube, or Safety Interlock Issue
description: Exposure inhibited conditions caused by room safety interlocks, positioning status, detector readiness, generator faults, or tube-related protection conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not continue attempting exposures while the system is inhibited. Stop the exam if exposure is blocked. Move the patient off the table or wall stand if troubleshooting will delay care. Use another radiographic room or mobile X-ray unit if imaging is urgent. Do not bypass door switches, emergency stops, collision sensors, or exposure safety interlocks. Expected outcome: The patient is protected before troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: Exposure inhibit conditions are designed to prevent unsafe or incomplete X-ray operation.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the system is truly inhibiting exposure or whether the issue is workflow-related. Ask the technologist when the inhibit occurs: During prep. During exposure attempt. After positioning. After protocol selection. After detector selection. Check the operator console for messages such as generator fault, tube heat, exposure blocked, interlock open, detector not ready, or system not ready. Record the exact message if displayed. Expected outcome: The failure mode is confirmed before parts or service escalation are considered.

steps.title: Check Room Emergency Stops and Safety Switches
steps.instructions: Verify that no emergency stop or room safety device is active. Check all emergency stop buttons in the room and control area. Confirm no E-stop is physically pressed or latched. Reset only if safe and allowed by site procedure. Verify room doors are fully closed if the room uses door interlocks. Check that no table, wall stand, tube stand, or ceiling suspension safety stop is active. Expected outcome: If an active E-stop or open interlock caused the inhibit, clearing it should restore system readiness. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm System Ready Status
steps.instructions: Make sure the Discovery XR656 is fully ready before attempting exposure. Verify the system completed startup without errors. Confirm the console, generator, tube stand, table or wall stand, and detector are online. Look for warning icons, red indicators, or unavailable status messages. Restart the workstation only if the system is not in clinical use and site procedure allows. Expected outcome: The system should show ready status before prep or exposure is attempted.

steps.title: Verify Exam, Protocol, and Technique Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm the selected exam setup is valid. Check that an exam and protocol are selected. Verify kVp, mA, mAs, exposure time, focal spot, and AEC selections are reasonable for the selected receptor. Confirm the selected technique is not outside system limits. Change to a known-good basic technique if appropriate for testing without a patient. Expected outcome: If the inhibit was caused by an invalid or incomplete technique setup, correcting the selection should restore exposure readiness.

steps.title: Check Detector and Image Receptor Selection
steps.instructions: Exposure may be inhibited if the selected detector is not ready or does not match the selected position. Confirm the correct detector or receptor is selected. Verify whether the exam is using the table, wall stand, or wireless detector. Make sure the detector is awake, charged, connected, and properly registered to the system. If using a wireless detector, confirm it is not powered off, asleep, or assigned to another room/system. Try a known-working detector if available and approved by site workflow. Expected outcome: If detector readiness caused the inhibit, selecting or reconnecting the correct receptor should allow exposure. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Table, Wall Stand, and Tube Positioning
steps.instructions: The system may block exposure if tube, detector, or patient support positioning is not valid. Confirm the tube is aligned to the selected receptor. Check source-to-image distance if the system requires a valid position. Verify the bucky, wall stand, or table detector is seated correctly. Confirm the tube stand, table, and wall stand are not in a collision, limit, or unlocked position state. Move the tube and receptor to a simple known-good position if safe. Expected outcome: If a positioning or alignment condition caused the inhibit, correcting the geometry should restore readiness.

steps.title: Check Collimator and Field Light Status
steps.instructions: Some systems may inhibit or warn if collimator communication or positioning is abnormal. Verify the collimator powers on. Check whether the field light works. Confirm the collimator moves normally and is not physically jammed. Look for collimator communication or blade-position errors. Expected outcome: Normal collimator function supports exposure readiness. A failed or unresponsive collimator may require service escalation.

steps.title: Check Exposure Hand Switch or Control Console Input
steps.instructions: Rule out a simple activation-control issue. Inspect the exposure hand switch for damage, stuck buttons, or a loose connector. Confirm prep and expose stages feel normal if using a two-stage switch. Try a known-good exposure switch if available and compatible. Check whether the console exposure button behaves differently than the hand switch, if applicable. Expected outcome: If the hand switch or exposure control caused the issue, replacing or reseating it should restore normal prep/exposure behavior.

steps.title: Check Generator Ready and Error Status
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the generator is reporting a fault or not reaching ready state. Review the console for generator fault messages. Listen for abnormal generator behavior during prep, such as repeated clicking, failed rotor prep, or no ready indication. Do not open generator covers or attempt internal high-voltage troubleshooting. Record the exact generator error message or code. Expected outcome: If the generator does not reach ready after external checks, the issue likely requires qualified imaging service evaluation.

steps.title: Check X-Ray Tube Heat or Tube Protection Status
steps.instructions: Exposure may be inhibited if the tube is overheated or under a protection lockout. Ask whether multiple high-technique exposures were recently performed. Check the console for tube heat, tube cooling, rotor, focal spot, or tube protection messages. Allow the tube to cool if the system indicates heat-related lockout. Do not continue repeated exposure attempts during a tube heat warning. Expected outcome: If tube heat caused the inhibit, the system should return to ready after adequate cooling. If tube errors return immediately, escalate.

steps.title: Check Door, Room, and External Interlock Status
steps.instructions: Confirm that facility interlocks are not preventing exposure. Verify control booth door and exam room door status if tied into the system. Check for any room warning light, exposure lockout, or interlock relay issue. Ask Facilities or Imaging Service whether recent room work, door work, electrical work, or construction occurred. Do not bypass safety interlocks. Expected outcome: If an external room interlock is open, correcting the facility-side condition should restore exposure readiness.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: A controlled restart may clear a temporary software or communication state. Confirm no patient is on the system. Follow site-approved shutdown and startup procedure. Allow the system to fully boot and complete self-checks. Recheck for system-ready status. Expected outcome: If the inhibit was caused by a temporary communication fault, the system may return to normal operation. If the same error returns, continue escalation.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Non-Patient Function Check
steps.instructions: After correcting an external issue, verify the system safely. Use site-approved test procedures. Confirm system ready status. Confirm detector readiness. Verify that prep and exposure functions respond normally. Do not perform unnecessary radiation exposures outside site policy. Expected outcome: The system should complete normal prep and exposure workflow without inhibit messages.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the GE Discovery XR656 would not expose and displayed an exposure inhibited condition during chest exam setup.
CCR.Cause: Found the selected wall stand detector was not ready because the system was assigned to the table receptor while the tube was positioned for the wall stand.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected receptor selection, verified detector ready status, confirmed normal prep and exposure workflow, and returned the room to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact console message or error code
Whether the inhibit occurred during prep or exposure
Table, wall stand, or wireless detector selected
Hand switch or console exposure button behavior
Any unusual clicking, arcing sound, odor, heat, or failed rotor sound

title: GE Healthcare Discovery XR656 Radiographic System - Collimator Light, Centering, or Field Size Issue
description: Collimator light, field size, or centering issues caused by setup, SID, positioning, accessory, or collimator alignment problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot collimator alignment or field size problems during an active exposure or while a patient is positioned for imaging. Move the patient away from the table, wall stand, or imaging area if troubleshooting is needed. Confirm the room is safe before activating the collimator light. Do not perform test exposures unless clinically approved and radiation safety procedures are followed. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is performed without unnecessary patient exposure or positioning risk. Why it matters: Collimator and centering issues can affect radiation field accuracy, image quality, repeat imaging, and patient dose.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what clinical staff observed. Ask whether the issue involves: Collimator light not turning on; Light field not matching expected image field; Field size changing unexpectedly; Centering light or crosshair misaligned; Collimator blades not opening or closing correctly; Auto-collimation not working as expected. Expected outcome: The reported symptom is clearly identified before testing. If the issue was user setup-related and cannot be reproduced, document findings and return the system to service if all checks pass.

steps.title: Check Room and System Power Status
steps.instructions: Verify that the radiographic system is powered on normally and not in a standby or fault condition. Confirm the operator console, overhead tube crane, wall stand, table, and generator are powered normally. Check for active system messages, interlocks, or warnings. Verify the room is not in emergency stop, exposure inhibit, or safety lockout condition. Expected outcome: The system is fully powered and ready for positioning and collimator operation. If system power or room readiness is abnormal, correct that issue first and retest.

steps.title: Test the Collimator Light Activation
steps.instructions: Check whether the collimator lamp or LED activates from the expected control. Press the collimator light button at the tube head or system control. Observe whether the light turns on, flickers, times out normally, or does not respond. Check whether the light works intermittently when the tube is moved. Expected outcome: The collimator light turns on consistently and remains visible long enough for positioning. If the light does not activate but the system otherwise functions, continue checking controls and cabling. If the light works normally, proceed to alignment and field size checks.

steps.title: Inspect the Collimator Face and Light Window
steps.instructions: Look for obvious external obstruction, contamination, or physical damage. Inspect the collimator face, light exit window, mirrors if visible externally, and accessory rails. Check for tape, covers, dust, fluid residue, cracked plastic, or damaged external parts. Remove only external obstructions that can be safely removed without disassembly. Expected outcome: The light path is clear and the collimator face is not visibly damaged. If the light window or collimator face is damaged, remove the system from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Verify Source-to-Image Distance and Positioning
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is being tested at the correct SID and detector position. Set the tube, table, or wall stand to a commonly used SID. Verify the detector is properly parked, centered, and recognized. Confirm the tube is positioned squarely to the table or wall stand. Expected outcome: The light field and center mark are checked under a normal, repeatable setup condition. Why it matters: Off-angle tube positioning, incorrect SID, or improper detector positioning can make the field appear miscentered even when the collimator is working correctly.

steps.title: Check Tube and Detector Centering
steps.instructions: Confirm the tube is aligned to the active image receptor. Use the system’s centering indicators, detents, locks, or position displays. Check the tube head alignment to the table bucky or wall stand bucky. Confirm the detector is seated correctly and not shifted in the tray or holder. Expected outcome: The tube, detector, and receptor center are physically aligned. If centering improves after correcting tube or detector position, the issue was setup-related. Document and stop.

steps.title: Check Collimator Blade Movement
steps.instructions: Verify that the field size controls actually move the collimator blades. Adjust the longitudinal and transverse field size controls. Watch the light field edges move smoothly. Listen for unusual grinding, clicking, binding, or motor noise. Confirm both axes respond. Expected outcome: The field edges move smoothly and predictably. If the blades do not move, move only one direction, bind, or make abnormal noise, remove the system from service and escalate for collimator evaluation.

steps.title: Compare Manual and Automatic Collimation Behavior
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is limited to auto-collimation or affects manual field sizing too. Test manual collimation first. Then test auto-collimation, if available and normally used by the site. Confirm the detector size and selected exam protocol match the expected field size. Expected outcome: Manual field size works reliably, and auto-collimation matches the selected receptor and protocol. If manual collimation works but auto-collimation does not, suspect detector recognition, receptor selection, protocol setup, or system communication issue before assuming collimator failure.

steps.title: Check Exam Protocol and Receptor Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm the selected exam and receptor match the expected field behavior. Review the selected anatomy, view, table or wall stand receptor, and detector size. Confirm staff did not select the wrong receptor or a protocol with a preset field size. Try a known-good basic exam selection used routinely in the room. Expected outcome: Field size and centering behavior match the selected receptor and exam setup. If the issue only occurs on one protocol, escalate for application or configuration review rather than hardware repair.

steps.title: Inspect External Cables and Movement-Related Symptoms
steps.instructions: Check for visible cable strain or intermittent behavior during tube movement. Inspect external cable carriers, tube head cable loops, connectors, and visible strain reliefs. Move the tube slowly through normal range while observing the collimator light and field control response. Do not open covers or manipulate internal wiring. Expected outcome: Collimator light and controls remain stable during movement. If the light flickers, controls drop out, or errors appear during motion, remove the system from service and escalate for cable, harness, or tube head evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages or Service Indicators
steps.instructions: Look for system messages that point toward collimator, tube stand, detector, or positioning faults. Review the operator console for active warnings. Note any messages related to collimator, field size, detector centering, tube position, SID, or system communication. Record the exact wording or code in the work order. Expected outcome: Any system-reported fault is captured accurately for repair follow-up. If an active collimator or positioning fault remains, remove the system from service and escalate.

steps.title: Perform a Non-Exposure Light Field Check
steps.instructions: If allowed by site policy, compare the projected light field to the expected receptor area without making an exposure. Use the table or wall stand reference markers. Set a known SID and center position. Adjust the field to a known size. Visually confirm whether the light field appears reasonably centered and square. Expected outcome: The light field appears centered, square, and responsive. If the light field is obviously skewed, offset, uneven, or not matching control settings, remove the system from service for collimator alignment or calibration evaluation.

steps.title: Determine Whether the System Can Remain in Service
steps.instructions: Make a service decision based on patient safety and imaging accuracy. Return the system to service only if: Collimator light works consistently; Field size controls respond normally; Tube and detector centering are correct; No active faults are present; Staff can reproduce normal operation. Expected outcome: The room is only returned to clinical use when field alignment and collimator behavior appear reliable. If there is any doubt about radiation field accuracy, remove the system from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the GE Discovery XR656 collimator light field appeared off-center from the wall stand detector during chest imaging.
CCR.Cause: Verified tube and detector centering, checked SID and receptor selection, and found the light field remained visibly offset with no external obstruction present.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the room from service, labeled the system Out of Service, and escalated for collimator alignment and service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Outlet or room power status verified
Any active console messages or fault codes
Collimator light turns on, flickers, or fails completely
Field appears offset, oversized, undersized, skewed, or uneven
Unusual collimator noise, binding, heat, or smell

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: COMMUNICATION FAILED
description: Troubleshooting COMMUNICATION FAILED on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm when workstation-to-C-Arm communication is lost and future X-rays are disabled.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if the error appears during setup, startup, positioning, or a clinical procedure. Stop attempting X-ray exposure if communication is unreliable or future X-rays are disabled. Use another C-Arm or approved imaging method if imaging is needed immediately.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that the displayed message reads COMMUNICATION FAILED. Observe when the error appears and confirm whether future X-rays are disabled after the message appears.

steps.title: Power Cycle the System Correctly
steps.instructions: Power the system off, wait at least 5 seconds, restart the C-Arm and workstation normally, and allow the full boot sequence to complete before attempting additional use.

steps.title: Check the Workstation-to-C-Arm Interconnect Cable
steps.instructions: Inspect the interconnect cable for loose connections, bent pins, damaged connector shells, cable strain, cuts, kinks, crushed sections, or poor seating at either end.

steps.title: Confirm Both System Sections Are Powered and Booting Normally
steps.instructions: Verify that both the workstation and C-Arm power up normally. Check for normal startup screens, expected indicator lights, abnormal boot delays, repeated restarts, blank displays, or unrelated startup errors.

steps.title: Check for Startup Sequence or Connection Timing Issues
steps.instructions: Restart the system using the normal facility startup process and avoid unplugging or reconnecting cables while the system is booting unless required by service procedure.

steps.title: Inspect for External Damage or Recent Movement
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the system was recently moved, bumped, transported, stored, or cleaned. Inspect the workstation, C-Arm base, cable path, and connector areas for physical damage.

steps.title: Identify Whether the Failure Is Intermittent or Constant
steps.instructions: Observe whether the error appears immediately every time, only after movement, when the cable is flexed, after the system has been powered on for a while, or randomly during use.

steps.title: Escalate for Service-Level Communication Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: If power cycling and external cable checks do not resolve the issue, suspect a device-level communication fault such as ARCNET communication failure, boot communication problems, corrupted persistent object files, or software-related faults.

CCR.Complaint: GE OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displayed COMMUNICATION FAILED. Staff reported communication was lost between the workstation and C-Arm, and future X-rays were disabled.
CCR.Cause: COMMUNICATION FAILED error confirmed. System was power cycled, workstation-to-C-Arm interconnect cable was inspected, both system sections were checked for normal startup, and error returned after restart.
CCR.Resolution: Powered system off for greater than 5 seconds and restarted. Checked external interconnect cable, connector seating, startup behavior, and visible damage. Error persisted. Removed device from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: DATA ERROR
description: Troubleshooting guide for DATA ERROR shutdowns on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm, including startup checks, external connection checks, patient safety precautions, and escalation for suspected corrupted data or software faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if DATA ERROR appears or the system shuts down. Use another imaging system if fluoroscopy is required. Do not continue troubleshooting during an active procedure.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed message is DATA ERROR. Document when it appears, whether the system shuts down, whether it happens every boot, and whether other messages appear first.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Reboot
steps.instructions: Power the system off, wait at least 5 seconds, then restart and allow the boot process to complete without unnecessary control input.

steps.title: Check AC Power and Startup Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the workstation is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Check the power cord condition and avoid unstable outlets, questionable extension cords, or shared power strips.

steps.title: Check the Workstation-to-C-Arm Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect and reseat the workstation/C-Arm interconnect cable. Check for loose connections, bent pins, damaged connector shells, cable strain, crushed sections, or contamination.

steps.title: Check for Recent Service, Software, or Component Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether software, flash memory, workstation, C-Arm, generator board, processor board, batteries, or calibration-related items were recently serviced or replaced.

steps.title: Check for Repeated or Consistent Shutdown
steps.instructions: After external checks, restart the system one additional time and observe whether DATA ERROR returns at the same point during startup.

steps.title: Do Not Attempt Clinical Workarounds
steps.instructions: Do not use partial startup, intermittent clearing, or repeated power cycling as a clinical workaround. Remove the unit from service if the error persists.

CCR.Complaint: GE OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displayed DATA ERROR during startup and shut down. Staff unable to use C-Arm for imaging.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed DATA ERROR on boot. AC power and workstation/C-Arm interconnect cable checked and reseated. Error persisted, indicating likely corrupted nonvolatile data, software/data access issue, ABS table problem, or flash/workstation mismatch.
CCR.Resolution: Removed C-Arm from service and labeled Out of Service. Sent for bench evaluation/software-data troubleshooting. Software reload or data repair may be required before return to service.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: AD CHANNEL %s FAIL Startup Failure
description: Troubleshooting AD CHANNEL %s FAIL startup shutdowns on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm caused by failed analog-to-digital startup checks, analog input faults, or related circuitry issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the C-Arm while it is positioned for active patient imaging. Remove the device from patient use if the system shuts down, will not complete startup, or displays the AD CHANNEL failure. Use another C-Arm or approved imaging method if imaging is needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the system on and confirm whether the display shows AD CHANNEL %s FAIL – POWER OFF. WAIT 5 SECONDS. Record the actual channel number or identifier shown in place of %s and note whether the system shuts down immediately.

steps.title: Power Off and Wait 5 Seconds
steps.instructions: Turn the C-Arm system power off, wait at least 5 seconds, then power the system back on. Observe whether the error clears or returns.

steps.title: Check AC Power and Power Stability
steps.instructions: Confirm the workstation is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Check for loose plugs, damaged power cords, tripped breakers, or poor outlet contact.

steps.title: Check the C-Arm and Workstation Interconnect
steps.instructions: Inspect the workstation-to-C-Arm interconnect cable for loose connectors, bent pins, damaged cable jackets, strain damage, contamination, or incomplete seating. Reseat the cable if appropriate and restart the system.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious External Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the C-Arm, control panels, cable ports, generator area covers, and workstation exterior for fluid intrusion, impact damage, damaged connectors, pinched cables, burning smell, unusual heat, or recent cleaning fluid exposure.

steps.title: Identify Whether the Failure Is Repeatable
steps.instructions: After confirming safe conditions, power cycle the system only as needed to document whether the same channel fails each time, the error is intermittent, the channel changes, or the system shuts down before normal operation.

steps.title: Isolate Simple External Contributors
steps.instructions: Confirm no accessories, controls, cables, or external components are damaged or incorrectly connected. Review whether the issue began after cleaning, movement, storage, transport, or cable reconnection.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the error returns after power cycling and external checks, treat the issue as a device-level fault. Do not continue repeated restarts for clinical use. Remove the C-Arm from service for bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: GE OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displays AD CHANNEL %s FAIL – POWER OFF. WAIT 5 SECONDS during startup. System shuts down and cannot be used for imaging.
CCR.Cause: Startup self-test detected an analog-to-digital channel failure. Power cycle and external connection checks did not clear the error. Likely internal analog input, A/D channel, wiring, or related control circuitry fault.
CCR.Resolution: Powered system off for more than 5 seconds and restarted. Verified error returned during startup. Checked AC power, interconnect cable, external connectors, and visible device condition. Removed C-Arm from service and sent for bench evaluation/repair.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: FAST STOP ACTIVATED – POWER OFF. WAIT 5 SECONDS
description: Troubleshooting FAST STOP ACTIVATED – POWER OFF. WAIT 5 SECONDS on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm when an open interlock condition disables X-ray generation, column lift motors, and related system functions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if the error appears during setup, positioning, or a procedure. Confirm X-ray generation is disabled, notify clinical staff, and use another imaging system if imaging is needed immediately.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows FAST STOP ACTIVATED – POWER OFF. WAIT 5 SECONDS. Document when the error occurred and whether X-ray generation, system controls, or column lift motors are disabled.

steps.title: Power Down, Wait, and Reboot
steps.instructions: Power the system off, wait at least 5 seconds, then power the system back on. Observe whether the system boots normally and whether the FAST STOP message returns.

steps.title: Inspect the FAST STOP Switches
steps.instructions: Check all FAST STOP switches for an activated, stuck, cracked, loose, contaminated, or damaged condition. Reset any activated switch and power cycle the system again.

steps.title: Check for Accidental Activation or User Workflow Issue
steps.instructions: Ask staff if a FAST STOP switch was pressed intentionally or accidentally. Check whether the C-Arm was bumped, moved, cleaned, or parked when the error appeared.

steps.title: Check External Cable and Connection Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the workstation and C-Arm interconnect cable is fully seated and not strained, pinched, damaged, contaminated, or showing bent pins. Reseat accessible connectors only if safe and permitted by facility practice.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect FAST STOP switch locations, column area, control panels, cable routing, and base for cracked housings, loose switch assemblies, fluid residue, damaged strain reliefs, or evidence of impact.

steps.title: Evaluate for an Intermittent Interlock Condition
steps.instructions: If the system intermittently boots normally, check whether movement near a specific switch, cable, or column area causes the error to return. Do not continue clinical use if the error is intermittent.

CCR.Complaint: GE OEC 9900 displayed FAST STOP ACTIVATED – POWER OFF. WAIT 5 SECONDS. X-ray generation and column lift functions were disabled during attempted use.
