CCR.Cause: FAST STOP/interlock condition observed. FAST STOP switches and external interconnect cable were inspected. No physical damage found. Error cleared after proper power cycle.
CCR.Resolution: Powered system off, waited 5 seconds, rebooted, verified FAST STOP switches were released, confirmed normal startup and basic system operation, and returned C-Arm to service.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: CHARGER FAILED
description: Troubleshooting guide for the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm CHARGER FAILED error, including AC power checks, interconnect cable inspection, charger indicator review, battery symptom checks, escalation, and CCR work order documentation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if battery status is uncertain, the charger fault repeats, or the unit may lose power during use. Use another imaging system if fluoroscopy is required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the system on and confirm whether CHARGER FAILED appears during bootup, whether the message alternates with technique information, and whether related battery or power warnings are present.

steps.title: Check AC Power First
steps.instructions: Confirm the workstation power cord is fully seated and plugged into a known-good hospital-grade AC outlet. Test or change the outlet if needed.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Inspect the AC cord, plug, strain relief, and cord path for cuts, crushed insulation, loose plug fit, bent prongs, heat marks, or arcing damage.

steps.title: Check the Workstation/C-Arm Interconnect Cable
steps.instructions: Verify the workstation/C-Arm interconnect cable is fully connected at both ends. Inspect connector pins, locking hardware, cable jacket, and strain relief. Reseat the cable if allowed by facility procedure.

steps.title: Observe the Battery Charger Light
steps.instructions: Observe the battery charger light during AC connection and startup. Document whether it is on normally, off, flashing unexpectedly, intermittent, or different from known normal behavior.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Confirm Whether the Error Returns
steps.instructions: After confirming AC power and cable connections, power cycle the system using normal shutdown and startup procedure. If CHARGER FAILED returns, keep the device out of service.

steps.title: Check for Related Power or Battery Symptoms
steps.instructions: Check for poor battery runtime, failure to charge, battery warnings, shutdown when unplugged, unusual charger smell, heat, sound, or intermittent boot behavior.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Fuses and External Power Path per Facility Procedure
steps.instructions: If permitted, inspect accessible charger-related fuses according to approved procedure. Do not bypass fuses or substitute incorrect ratings.

steps.title: Determine Whether External Causes Have Been Ruled Out
steps.instructions: Confirm that AC power, power cord condition, interconnect cable seating, charger light behavior, and related battery symptoms have been checked. If the error remains, suspect an internal charger or power-path issue.

CCR.Complaint: User reported GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displayed CHARGER FAILED during bootup. Battery charging status was uncertain and charger fault returned after restart.
CCR.Cause: Verified AC outlet and power cord. Workstation/C-Arm interconnect cable was checked and reseated. Charger indicator behavior was abnormal and CHARGER FAILED returned after restart, indicating likely internal charger or power-path failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed C-Arm from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation of charger PCB, charger wiring, related fuses, battery charging circuit, and power/signal interface path.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: COLLIMATOR STUCK
description: Troubleshooting COLLIMATOR STUCK on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm when the collimator iris cannot open or close and beam limitation may be unavailable.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if the collimator cannot be adjusted or the radiation field cannot be controlled. Use another C-Arm if imaging is needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Error: COLLIMATOR STUCK. Observe whether the error occurs at startup, during iris adjustment, intermittently, or after transport.

steps.title: Check for Obvious External or Physical Causes
steps.instructions: Inspect the collimator opening area for debris, tape, drapes, residue, physical damage, or signs of impact. Do not force the iris blades.

steps.title: Check C-Arm Control Inputs
steps.instructions: Operate the collimator controls and verify other nearby control functions respond normally. Look for stuck, damaged, or non-responsive buttons.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: Restart the system using normal shutdown and startup procedures. Check whether the error returns during startup or only when iris movement is commanded.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Cables and Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect visible cable routing around the C-Arm head and collimator area for loose connectors, pinched wiring, damaged insulation, or strain from movement.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Binding Symptoms
steps.instructions: Listen for clicking, humming, grinding, or stalled motor sounds during iris adjustment. Do not manually force the collimator mechanism.

steps.title: Consider Iris Motor or Drive Failure
steps.instructions: If the motor is silent or runs without iris movement, suspect motor drive, wiring, coupling, mechanical binding, or seized motor issues.

steps.title: Consider Position-Feedback Problems
steps.instructions: If the iris moves but the error remains, suspect position-feedback failure or inconsistent iris position sensing.

CCR.Complaint: User reported GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displayed COLLIMATOR STUCK and the collimator iris would not open or close during adjustment.
CCR.Cause: Verified COLLIMATOR STUCK error during iris adjustment. No external obstruction or visible damage found. Error persisted after restart, indicating likely collimator iris motor, mechanical binding, wiring, control circuit, or position-feedback issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed C-Arm from service and labeled Out of Service. Escalated for imaging service repair or bench evaluation of the collimator iris motor, wiring, control circuitry, and position-feedback components.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: COLLIMATOR CAL REQUIRED - PRESS ANY KEY
description: Troubleshooting guide for the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm COLLIMATOR CAL REQUIRED error, where collimator position values do not match stored references and X-ray or generator functions may be disabled.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if the error appears before or during a procedure. Stop use if generator functions or X-rays are disabled, notify clinical staff, and use another C-Arm if imaging is needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the OEC 9900 and confirm the message displays exactly as COLLIMATOR CAL REQUIRED - PRESS ANY KEY. Note when the error appears and whether X-ray or generator functions are disabled.

steps.title: Clear the Message from the C-Arm Control Panel
steps.instructions: Press any C-Arm control panel key to acknowledge and clear the message. Observe whether normal operation returns and verify basic function if the message clears.

steps.title: Restart the System and Recheck
steps.instructions: If the message clears but returns, perform a normal shutdown, wait briefly, restart the system, and observe whether the error returns during startup.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Collimator Movement Problems
steps.instructions: Externally inspect the collimator area for damage, debris, binding, impact, or contamination. Do not force the blades or perform internal disassembly during clinical troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Recent Handling or Service History
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the unit was recently moved, bumped, serviced, stored, or powered off unexpectedly. Check for visible damage around the C-Arm head, collimator area, cable strain points, and control panel.

steps.title: Perform Collimator Calibration Only If Qualified
steps.instructions: If the error returns after acknowledgment and restart, collimator calibration may be required. Perform calibration only if qualified, authorized, and using the correct GE service procedure and tools.

steps.title: Verify Operation Before Return to Service
steps.instructions: Confirm the system boots without the error, collimator iris and leaf movement operate normally, imaging functions are available, and no related collimator or X-ray disabled messages return.

CCR.Complaint: GE OEC 9900 displayed COLLIMATOR CAL REQUIRED - PRESS ANY KEY. Staff reported X-rays/generator functions were disabled and the unit could not be used for imaging.
CCR.Cause: Collimator iris and leaf position values did not match stored reference values. Error cleared initially but returned after restart, indicating a possible collimator calibration or position feedback issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed C-Arm from clinical use. Pressed C-Arm control panel key to acknowledge message, restarted system, and inspected for obvious external collimator obstruction or damage. Device remains Out of Service pending calibration or repair.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: COLL IRIS POTENTIOMETER ERROR - PRESS ANY KEY
description: Troubleshooting guide for COLL IRIS POTENTIOMETER ERROR - PRESS ANY KEY on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm, where missing or incorrect collimator iris position feedback may disable X-rays and prevent safe imaging operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if X-rays are disabled or collimator function is questionable. Use another imaging system if imaging is needed immediately.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows COLL IRIS POTENTIOMETER ERROR - PRESS ANY KEY. Note whether the error appears during startup, during collimator adjustment, or intermittently during use.

steps.title: Check for Obvious External or Mechanical Problems
steps.instructions: Inspect the collimator area for visible damage, impact, loose covers, contamination, obstruction, or mechanical binding. Do not force the iris mechanism.

steps.title: Check Collimator Iris Movement
steps.instructions: Attempt normal iris adjustment if the system allows it. Observe whether the iris moves smoothly, partially, or not at all, and listen for abnormal motor noise.

steps.title: Inspect Potentiometer Wiring and Connector Seating
steps.instructions: Check accessible potentiometer wiring and connectors for loose seating, bent pins, damaged insulation, pinched wiring, corrosion, contamination, or signs of strain.

steps.title: Check Whether the COLL_IRIS_POSN Signal Changes
steps.instructions: Check the COLL_IRIS_POSN signal on the Fluoro Functions PCB according to site procedure and service-level access. Confirm whether the signal changes smoothly as the iris moves.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Feedback Path Cause
steps.instructions: If the iris moves but the signal does not change, focus on the potentiometer and wiring. If the signal reaches the board but the error remains, suspect Fluoro Functions PCB input processing or related circuitry.

steps.title: Clear the Error and Recheck Operation
steps.instructions: After correcting any external, wiring, or mechanical issue, power cycle if required. Verify the error does not return and confirm normal iris and generator operation only after required safety checks.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displayed COLL IRIS POTENTIOMETER ERROR - PRESS ANY KEY. User reported X-rays or generator functions were disabled and collimator iris operation could not be confirmed.
CCR.Cause: Collimator iris position feedback fault observed. COLL_IRIS_POSN signal did not change normally with iris movement, indicating possible iris potentiometer, wiring, connector, mechanical iris, or Fluoro Functions PCB feedback issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed C-Arm from service. Inspected collimator iris mechanics, potentiometer wiring, connector seating, and feedback signal path. Error persisted after external checks. Unit labeled Out of Service and escalated for repair or bench evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: FILAMENT REGULATOR FAILURE - PRESS ANY KEY
description: Troubleshooting guide for FILAMENT REGULATOR FAILURE - PRESS ANY KEY on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm, where X-ray tube filament current is out of tolerance and image resolution may be degraded.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from active patient use if image quality is unreliable or the error repeatedly returns. Use another C-Arm if reliable imaging is required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the message reads FILAMENT REGULATOR FAILURE - PRESS ANY KEY. Note whether it occurs during startup, fluoro, repeated exposures, or intermittently during use.

steps.title: Clear the Message Using the C-Arm Keypad
steps.instructions: Press any C-Arm control panel key to continue. Observe whether the error clears and only continue operation if clinically safe and allowed by site procedure.

steps.title: Check for Image Quality Degradation
steps.instructions: Perform a basic image quality check using an approved test object or facility procedure. Look for low brightness, noise, poor resolution, inconsistent output, or unstable fluoro image.

steps.title: Check Power and Basic External Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify proper AC power, seated power cord, workstation-to-C-Arm interconnect cable seating, connector condition, and absence of questionable outlets or extension cords.

steps.title: Inspect External Cabling and Physical Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible cables, connectors, panels, and the workstation/C-Arm exterior for impact, fluid intrusion, overheated smell, contamination, looseness, or damage.

steps.title: Confirm the Fault Pattern
steps.instructions: Determine whether the error occurs during boot, low-dose fluoro, high-level fluoro, repeated exposures, long cases, or after idle time. Record whether it clears or returns repeatedly.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: If the device is not in clinical use, perform a controlled restart and recheck startup and basic operation according to site policy. Remove the device from service if the error returns.

steps.title: Escalate for Qualified Service
steps.instructions: If the message persists or image quality is degraded, stop routine troubleshooting and escalate for bench evaluation of filament regulator circuitry, filament drive circuitry, generator boards, and related wiring.

CCR.Complaint: User reported GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displayed FILAMENT REGULATOR FAILURE - PRESS ANY KEY. Staff stated the system allowed continued operation, but image quality may have been degraded.
CCR.Cause: Filament current was reported out of tolerance by the system. External power, interconnect cable seating, visible damage, and basic image quality behavior were checked. Persistent fault indicates likely filament regulator, filament drive, generator board, or related wiring issue.
CCR.Resolution: Pressed C-Arm key to clear message and performed basic operational/image quality check. Error returned during testing. Removed C-Arm from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated for generator/filament circuit repair evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: FILAMENT SELECT ERROR - PRESS ANY KEY
description: Troubleshooting FILAMENT SELECT ERROR - PRESS ANY KEY on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm when a filament selection mismatch disables X-rays.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if X-rays are disabled or the error appears during a procedure. Stop attempting exposures, notify clinical staff, and bring in another C-Arm if imaging is needed for patient care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the C-Arm and confirm the display reads FILAMENT SELECT ERROR - PRESS ANY KEY. Note whether the error appears at startup, during X-ray preparation, during mode selection, intermittently during use, or after movement or cable strain.

steps.title: Understand What the Error Means
steps.instructions: Treat the error as a mismatch between the software-selected filament size and the filament selection detected by PIO hardware. Do not assume the X-ray tube itself has failed without checking external and control-related causes first.

steps.title: Attempt the Operator-Level Clear
steps.instructions: Press any C-Arm key as instructed by the message. If the message clears and does not return, verify normal operation according to facility procedure before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: If the error returns, power down the system, wait briefly for shutdown to complete, and power it back on. Observe whether the error returns during startup or only when X-ray functions are selected.

steps.title: Check Basic External Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm proper power, inspect the interconnect cable, check visible cables for damage or strain, look for fluid intrusion or impact damage, and confirm control panels are not stuck, contaminated, or physically damaged.

steps.title: Check Whether the Error Is Mode-Related
steps.instructions: If the error clears, observe whether it returns during fluoro readiness, high-level fluoro, snapshot or radiographic exposure, mode changes, or technique changes. Do not repeatedly attempt exposures if the error returns.

steps.title: Inspect for Accessory or Connection-Related Triggers
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible external cable connections and observe whether movement of the C-Arm, workstation, or cable bundle causes the error to appear or disappear. Remove the device from service if cable movement affects the error.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Reproducible
steps.instructions: After clearing the error, note whether it returns immediately after pressing a key, during boot, during X-ray enable, during mode selection, or during C-Arm movement.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Failure Area
steps.instructions: If external causes are ruled out, suspect filament select circuitry, PIO hardware, filament driver circuitry, or related wiring and connectors. Do not perform internal disassembly at the point of use.

CCR.Complaint: GE OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displayed FILAMENT SELECT ERROR - PRESS ANY KEY and X-rays were disabled while the error was active.
CCR.Cause: Error cleared after pressing a C-Arm key. External connections were inspected and no cable damage, fluid intrusion, or repeated error was observed during operational check.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared error, inspected external connections, verified system readiness, and returned device to service after testing per facility procedure.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: HOUSING OVERHEATED
description: Troubleshooting HOUSING OVERHEATED on a GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm when tube housing temperature causes thermal shutdown and prevents safe X-ray operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use after a housing overheat shutdown. Use another C-Arm or approved alternate imaging method if imaging is still required. Do not attempt repeated restarts to continue a procedure.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that the display showed HOUSING OVERHEATED and determine whether the system shut down immediately. Ask when the error occurred and what imaging mode or exposure pattern was being used.

steps.title: Allow the Tube Housing to Cool
steps.instructions: Leave the system out of patient use and allow the X-ray tube housing to cool naturally. Do not restart immediately or attempt additional exposures. Adequate cooling may require an hour or more.

steps.title: Check Recent Imaging Use and Heat Loading
steps.instructions: Ask what type of imaging was being performed before shutdown. Check for long fluoroscopy time, repeated high-dose exposures, High Level Fluoro, Digital Cine, large patient imaging, or frequent exposures with little cooling time.

steps.title: Inspect External Airflow and Ventilation
steps.instructions: Check for blocked vents, restricted airflow, drapes, covers, wall placement, or crowded equipment around cooling areas. Remove obstructions and make sure the device is not operating near excessive environmental heat.

steps.title: Check for Fan Operation and Cooling Behavior
steps.instructions: After the unit has cooled enough to safely power on, listen for fan operation and check for abnormal fan noise, weak airflow, hot smell, excessive heat, or the error returning quickly after minimal use.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible exterior areas, tube housing area, vents, and cooling paths for dust buildup, blocked vents, fluid contamination, damaged covers, loose panels, impact damage, burning smell, discoloration, or unusual heat.

steps.title: Restart Only After Adequate Cooling
steps.instructions: After a proper cooling period, power the system back on and observe whether the error returns immediately or only after additional X-ray use. Do not perform unnecessary exposures just to test the issue in a patient care area.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Use-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the error followed extended high-load imaging and clears after cooling, it was likely heat-loading related. If it returns quickly after light use, fans are abnormal, or shutdown occurs immediately after startup, suspect a device fault.

CCR.Complaint: User reported GE OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displayed HOUSING OVERHEATED and shut down during use. X-ray operation was unavailable after thermal shutdown.
CCR.Cause: X-ray tube housing reached overheat cutoff condition. Heavy heat-loading use was reported prior to shutdown. Device required extended cooling before further operation.
CCR.Resolution: Removed C-Arm from patient use and allowed tube housing to cool. Checked for blocked airflow, fan operation, abnormal heat, odor, and visible damage. Unit restarted normally after cooling and error did not immediately return. Returned to service after confirming stable operation.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: ANODE IS HOT - XX%
description: Troubleshooting ANODE IS HOT - XX% on a GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm when the X-ray tube anode reaches a heat threshold and High Level Fluoro or FILM exposures may be blocked until cooling occurs.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not continue high-load imaging if the system is warning that the anode is hot. Remove the C-Arm from active use if imaging cannot safely continue, notify clinical staff, and use another C-Arm if urgent imaging is required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact message ANODE IS HOT - XX%, check when it appeared, determine whether High Level Fluoro or FILM exposures are blocked, and press ALARM RESET to silence the audible alarm if appropriate.

steps.title: Allow the X-Ray Tube to Cool
steps.instructions: Stop high-load exposures, allow the X-ray tube to cool, avoid unnecessary exposure attempts, and monitor whether the displayed heat percentage decreases over time.

steps.title: Review Recent Imaging Use
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what imaging was performed before the warning appeared. Check for long fluoroscopy time, repeated high-dose imaging, frequent FILM exposures, High Level Fluoro use, large patient body habitus, or limited pauses between exposures.

steps.title: Check for Cooling Obstructions or Environmental Problems
steps.instructions: Inspect for blocked vents, drapes, covers, equipment, positioning issues, contamination, dust buildup, external damage, or a hot room environment that may limit cooling.

steps.title: Check Whether the Warning Clears Normally
steps.instructions: After the cooling period, verify that the displayed percentage decreases, the warning clears, and blocked exposure modes are restored. Do not perform unnecessary high-load exposures just to test the system.

steps.title: Identify Abnormal Warning Behavior
steps.instructions: Remove the device from service if the warning appears shortly after startup with minimal X-ray use, the percentage does not decrease after cooling, the warning repeatedly appears faster than expected, or High Level Fluoro or FILM remain blocked after cooling.

CCR.Complaint: User reported ANODE IS HOT - XX% warning on GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm. Audible alarm sounded and High Level Fluoro/FILM exposures were unavailable.
CCR.Cause: X-ray tube anode heat level reached warning threshold after high-load imaging use. No external cooling obstruction found. Warning cleared after tube cooling.
CCR.Resolution: Allowed X-ray tube to cool. Pressed ALARM RESET to silence alarm. Verified anode heat percentage decreased and exposure functions returned to normal. Unit returned to service.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: 24-HOUR RECHARGE REQUIRED - X RAYS DISABLED
description: Troubleshooting Error: 24-HOUR RECHARGE REQUIRED - X RAYS DISABLED on a GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm when generator functions are disabled because the C-Arm battery system requires an extended recharge period.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not attempt to use the C-Arm for a procedure while this error is active. Remove the unit from clinical use if X-ray capability is needed and provide another C-Arm or approved imaging method.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display reads 24-HOUR RECHARGE REQUIRED - X RAYS DISABLED. Verify that X-ray or generator functions are disabled and ask whether the unit had been stored, unused, or unplugged for an extended period.

steps.title: Understand What the Error Means
steps.instructions: Treat this as a required battery recharge condition, not a user setting problem. Do not repeatedly reboot the system expecting the error to clear immediately.

steps.title: Connect the Workstation to AC Power
steps.instructions: Plug the workstation power cord into a known-good AC outlet. Confirm the cord is fully seated and check for facility power issues, damaged cords, loose plugs, or tripped outlets.

steps.title: Verify the Interconnect Cable Is Connected
steps.instructions: Inspect the interconnect cable between the workstation and C-Arm. Verify it is fully seated and check for bent pins, damaged connectors, loose strain reliefs, crushed cable sections, or contamination.

steps.title: Leave the System Plugged In for the Full Recharge Period
steps.instructions: Leave the workstation power cord plugged into AC power and keep the interconnect cable connected. The workstation does not need to be turned on during this recharge period. Allow the full 24 hours.

steps.title: Recheck the System After 24 Hours
steps.instructions: Power the system on after the full 24-hour recharge period. Confirm the error is no longer displayed, verify normal startup behavior, and perform required operational checks before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Check for Repeat or Persistent Recharge Errors
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit remained plugged into powered AC for the full period and that the interconnect cable stayed connected. If the error remains, remove the device from service for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: GE OEC 9900 C-Arm displayed Error: 24-HOUR RECHARGE REQUIRED - X RAYS DISABLED. Staff reported the unit could not perform X-ray functions after being unused or stored for an extended period.
CCR.Cause: C-Arm battery system required extended recharge. Workstation AC power and interconnect cable were checked. Error condition was consistent with depleted batteries after long-term storage.
CCR.Resolution: Connected workstation to known-good AC power, verified interconnect cable was fully seated, and allowed required 24-hour recharge period. After recharge, system was powered on and error cleared. Operational checks completed and unit returned to service.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: 4-HOUR WARM-UP REQUIRED - PRESS ANY KEY
description: Troubleshooting Error: 4-HOUR WARM-UP REQUIRED - PRESS ANY KEY on a GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm when extended inactivity requires a four-hour warm-up and generator functions are disabled.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot this condition during an active procedure. Remove the C-Arm from clinical use if X-ray imaging is needed immediately and provide another available C-Arm or approved imaging method.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the OEC 9900 and confirm the displayed message reads Error: 4-HOUR WARM-UP REQUIRED - PRESS ANY KEY. Confirm whether X-ray or generator functions are disabled.

steps.title: Confirm Extended Inactivity
steps.instructions: Check department history, equipment history, or work order records to determine whether the C-Arm has been inactive for more than 60 days.

steps.title: Do Not Attempt to Bypass the Warm-Up
steps.instructions: Do not repeatedly power cycle the unit or attempt X-ray operation while generator functions are disabled. Advise staff that the four-hour delay must complete before normal use.

steps.title: Allow the Four-Hour Warm-Up Period to Complete
steps.instructions: Keep the unit connected to a proper power source and allow the full four-hour warm-up period to complete without interruption unless safety requires shutdown.

steps.title: Press Any C-Arm Key After Warm-Up
steps.instructions: After the warm-up finishes, press any key on the C-Arm control panel and observe whether the error clears and the system proceeds to normal operation.

steps.title: Verify Basic Operation After the Error Clears
steps.instructions: Perform normal operational checks according to facility procedure. Verify normal startup, control panel response, generator availability, approved X-ray or fluoro testing, and no additional errors.

steps.title: Check for Repeat or Abnormal Behavior
steps.instructions: If the error returns after the completed warm-up, check for abnormal startup behavior, additional generator or image-chain errors, and remove the device from service for evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: User reported GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displayed Error: 4-HOUR WARM-UP REQUIRED - PRESS ANY KEY during startup. Generator functions were disabled and the unit could not be used for X-ray imaging.
CCR.Cause: System indicated extended inactivity condition requiring four-hour image intensifier warm-up before generator operation could resume. No external power, cable, accessory, or user setup issue was identified.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from clinical use during warm-up. Allowed required four-hour warm-up period to complete. Pressed C-Arm control panel key after warm-up and verified error cleared. Performed functional check and confirmed normal startup and generator availability before returning unit to service.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: CONTROL PANEL KEY STUCK - RELEASE, THEN PRESS ANY KEY TO CONTINUE
description: Troubleshooting a stuck control panel key error on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm that can disable generator functions and prevent X-ray operation during bootup.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if generator functions or X-rays are disabled. Confirm the device is not being used for an active procedure and provide another C-Arm or approved imaging method if imaging is needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the OEC 9900 on and confirm the displayed message is CONTROL PANEL KEY STUCK - RELEASE, THEN PRESS ANY KEY TO CONTINUE. Note when the error appears and whether generator functions, X-rays, or keypad response are affected.

steps.title: Check for Anything Pressing the Control Panel
steps.instructions: Inspect the control panel and remove drapes, covers, tape, labels, gel residue, accessories, cleaning wipes, or objects that may be pressing a key.

steps.title: Free the Suspected Stuck Key
steps.instructions: Gently press and release each control panel key. Look for a key that feels sticky, does not return fully, sits low, appears damaged, or responds differently than the others.

steps.title: Press Any C-Arm Control Panel Key to Continue
steps.instructions: After the stuck-key condition is corrected, press any C-Arm control panel key and observe whether the system continues through startup.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: Shut the system down normally, restart the C-Arm, and confirm the stuck key error does not return during startup.

steps.title: Inspect for Cleaning Residue, Fluid, or Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the control panel for cracked membrane material, damaged buttons, fluid residue, cleaning chemical buildup, loose overlay, impact damage, or keys that remain partially depressed.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the error clears after removing an obstruction or freeing a key, document the external cause. If it returns with no visible obstruction or contamination, suspect keypad, control panel, membrane switch, or related input hardware failure.

CCR.Complaint: GE OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displays CONTROL PANEL KEY STUCK - RELEASE, THEN PRESS ANY KEY TO CONTINUE during bootup. Generator functions and X-rays are disabled.
CCR.Cause: Control panel key input was detected as stuck during startup. No drape, tape, cover, residue, or object was found pressing the keypad. Error returned after power cycle, indicating likely keypad or control panel hardware fault.
CCR.Resolution: Inspected control panel, checked all keys for sticking, removed external causes, power cycled the system, and verified the error persisted. Removed C-Arm from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: HV GENERATOR ERROR
description: Troubleshooting guide for HV GENERATOR ERROR on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm, including patient safety, power checks, startup behavior, cooling, escalation, and CCR documentation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if HV GENERATOR ERROR appears, if the system shuts down, or if imaging cannot be safely performed. Use another imaging system for active clinical needs.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed message is HV GENERATOR ERROR. Note whether it appears during bootup, initialization, fluoro attempt, exposure, after warmup, or after movement.

steps.title: Check Facility Power First
steps.instructions: Verify the system is connected to a known-good approved outlet. Check for loose plugs, unstable power, shared heavy-load outlets, or facility power issues.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Main Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord, plug, strain relief, and system power connection for damage, heat marks, looseness, or intermittent power behavior.

steps.title: Confirm Normal Startup and Power Cycling
steps.instructions: Power the system down fully, allow it to shut down completely, then restart using the normal facility-approved startup process. Avoid rapid repeated power cycling.

steps.title: Check for Recent Movement, Impact, Cleaning, or Service
steps.instructions: Ask whether the C-Arm was moved, struck, cleaned heavily, serviced, reassembled, plugged into another room, or used after a power outage. Inspect for visible external damage or fluid residue.

steps.title: Check Cooling and Ventilation
steps.instructions: Check for blocked vents, heavy dust, poor clearance, covers, drapes, or storage conditions that restrict airflow. Allow the system to cool if the error appeared after extended use.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Functional Check Off the Patient
steps.instructions: With the device removed from patient use, observe whether the error appears before exposure, during fluoro attempt, or only after generator demand.

steps.title: Rule Out External Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm stable power, normal startup, adequate cooling, no obvious physical damage, and no external setup issue. If the error persists, treat it as a device-level generator fault.

steps.title: Remove from Service and Escalate
steps.instructions: Remove the OEC 9900 from service, label it Out of Service, and send it for repair, vendor service, or bench evaluation. Do not return it to clinical use until repair and verification are complete.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displayed HV GENERATOR ERROR and shut down during startup or fluoro attempt. Unit unavailable for imaging.
CCR.Cause: Verified HV GENERATOR ERROR. Checked approved power outlet, power cord condition, startup behavior, cooling, ventilation, and obvious physical damage. Error persisted, indicating likely device-level high-voltage generator control or regulation fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed C-Arm from service and labeled Out of Service. Sent for service-level generator troubleshooting or vendor repair. Unit not returned to clinical use pending repair and operational verification.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: HV REGISTER FAIL
description: Troubleshooting guide for HV REGISTER FAIL on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm when the system detects a high-voltage generator startup or register fault and may shut down before imaging is available.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-Arm from patient use if the error appears during startup, boot, generator initialization, or attempted imaging. Use another imaging system if fluoroscopy or X-ray imaging is required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the OEC 9900 on and confirm that the displayed error is exactly HV REGISTER FAIL. Note when the error appears, whether the system shuts down, and whether other generator, power, or communication errors are present.

steps.title: Check Facility Power First
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Inspect the power cord, plug, strain relief, and outlet connection. Avoid extension cords, damaged outlets, loose receptacles, or questionable power strips.

steps.title: Check Main Power and Startup Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe whether the monitor cart, C-Arm, fans, indicators, and display power normally. Listen for abnormal relay cycling, fan failure, buzzing, arcing, or sudden power loss. Check for heat, odor, damage, or fluid intrusion.

steps.title: Check External Cable Connections Between C-Arm and Workstation
steps.instructions: Power the system down, inspect and reseat accessible external cables between the C-Arm and workstation cart, and check for bent pins, loose connectors, damaged cable jackets, poor locking collars, or cable strain.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Physical or Environmental Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect for impact damage, fluid exposure, missing covers, blocked vents, overheating, or recent movement from cold, hot, or humid conditions. Allow the system to stabilize if environment may be contributing.

steps.title: Attempt a Controlled Restart
steps.instructions: After patient safety and external checks are complete, power the system down fully, wait for a normal reset period, restart the system, and observe whether HV REGISTER FAIL returns. Do not repeatedly power-cycle if the error is consistent.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Fault Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Rule out unstable wall power, loose external system cables, poor outlet connection, recent movement, environmental moisture, or overheating before escalating to internal high-voltage generator diagnostics.

steps.title: Stop If the Error Clears
steps.instructions: If the error clears after correcting an external issue, verify normal startup, confirm ready status, perform required operational checks according to facility policy, and document what was corrected.

CCR.Complaint: GE OEC 9900 C-Arm displays HV REGISTER FAIL during startup. System detects high-voltage generator register failure and shuts down before clinical imaging is available.
CCR.Cause: Facility power and external connections were checked. Error remained after controlled restart. Fault indicates likely internal high-voltage generator register/startup path, generator control circuitry, or associated generator board failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed C-Arm from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated for service-level high-voltage generator evaluation/repair. Unit not returned to clinical use.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: INTERLOCK FAILURE
description: Troubleshooting guide for a GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm INTERLOCK FAILURE error during power-up, where the interlock circuits are in an incorrect state and the system shuts down.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot a C-Arm interlock failure during an active procedure. Remove the OEC 9900 from patient use if it shuts down, will not complete startup, or displays INTERLOCK FAILURE. Provide another imaging system if imaging is needed for patient care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the system on and confirm whether the display shows INTERLOCK FAILURE. Note whether the system shuts down immediately, powers partially, fails only during startup, or has an intermittent failure.

steps.title: Check Basic Power and Startup Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the C-Arm and workstation are connected to the correct power source. Check the power cord, outlet, plugs, breakers, and facility power before focusing on interlock hardware.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Mechanical or Cover Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible panels, covers, doors, latches, brackets, and access areas for looseness, misalignment, missing hardware, or damage. Reseat only user-accessible covers or panels that appear loose.

steps.title: Check C-Arm Positioning, Locks, and Movement-Related Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the C-Arm is parked or positioned normally for startup. Check mechanical locks, brakes, carriage position, orbital movement, column movement, impact damage, binding, and misaligned components.

steps.title: Inspect External and Accessible Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Power the system down before reseating connectors. Inspect accessible cables between the C-Arm, workstation, monitor cart, power components, and system interface areas for loose connectors, bent pins, damaged jackets, crushed cables, or strain.

steps.title: Check for Recent Service, Transport, Cleaning, or Impact
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the system was recently moved, cleaned, impacted, serviced, stored unusually, or had panels opened or cables pulled. Inspect the area connected to any recent event.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Interlock Areas Without Deep Disassembly
steps.instructions: Look for visible interlock switches, actuator tabs, magnets, latch points, switch brackets, bent hardware, disconnected wiring, or pinched wiring. Do not bypass, tape, or defeat interlock circuits.

steps.title: Power Cycle After Correcting Simple Issues
steps.instructions: If a loose cover, connector, or obvious mechanical condition was corrected, power the system fully off, wait briefly, power it back on, and check whether INTERLOCK FAILURE returns.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External, Mechanical, or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Use findings to classify the problem. If no visible external cause is found, suspect an internal interlock circuit, wiring, switch, connector, or control hardware issue and escalate for service.

CCR.Complaint: GE OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displays INTERLOCK FAILURE during power-up and shuts down before completing startup.
CCR.Cause: Interlock circuit state incorrect during startup. External checks performed for power, covers, mechanical position, cables, connectors, and accessible interlock areas. Persistent fault indicates likely interlock switch, wiring, connector, or internal interlock circuit failure.
CCR.Resolution: Verified reported INTERLOCK FAILURE. Checked power source, visible panels and covers, mechanical positioning, external connections, and accessible interlock components. Error persisted. Removed C-Arm from service, labeled Out of Service, and referred for repair or qualified service evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: FOOTSWITCH SECURITY ERROR
description: Troubleshooting guide for FOOTSWITCH SECURITY ERROR on the GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm, where the X-ray security line does not match the X-ray control signal and may cause X-ray disablement or system shutdown.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the C-Arm is not being used for an active procedure. If imaging is needed, have clinical staff switch to another approved imaging system. Do not repeatedly attempt X-ray activation while the FOOTSWITCH SECURITY ERROR is active.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed message is FOOTSWITCH SECURITY ERROR. Note whether the error appears during startup, when the footswitch is connected, or when X-ray is attempted. Identify whether the left pedal, right pedal, hand switch, or all X-ray controls are affected.

steps.title: Inspect the Footswitch for Damage or Sticking
steps.instructions: Inspect the footswitch housing, pedal movement, cable strain relief, and connector. Look for fluid intrusion, cracked housing, crushed cable, bent pins, stuck pedal movement, or impact damage.

steps.title: Check Footswitch Connection and Seating
steps.instructions: Power the system down according to facility procedure. Disconnect and reconnect the footswitch connector. Inspect for bent pins, contamination, looseness, damaged locking hardware, or poor seating. Power the system back on and check whether the error returns.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Footswitch if Available
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible OEC footswitch if available. Power the system on and verify whether the FOOTSWITCH SECURITY ERROR returns. If the error clears, the original footswitch or cable is the likely cause.

steps.title: Check the X-Ray Hand Control if Applicable
steps.instructions: Inspect the X-ray hand switch or hand control connection if installed. Look for damaged cable, loose connector, stuck exposure button, contamination, or physical stress. Determine whether the fault is footswitch-related, hand-control-related, or present regardless of external control.

steps.title: Inspect External Cable Routing and Strain Points
steps.instructions: Trace the footswitch and control cables. Check for pinching under wheels, stretching near the C-Arm base, crushed insulation, sharp bends, or intermittent movement-related faults.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Accessory Configuration
steps.instructions: Verify the connected footswitch or X-ray control is compatible with the OEC 9900 Series. Check whether the error began after a footswitch swap, repair, loaner accessory, cable change, or cleaning event.

steps.title: Check for Contamination or Fluid Intrusion
steps.instructions: Inspect the footswitch and connector area for moisture, dried residue, corrosion, or cleaning chemical buildup. Do not use the footswitch if fluid intrusion is suspected.

steps.title: Isolate Device-Side Failure
steps.instructions: If the error remains with known-good external controls, document that external controls and cables were ruled out. Remove the C-Arm from service and escalate for service-level troubleshooting of the control input path, connector interface, internal wiring, or X-ray control/security circuitry.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm displays FOOTSWITCH SECURITY ERROR and system shuts down or will not enable X-ray when the footswitch or X-ray control is connected or activated.
CCR.Cause: Footswitch security line did not match the X-ray control signal. Footswitch, hand control, connector seating, cable condition, accessory compatibility, and visible contamination were checked. Fault remained with known-good compatible control, indicating likely control/security signal path issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed C-Arm from clinical use, inspected external X-ray controls and connectors, tested with known-good compatible footswitch where available, labeled unit Out of Service, and escalated for service evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series C-Arm - Error: HLF OVERTIME
description: Troubleshooting HLF OVERTIME when High Level Fluoro or Digital Cine exceeds the preset time limit and X-rays are terminated to help limit patient radiation dose.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not continue attempting fluoroscopy until the timeout condition is understood and cleared. Remove the C-arm from active imaging use if X-rays will not re-enable or if the alarm cannot be cleared. Use another imaging system if continued imaging is clinically urgent.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Error: HLF OVERTIME. Ask whether High Level Fluoro, Digital Cine, continuous high-dose imaging, or extended X-ray control activation was being used. Confirm whether X-rays stopped automatically and whether the alarm cleared after releasing the control.

steps.title: Release the X-Ray Control
steps.instructions: Have the operator release the active X-ray control, such as the hand switch or footswitch. Confirm the alarm silences, the message clears, and X-rays become available again.

steps.title: Confirm the X-Ray Control Is Not Stuck
steps.instructions: Inspect the footswitch and hand switch for sticking, fluid contamination, damaged housings, jammed buttons, debris, cable strain, or crushed cables. Confirm the exposure control returns freely to its released position.

steps.title: Check Operator Workflow
steps.instructions: Ask whether High Level Fluoro or Digital Cine was held continuously for an extended period. Confirm staff understand the system may terminate X-rays after the preset time limit as part of radiation safety behavior.

steps.title: Check for Repeated or Abnormal Alarm Behavior
steps.instructions: Determine whether the alarm occurs only after extended high-level exposure or appears immediately, randomly, without the control pressed, or with no active exposure request.

steps.title: Inspect External Cables and Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect the hand switch, footswitch, and related cables for loose connectors, bent pins, damaged insulation, liquid contamination, crushed cables, or intermittent switch response. Reseat accessible external connections where appropriate.

steps.title: Verify Normal Imaging Recovery
steps.instructions: Perform a basic functional check according to facility policy. Confirm standard fluoro, High Level Fluoro, and Digital Cine start and stop correctly, the alarm is not present at idle, and X-rays stop when the control is released.

CCR.Complaint: Operator reported GE Healthcare OEC 9900 Series displayed Error: HLF OVERTIME during High Level Fluoro or Digital Cine use. X-rays stopped and alarm sounded.
CCR.Cause: High Level Fluoro or Digital Cine exceeded preset exposure time. System terminated X-rays as a dose-protection function. X-ray control was released and the message cleared.
CCR.Resolution: Released X-ray control, confirmed alarm cleared, verified X-rays re-enabled, inspected footswitch and hand switch for sticking or damage, checked external cables, and confirmed normal imaging operation. Unit returned to service.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE Central Station Central Monitoring Station - Bedside Monitor Not Communicating With Central Station
description: Troubleshooting loss of communication between a GE bedside monitor and CARESCAPE Central Station from external, network, setup, or assignment issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: A bedside monitor communication failure can prevent alarms, waveforms, or vital signs from appearing at the central station. Confirm the patient is still being monitored locally at the bedside monitor. Have clinical staff verify patient condition, active alarms, and vital signs directly at the bedside. Do not rely on the central station display until communication is restored and verified. If central monitoring is required and cannot be restored quickly, move the patient to another monitored bed or use an approved alternate monitoring method. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely while the communication issue is investigated. Why it matters: The bedside monitor may still be functioning, but the central station may not be receiving data or alarms.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Communication Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is isolated to one bed or affects multiple beds. Check the CARESCAPE Central Station for the affected bed sector. Look for messages such as offline, no communication, no data, disconnected, blank waveform area, or missing parameters. Ask clinical staff when the issue started and whether the bedside monitor was moved, rebooted, swapped, admitted, discharged, or reassigned. Expected outcome: You determine whether the issue is a single-bed problem, a unit-wide communication problem, or a patient-association issue. If the issue affects multiple beds, skip ahead to network and central station checks.

steps.title: Check the Bedside Monitor Locally
steps.instructions: Go to the bedside monitor and confirm it is powered on and monitoring the patient correctly. Verify the bedside monitor display is active. Confirm ECG, SpO2, NIBP, invasive pressure, respiration, or other expected parameters are displayed locally. Check for local technical alarms, network indicators, communication icons, or connection warnings. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor is confirmed to be functioning locally. If the bedside monitor itself is frozen, powered off, or not acquiring patient data, troubleshoot the bedside monitor first before focusing on the central station.

steps.title: Confirm the Patient Is on the Correct Bedside Monitor
steps.instructions: Verify that the monitor being viewed at the bedside is the monitor expected to report to that central station bed sector. Check the bed label, room number, care unit, and patient association at the bedside monitor. Compare it to the bed sector shown on the CARESCAPE Central Station. Confirm the monitor was not moved from another room without updating the bed assignment. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor and central station are looking for the same bed, room, and patient context. If the bed label or unit assignment is incorrect, correct the assignment according to site workflow and verify communication returns. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check the Network Cable at the Bedside
steps.instructions: A loose or damaged network cable is one of the most common external causes of central station communication loss. Inspect the network cable connected to the bedside monitor, docking station, wall jack, or monitor network adapter. Confirm the cable is fully seated at both ends. Look for broken locking tabs, crushed cable, bent connector pins, or signs of strain. Reseat the cable if safe to do so. Expected outcome: A secure physical network path is confirmed between the bedside monitor and the wall or network connection point. If reseating the cable restores central station communication, monitor briefly and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Network Link Lights If Visible
steps.instructions: Network link lights can help confirm whether the monitor has a live physical network connection. Check the bedside network port, docking station, wall jack, or nearby switch port if accessible. Look for active link or activity lights. Compare the affected connection to a known working bedside monitor connection if possible. Expected outcome: A normal network link should show active connection or activity indicators. If there are no link lights on the affected connection but a known-good cable and monitor work elsewhere, suspect the wall jack, patch cable, switch port, or network infrastructure.

steps.title: Swap with Known-Good External Components When Safe
steps.instructions: Use controlled swaps to isolate whether the failure follows the monitor, cable, or location. Swap the network cable with a known-good cable if available. If permitted by site policy, connect the affected bedside monitor to a known-good network jack. If clinically appropriate, compare with another bedside monitor in the same room or nearby bed. Expected outcome: If the issue follows the bedside monitor, suspect monitor configuration, network settings, or monitor hardware. If the issue stays with the room or jack, suspect the network drop, patch panel, switch port, or infrastructure. If the issue resolves after replacing the cable, document the cable as the cause and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Bedside Monitor Is Assigned to the Correct Central Station
steps.instructions: Communication may fail if the monitor is on the wrong care unit, wrong bed, wrong network group, or wrong monitoring zone. Check the bedside monitor’s care unit, bed name, room assignment, and central monitoring destination according to site workflow. Verify the central station has the bed sector configured and available. Confirm the patient was not discharged, transferred, or admitted under a different bed location. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor is assigned to the correct central station and bed sector. If correcting the assignment restores communication, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Duplicate or Conflicting Bed Assignments
steps.instructions: A bedside monitor may not communicate correctly if another device is assigned to the same bed or if the central station is expecting a different device. Look for duplicate bed labels or duplicate patient sectors at the central station. Check whether another monitor is still associated with the same bed. Ask staff whether a monitor was recently swapped, transported, cleaned, or returned from another room. Expected outcome: Only the correct bedside monitor is associated with the intended bed sector. If a duplicate or stale assignment is found, correct it according to site workflow and verify the central station receives data.

steps.title: Restart Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: A reboot may clear a temporary software or communication lockup, but it should not be done while it could interrupt active patient monitoring. Confirm clinical staff approve the restart. Ensure alternate patient monitoring is available if needed. Restart the bedside monitor only if local monitoring interruption is acceptable. If the central station sector appears frozen or only one sector is affected, follow site procedure for refreshing or restarting the affected application or workstation. Expected outcome: Temporary communication or software lockups clear after a controlled restart. If communication returns and remains stable, document the restart and final status.

steps.title: Check Whether the Issue Is Central Station-Wide
steps.instructions: If multiple bedside monitors are not communicating, treat the issue as a possible central station, gateway, network switch, VLAN, server, or interface problem. Check whether other beds on the same unit are communicating. Check whether other central stations can see the same bedside monitors. Ask whether there was recent network maintenance, power interruption, server work, or application restart. Inspect the central station workstation for frozen display, network disconnect, error banners, or abnormal system behavior. Expected outcome: You determine whether the issue is isolated to one monitor or part of a larger central monitoring communication failure. If multiple beds are affected, escalate according to site policy to Clinical Engineering, IT/network support, and the monitoring vendor as appropriate.

steps.title: Verify Communication After Any Correction
steps.instructions: Do not close the issue immediately after the bed appears online. Confirm live waveform data is updating at the central station. Verify current vital signs match the bedside monitor. Confirm alarms from the bedside monitor are visible or routed correctly according to hospital policy. Ask clinical staff to confirm the correct patient and bed are displayed. Expected outcome: The central station is receiving accurate, current data from the correct bedside monitor. If data is delayed, intermittent, or assigned to the wrong patient, continue troubleshooting.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported that the bedside monitor in Room 412 was not communicating with the GE CARESCAPE Central Station.
CCR.Cause: Found the bedside monitor operating locally, but the network cable at the wall jack had a broken latch and no stable network link.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the network cable, verified the correct bed assignment, confirmed live ECG waveform and vital signs were updating at the CARESCAPE Central Station, and returned the bed to service.

helpfulDetails: Patient was verified safe before troubleshooting.
Correct bed, room, and patient assignment were checked.
Wall jack or switch link light status.
Whether issue affected one bed or multiple beds.
Any recent monitor swap, room transfer, or patient discharge/admit.
Waveform and vital sign update status after correction.
Alarm behavior after communication was restored.
Environmental factors such as recent network maintenance, power interruption, server work, or workstation restart.
Indicator lights, network icons, link lights, or warning messages observed.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE Central Station Central Monitoring Station - Waveform Dropout or Delayed Vital Sign Updates
description: Troubleshooting intermittent waveform loss, delayed parameter updates, or slow bedside monitor data refresh at the central station.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Waveform dropout or delayed vital sign updates can affect alarm awareness and patient visibility at the central station. Action: Confirm the patient is being actively monitored at the bedside monitor. Notify clinical staff that central monitoring data may be delayed or intermittent. Have staff rely on the bedside monitor until central station display accuracy is confirmed. Do not assume the central station is showing real-time patient data until verified. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely while the central display issue is investigated. Why it matters: The bedside monitor is the immediate source of patient data. A central station display delay may create a false sense of visibility.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is waveform dropout, delayed numeric updates, missing parameters, or a complete bed communication loss. Action: Ask staff which bed, waveform, or parameter is affected. Check whether ECG, SpO2, NIBP, invasive pressure, respiration, or all parameters are delayed. Compare the central station display to the bedside monitor in real time. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to one parameter, one bed, multiple beds, or the entire central station. If the central station updates correctly after comparison and no dropout occurs, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check the Bedside Monitor First
steps.instructions: Verify that the bedside monitor itself has stable waveforms and current vital signs. Action: Go to the affected bedside monitor. Confirm the waveform is present and stable locally. Check for loose ECG leads, disconnected SpO2 probes, inactive modules, or paused measurements. Verify that the bedside monitor is not in standby, discharge, transport, demo, or disconnected mode. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor should display clean, current patient data. If the bedside monitor is not producing stable data, correct the bedside issue first. The central station cannot display data that the source monitor is not providing.

steps.title: Confirm the Issue Is Not Patient Lead or Accessory Related
steps.instructions: A waveform dropout at the central station may be caused by poor signal quality at the patient connection. Action: Inspect ECG lead wires, trunk cable, SpO2 sensor, pressure cables, and other connected accessories. Look for intermittent movement-related dropout. Ask clinical staff whether the issue happens during patient movement, repositioning, bathing, transport, or lead changes. Swap a suspected external accessory if available and safe to do so. Expected outcome: The waveform should remain stable at the bedside and central station after poor accessories or connections are corrected. If replacing or reseating the external accessory resolves the dropout, document the accessory issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Bed Assignment and Patient Sector
steps.instructions: Confirm the bedside monitor is assigned to the correct bed and is displayed in the correct central station sector. Action: Verify the bed label shown at the bedside monitor. Compare it to the bed label at the CARESCAPE Central Station. Confirm the patient sector is not showing an old bed, wrong unit, discharged patient, or duplicate bed association. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor and central station should show the same bed association. If the wrong bed is assigned, correct the bed assignment through the approved clinical workflow or escalate to the appropriate system administrator.

steps.title: Check for a Single-Bed Versus Multi-Bed Problem
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue affects only one monitored bed or multiple beds. Action: Review other patient sectors on the CARESCAPE Central Station. Ask staff if other beds have delayed updates or waveform dropout. Compare one known-good bedside monitor to the central station. Expected outcome: If only one bed is affected, focus on that bedside monitor, cable path, network jack, or bed assignment. If multiple beds are affected, suspect a central station, network, switch, gateway, or system communication issue.

steps.title: Inspect Network and Physical Connections
steps.instructions: External network problems can cause delayed updates, intermittent waveform loss, or communication dropouts. Action: Check the bedside monitor network cable if wired. Verify the cable is fully seated at the monitor, wall jack, and any approved local network connection. Inspect for damaged cables, loose connectors, bent clips, or cables under tension. Check the central station network connection if accessible and safe. Do not move network cables between unknown ports unless following site-approved network labeling and configuration practices. Expected outcome: Network cabling should be secure, undamaged, and connected to the correct labeled ports. If a loose or damaged cable is found and corrected, verify stable real-time waveform updates before returning the setup to normal use.

steps.title: Check for Local Display or Workstation Lag
steps.instructions: Sometimes the patient data is being received, but the central station display is slow, frozen, or not refreshing properly. Action: Look for delayed mouse response, frozen patient sectors, slow screen redraw, or repeated display lag. Check whether only one display is affected or all CARESCAPE Central Station screens are affected. Confirm whether alarms and numerics are updating while waveforms lag, or whether all data is delayed. Expected outcome: The central station should respond normally and refresh patient data without visible lag. If the central station appears frozen or sluggish, follow your facility-approved restart or escalation process. Do not reboot a central monitoring station without notifying clinical staff and ensuring patient monitoring coverage.

steps.title: Review Alarm and Communication Indicators
steps.instructions: Communication indicators can help distinguish a signal issue from a network or central station issue. Action: Look for monitor offline messages, communication loss indicators, delayed alarm banners, missing waveforms, or stale numeric values. Check whether alarm annunciation still occurs when waveform dropout happens. Confirm whether the bedside monitor shows any network or central monitoring communication alerts. Expected outcome: Communication alarms or offline indicators help identify whether the issue is network-related rather than patient-signal-related. If alarms are delayed or not annunciating, treat the issue as higher priority and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Verify Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Recent changes often explain central monitoring communication problems. Action: Ask whether the bedside monitor was recently moved, replaced, discharged, admitted, transferred, or reassigned. Check whether the central station, network switch, CARESCAPE Gateway, or monitoring system had recent maintenance. Ask if the issue began after a power outage, network event, software update, or unit move. Expected outcome: Recent changes may identify a configuration, assignment, or network path issue. If the issue started after a known system change, escalate to the monitoring system administrator, network team, or vendor support as appropriate.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Recheck
steps.instructions: After correcting any external issue, verify that updates are stable. Action: Compare the bedside monitor and central station for several minutes. Confirm waveforms are continuous. Confirm numerics update within the expected clinical workflow. Confirm alarms display and annunciate at the central station according to facility policy. Expected outcome: The central station should show current, stable patient data matching the bedside monitor. If the waveform and vital sign updates remain stable, document the resolution and return the system to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported intermittent ECG waveform dropout and delayed vital sign updates for Bed 12 on the GE CARESCAPE Central Station.
CCR.Cause: Bedside monitor waveforms were stable, but the network cable at the bedside monitor was loose and caused intermittent central station communication.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the bedside network cable, verified correct bed assignment, confirmed stable waveform and numeric updates at the central station, and returned monitoring path to service.

helpfulDetails: Outlet or UPS status checked if central station power was involved
Whether one bed or multiple beds were affected
Any monitor offline or communication messages
Network cable or wall jack checked
Recent bed move, discharge, admit, or system change
Indicator lights observed on monitor, network jack, switch, workstation, or connected equipment
Final device or monitoring path status

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE Central Station Central Monitoring Station - Alarm Not Annunciating At Central Station
description: Troubleshooting missed, silent, delayed, or non-displayed alarms at a GE CARESCAPE Central Station.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat any report of alarms not annunciating at the central station as a patient safety issue. Action: Confirm the bedside monitor is actively alarming at the patient location. Have clinical staff respond to the patient at the bedside. Do not rely on the central station until alarm display and audible annunciation are verified. If needed, assign direct bedside observation or move the patient to another monitored bed. Expected outcome: Patient safety is protected before technical troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: A central station alarm issue can delay clinical response if staff assume alarms are being displayed or heard centrally.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is not working. Action: Ask clinical staff what alarm occurred and where it was seen. Determine whether the issue is: No audible alarm. No visual alarm. Alarm appears late. Alarm appears at bedside but not central. Alarm appears on one central station but not another. Alarm message appears but sound is muted or too low. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to audio, display, routing, network communication, or alarm configuration. If the issue is only low volume or muted audio, continue with audio checks before assuming a system failure.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Patient Sector and Bed Assignment
steps.instructions: Verify that the correct bed is assigned to the expected central station sector. Action: Check that the patient’s bedside monitor is associated with the correct bed label. Confirm the patient sector is visible on the CARESCAPE Central Station. Verify that the patient has not been admitted, transferred, discharged, or moved to a different monitoring sector incorrectly. Expected outcome: The correct patient and bed appear at the central station. If the wrong bed, wrong patient, or missing patient sector is found, correct the patient association or escalate to the clinical systems/network support process as appropriate.

steps.title: Check Whether the Bedside Monitor Is Communicating
steps.instructions: A central station cannot annunciate alarms from a monitor that is not communicating properly. Action: Look for offline, disconnected, no data, or communication-related messages at the central station. Verify that waveforms and numeric parameters are updating. Compare the bedside monitor display to the central station display. Expected outcome: Live data from the bedside monitor is visible and updating at the central station. If live data is missing or frozen, troubleshoot the bedside monitor connection, network jack, cable, wireless connection, or monitoring network before focusing on alarm settings.

steps.title: Confirm the Alarm Is Active at the Bedside Monitor
steps.instructions: Verify that the bedside device is actually generating an alarm. Action: Check the bedside monitor for active physiological or technical alarms. Confirm the alarm priority and message. Check whether the alarm was already acknowledged, paused, silenced, or resolved before it could annunciate centrally. Expected outcome: The alarm condition is confirmed at the source device. If the bedside monitor is not alarming, the issue may be patient condition, parameter settings, alarm limits, or monitor configuration rather than the central station.

steps.title: Check Alarm Pause, Silence, and Audio Status
steps.instructions: Confirm that alarm audio has not been intentionally or accidentally silenced. Action: Check the CARESCAPE Central Station for alarm pause, audio pause, silence, or speaker-muted indicators. Verify the audible alarm volume is not set too low. Confirm the central station speakers are functioning. Check whether the workstation audio is routed through external speakers, built-in speakers, or another audio output device. Expected outcome: Alarm audio is enabled and loud enough to be heard in the clinical area. If alarm audio was paused or muted and restoring it resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Verify Workstation Sound Output
steps.instructions: If the alarm appears visually but is not heard, check the central station audio path. Action: Confirm the central station is not muted at the operating system or hardware level if accessible within approved support procedures. Check external speaker power, volume, and cable connection if external speakers are used. Inspect for loose, damaged, disconnected, or swapped audio cables. Listen for any normal system tones or test sounds if permitted by site procedure. Expected outcome: The central station can produce audible output. If visual alarms display correctly but no sound is produced, the issue may be speaker, audio output, workstation configuration, or internal audio hardware.

steps.title: Check Alarm Routing and Unit Assignment
steps.instructions: Confirm the alarm is supposed to annunciate at this central station. Action: Verify the patient bed is assigned to the correct unit, care area, or central monitoring group. Confirm that the alarm is not routed to a different central station, remote viewer, or surveillance area. Check whether the issue affects only one bed, one unit, or all monitored beds. Expected outcome: The affected bed is assigned to the correct central station alarm group. If the bed is routed incorrectly, correct the assignment if within Clinical Engineering scope, or escalate to the monitoring system administrator/network support team.

steps.title: Compare With Another Known-Good Bed
steps.instructions: Use comparison to determine whether the issue is isolated or system-wide. Action: Check a nearby bed that is known to be communicating properly. Confirm whether alarms from that bed display and sound at the central station. Compare sector behavior, waveform updates, alarm messages, and audio response. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as one-bed-specific, one-sector-specific, or central-station-wide. If only one bed is affected, focus on that bedside monitor, network connection, bed assignment, and patient association. If multiple beds are affected, focus on the central station, network path, switch, gateway, or alarm configuration.

steps.title: Check Network and Communication Indicators
steps.instructions: Alarm annunciation depends on reliable communication between the bedside monitor, monitoring network, and central station. Action: Inspect network cables connected to the central station workstation. Check for disconnected, damaged, or loose Ethernet connections. Verify network link lights where visible. Confirm whether other central monitoring functions are affected, such as waveform display, patient data updates, or remote viewing. Expected outcome: The central station has active network communication. If network communication is degraded or lost, escalate according to site procedure for monitoring network, switch, gateway, or server support.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: Do not restart a central monitoring station without protecting active patient monitoring. Action: Notify clinical staff before any reboot or application restart. Confirm another monitoring method is available during downtime. Restart the CARESCAPE Central Station application or workstation only if permitted by site procedure and patient monitoring coverage is maintained.
