After restart, verify that patient sectors return, waveforms update, and alarms annunciate correctly. Expected outcome: The system returns to normal operation without compromising patient monitoring. If the issue clears after restart, continue observing for recurrence and document the restart.

steps.title: Verify Resolution
steps.instructions: Confirm the central station is functioning correctly before returning it to normal use. Action: Verify live waveforms and numeric values are updating. Confirm the correct bed labels and patient associations are displayed. Confirm audible alarm annunciation is restored. Confirm clinical staff can hear and see alarms from the expected location. Expected outcome: The central station displays and annunciates alarms correctly for the assigned monitored beds. If the alarm annunciation issue is resolved, document the verified status and stop.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that alarms from a monitored patient were not annunciating at the GE CARESCAPE Central Station.
CCR.Cause: The bedside monitor was communicating, but the central station alarm audio was muted and the alarm volume was not audible in the nursing station.
CCR.Resolution: Restored central station alarm audio, verified live patient data, confirmed audible alarm annunciation with staff, and returned the central station to service.

helpfulDetails: Correct patient and bed label verified
Alarm visual display present or absent
Alarm audio present, muted, delayed, or low volume
Network cable and link status checked
Whether issue followed one bed, one sector, or entire central station

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE Central Station Central Monitoring Station - Incorrect Bed Label, Patient Association, Or Unit Assignment
description: Troubleshooting incorrect bed labels, patient associations, or unit assignments affecting patient display, alarm routing, or central station monitoring.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Incorrect bed or patient association at a central station can create a serious patient safety risk. Action: Confirm clinical staff are aware that the displayed patient, bed, or unit assignment may be incorrect. Verify the patient’s identity directly at the bedside before relying on central station data. Confirm alarms are being monitored at the bedside monitor while the central station issue is investigated. Do not make clinical decisions based only on a questionable central station patient association. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely while the display or assignment issue is corrected. Why it matters: A central station may show valid physiologic data under the wrong bed or patient label if the device, network, or admission association is incorrect.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Action: Confirm exactly what is incorrect. Wrong bed label shown at the CARESCAPE Central Station. Patient name shown under the wrong bed. Bed assigned to the wrong unit. Bedside monitor data appearing in the wrong patient sector. Patient demographic mismatch between the bedside monitor, central station, and ADT system. Expected outcome: The issue is clearly identified before changes are made. If the issue is only a display misunderstanding or old patient information that can be safely corrected through normal clinical workflow, correct it and stop.

steps.title: Compare Central Station Display to the Bedside Monitor
steps.instructions: Action: Go to the patient’s bedside monitor and compare the bed label. Compare the room number. Compare the patient name or ID, if displayed. Compare the unit or care area. Compare the active monitoring status. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor and central station should agree. If the bedside monitor itself has the wrong bed label or patient association, the issue likely originates at the bedside monitor configuration or patient admission workflow. Correct the bedside-side information per facility process and verify the central station updates.

steps.title: Confirm the Patient Is Physically in the Correct Bed or Room
steps.instructions: Action: Confirm with clinical staff whether the patient was recently transferred, discharged, moved, or swapped between rooms. Check whether the patient was moved before the bedside monitor or central station assignment was updated. Expected outcome: The equipment assignment should match the patient’s actual location. If the patient was recently moved and the system has not been updated, have clinical staff complete the proper transfer, discharge, admit, or bed reassignment workflow. Verify the central station updates afterward.

steps.title: Check for Duplicate or Old Patient Associations
steps.instructions: Action: Look for the same patient appearing in more than one bed sector. Look for an old patient name remaining after discharge. Look for a bed showing active data but assigned to a previous patient. Look for a new patient connected to a monitor still associated with the prior patient. Expected outcome: Each monitored patient should have only one correct central station association. If duplicate or stale patient information is found, follow the facility’s normal discharge, transfer, or reassociation process. Confirm the corrected patient appears in the proper bed sector.

steps.title: Verify Bed Label and Care Unit Assignment
steps.instructions: Action: Confirm the bed label assigned to the bedside monitor matches the intended unit and central station sector. Check for incorrect unit naming, room number mismatch, or wrong care area selection. Confirm the bed is assigned to the correct monitoring group or central station view. Expected outcome: The central station should display the bedside monitor in the correct unit and bed location. If the bed was assigned to the wrong care unit, correct the assignment through the approved configuration or clinical workflow. If Clinical Engineering does not have access to change this safely, escalate to the monitoring system administrator or IT/network support.

steps.title: Check Network and Communication Status
steps.instructions: Action: Verify the bedside monitor is communicating normally with the CARESCAPE Central Station. Check for intermittent connection. Check for an offline or disconnected bed. Check for delayed update after patient transfer. Check for incorrect data appearing after network recovery. Check for multiple monitors with similar bed labels. Expected outcome: The central station should receive current data from the correct bedside monitor. If communication is unstable, troubleshoot network connectivity, bedside monitor connection, wall network jack, patch cable, docking station, and switch port as appropriate.

steps.title: Confirm ADT or Demographic Interface Behavior
steps.instructions: Action: Determine whether patient demographics come from an ADT interface, manual admission, or another hospital system. Check whether the mismatch appears in the CARESCAPE Central Station only. Check whether the mismatch appears in the bedside monitor only. Check whether the mismatch appears in the EMR/ADT system only. Check whether the mismatch appears in multiple systems at the same time. Expected outcome: The source of the incorrect demographic information should be identified. If the incorrect patient association appears to originate from ADT, registration, or interface feed behavior, escalate to IT or the appropriate clinical informatics team.

steps.title: Refresh or Reopen the Central Station View
steps.instructions: Action: If the bedside monitor and source systems appear correct, refresh the central station display using approved local workflow. Change views and return to the patient sector, if approved by local workflow. Log out and back in, if appropriate. Restart the central station application or workstation only if patient monitoring coverage is maintained. Expected outcome: The central station should reload the correct bed and patient association. If the display corrects after refresh, monitor briefly to confirm the issue does not return. Document the action and stop.

steps.title: Check for Similar Bed Names or Configuration Conflicts
steps.instructions: Action: Look for duplicate bed labels. Look for old rooms still active in the system. Look for similar unit names. Look for an incorrectly assigned monitor location. Check whether there was a recent unit expansion, bed move, or central station configuration change. Expected outcome: Each monitor location should have a unique and correct bed identity. If a configuration conflict is suspected, do not make unverified changes. Escalate to the monitoring system administrator, IT network team, or GE support depending on facility process.

steps.title: Verify Alarm Routing After Correction
steps.instructions: Action: Confirm the correct patient appears in the correct sector. Confirm waveforms and numerics match the bedside monitor. Confirm alarm messages appear under the correct bed. Confirm alarm audio and visual indicators route to the correct central station. Expected outcome: Monitoring and alarm routing are correct before the issue is considered resolved. If alarm routing remains questionable, remove the affected bed from reliance on central monitoring until the configuration is verified.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported that the CARESCAPE Central Station was showing the wrong patient name in Bed 12 after a room transfer.
CCR.Cause: Bedside monitor information and recent transfer workflow were reviewed, and the central station still displayed stale patient association after the patient move.
CCR.Resolution: Patient association was corrected through approved workflow, central station display was refreshed, and Bed 12 waveform, numerics, patient name, and alarm routing were verified against the bedside monitor.

helpfulDetails: Accessories swapped, including network cable, patch cable, wall network jack, docking station, or monitor connection if applicable.
Power behavior, including workstation power status if workstation power was involved.
Environmental factors, including recent patient transfer, discharge, room move, bed swap, unit expansion, or central station configuration change.
Indicator lights, including network link lights, monitor communication status, offline indicators, or central station connection status.

Final device status, including whether the affected bed, monitor, central station sector, or monitoring path was returned to service, removed from clinical reliance, labeled Out of Service, or escalated to IT, clinical informatics, GE support, or system administration.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE Central Station Central Monitoring Station - Network Switch / CIC Pro / CARESCAPE Gateway Communication Issue
description: Troubleshooting communication loss between CARESCAPE Central Station, CIC Pro, network switches, bedside monitors, or CARESCAPE Gateway interfaces.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: A central monitoring communication issue can prevent alarms, waveforms, or patient data from displaying where staff expect to see them. Action: Confirm patients are being monitored directly at the bedside. Notify clinical staff that central monitoring or gateway communication may be unreliable. Have staff verify alarms locally at the bedside monitor. Do not rely on the central station as the only alarm observation point until communication is restored. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely while the network communication issue is investigated. Why it matters: A network issue may affect visibility of alarms and patient data at the central station, even if the bedside monitor is still functioning. If patient monitoring is protected and bedside monitoring is functional, continue.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Communication Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is not communicating. Action: Determine whether the issue affects one bedside monitor. Determine whether the issue affects one patient sector. Determine whether the issue affects one CIC Pro station. Determine whether the issue affects one CARESCAPE Central Station. Determine whether the issue affects multiple central stations. Determine whether the issue affects the CARESCAPE Gateway interface. Determine whether the issue affects an entire unit or VLAN/network segment. Expected outcome: The failure pattern helps separate a single-device issue from a network switch, gateway, or system-wide communication issue. If only one bedside monitor is affected, focus on that monitor, wall jack, cable, and assigned bed configuration first. If multiple devices are affected, continue network path troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Whether Bedside Monitoring Is Still Working Locally
steps.instructions: Go to the affected bedside monitor. Action: Verify the monitor is powered on. Confirm ECG, SpO2, NIBP, invasive pressure, or other active parameters are displaying locally. Check whether local alarms are active and audible. Confirm the patient is assigned to the correct bed or care area if applicable. Expected outcome: The bedside monitor should still show patient data locally even if central communication is down. If the bedside monitor itself is malfunctioning, troubleshoot the bedside monitor first. If the bedside monitor is functioning locally but not visible centrally, continue.

steps.title: Verify the Scope at the Central Station
steps.instructions: Check the CARESCAPE Central Station or CIC Pro display. Action: Look for messages such as Bed offline. Look for messages such as No communication. Look for messages such as Monitor disconnected. Look for messages such as Data unavailable. Look for messages such as Gateway communication failure. Look for messages such as Blank patient sector. Look for messages such as Missing waveforms or delayed data. Confirm whether the issue is isolated to one bed or multiple beds. Expected outcome: The central station should help identify whether communication loss is limited or widespread. If only one bed is offline, continue with the bedside network path. If many beds are offline at once, skip ahead to switch, gateway, or network infrastructure checks.

steps.title: Check the Physical Network Cable at the Bedside Monitor
steps.instructions: Inspect the network connection for the affected bedside monitor or docking location. Action: Verify the Ethernet cable is fully seated. Check for a broken latch, loose connector, damaged cable jacket, or kinked cable. Confirm the cable is connected to the correct wall jack or network port. If available and allowed by site policy, swap with a known-good network cable. Expected outcome: A loose or damaged network cable should be corrected, and communication may restore after the device reconnects. If communication restores, confirm the bed appears correctly at the central station and stop.

steps.title: Check the Wall Jack or Network Drop
steps.instructions: Inspect the wall port serving the affected monitor. Action: Confirm the Ethernet cable is connected to the correct labeled jack. Look for damaged wall plates, loose ports, bent contacts, or signs of impact. If the room has another known-good clinical network jack approved for monitoring, test from that jack according to site policy. Avoid using random network jacks that may be assigned to a different VLAN or non-monitoring network. Expected outcome: The monitor should communicate when connected to a valid, active monitoring network jack. If the issue follows the wall jack, escalate to IT/network support for jack, patch panel, or switch-port investigation.

steps.title: Check Network Link Lights Where Accessible
steps.instructions: At the bedside device, dock, switch, or patch point, check link/activity indicators if visible and accessible. Action: Look for network link lights. Compare with a known-working bed or station. Verify that the port is not dark, intermittently flashing abnormally, or physically damaged. Expected outcome: A valid network connection usually shows link/activity at the active network port. If there are no link lights on a known powered device, suspect cable, jack, patching, switch port, or network configuration.

steps.title: Verify CARESCAPE Central Station or CIC Pro Power and Network Connection
steps.instructions: Check the central station workstation or CIC Pro station. Action: Confirm the station is powered on and not frozen. Verify the network cable is connected. Check that the workstation is on the correct clinical monitoring network. Look for disconnected cables behind the workstation, under the desk, or at the wall jack. Confirm the keyboard, mouse, display, and system are responsive. Expected outcome: The central station should be powered, responsive, and physically connected to the correct network. If the station is frozen but other stations are working, perform a controlled restart only if approved by site policy and patient monitoring coverage is maintained.

steps.title: Compare With Another Central Station or Viewer
steps.instructions: Determine whether the same beds are visible on another central station. Action: Check a nearby CARESCAPE Central Station, CIC Pro station, or approved viewer. Compare whether the same patient sectors are online or offline. Confirm whether alarms and waveforms appear normally elsewhere. Expected outcome: If another central station displays the beds correctly, the issue may be local to one workstation, network port, or software session. If all central stations lose the same beds, suspect upstream network, switch, gateway, or bedside monitor communication.

steps.title: Check Network Switch Power and Basic Status
steps.instructions: If the issue affects multiple beds or an entire area, check the related network switch if accessible to Clinical Engineering. Action: Verify the switch has power. Check for power supply alarms, dark status LEDs, or abnormal indicator lights. Confirm network cables are seated. Look for accidental unplugging, recent work in the closet, or a tripped power strip/UPS issue. Do not move patch cables unless authorized and properly documented. Expected outcome: The switch should be powered, stable, and showing active links on expected monitoring ports. If the switch is powered off, unstable, or showing abnormal status, escalate to IT/network support immediately and keep clinical leadership informed.

steps.title: Check for Recent Network or Room Changes
steps.instructions: Communication failures often follow physical changes. Action: Ask whether a room move occurred recently. Ask whether a monitor swap occurred recently. Ask whether a bed reassignment occurred recently. Ask whether network closet work occurred recently. Ask whether a switch replacement occurred recently. Ask whether a patch panel change occurred recently. Ask whether a software update occurred recently. Ask whether a power outage occurred recently. Ask whether construction or cabling work occurred recently. Expected outcome: Recent changes may explain why the bedside monitor, CIC Pro, CARESCAPE Central Station, or Gateway is no longer communicating correctly. If the issue started after network work, involve IT/network support with the affected rooms, switch ports, and timestamps.

steps.title: Verify CARESCAPE Gateway Communication
steps.instructions: If the issue involves data not reaching another system, check the CARESCAPE Gateway path. Action: Confirm whether bedside data is visible at the central station. Confirm whether the issue is only with data leaving the monitoring network, such as export to an EMR, interface engine, or downstream system. Check the Gateway workstation/server power and network connection if accessible. Look for gateway-related error messages or interface communication warnings. Expected outcome: If central monitoring works but downstream data does not transfer, the issue may be isolated to the CARESCAPE Gateway or interface path. If the Gateway is not communicating, escalate to the appropriate GE, IT, or interface support team according to site policy.

steps.title: Check for IP Address, Bed Assignment, or Configuration Mismatch
steps.instructions: Do not assume hardware failure if communication fails after a device swap or bed move. Action: Confirm the bedside monitor is assigned to the correct bed/location. Verify that the central station sector corresponds to the correct care area. Check whether the device was recently replaced with another monitor. Confirm whether the replacement device is approved and configured for that unit. Expected outcome: The monitor should be assigned to the correct location and recognized by the central monitoring system. If configuration appears wrong, escalate to the authorized system administrator, GE support, or Clinical Engineering staff trained on CARESCAPE network configuration.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: A restart may restore a local workstation or monitor connection, but it should not be the first step during active monitoring. Action: Confirm patient monitoring coverage before restarting anything. Restart only the affected device or workstation if allowed by site policy. Do not restart network switches, central stations, CIC Pro systems, or gateway servers without understanding the patient monitoring impact. After restart, confirm communication, waveforms, parameters, and alarms return. Expected outcome: A temporary software or session issue may clear after a controlled restart. If the problem returns, treat it as unresolved and escalate.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm and Waveform Recovery
steps.instructions: Once communication appears restored, verify more than just the bed name. Action: Confirm the patient sector is visible. Verify ECG waveform or active parameters are updating. Confirm alarm status is displayed appropriately. Check that delayed, stale, or frozen data is not being mistaken for live data. Ask clinical staff to confirm the patient appears correctly at the central station. Expected outcome: The central station should show current patient data, live waveforms, and expected alarm behavior. If data is incomplete, delayed, or unstable, continue escalation.

CCR.Complaint: ICU reported that multiple beds were offline at the GE CARESCAPE Central Station and one CIC Pro station was not receiving patient data.
CCR.Cause: Found bedside monitors functioning locally, but the affected unit switch had no active link on several monitoring ports after recent network closet work.
CCR.Resolution: Verified patient safety with clinical staff, documented affected beds and switch location, escalated to IT/network support, and confirmed central station communication restored after switch port issue was corrected.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or central station messages displayed
Accessories swapped, including network cable, wall jack, or workstation connection
Power behavior, including outlet, UPS, switch, workstation, or bedside monitor power status
Environmental factors, including recent room moves, network closet work, construction, cabling work, or power outages
Indicator lights, including network link lights, switch status LEDs, workstation status, or gateway status
Final device status, including whether one bed, one station, multiple beds, CIC Pro, CARESCAPE Gateway, or the monitoring network remained affected

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: ACB EEPROM FAIL
description: Troubleshooting guide for ACB EEPROM FAIL on the GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine, indicating an Anesthesia Control Board EEPROM read/write or CRC memory failure that may affect system reliability.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the alarm is active, recurring, or associated with abnormal system behavior. If connected to a patient, have clinical staff transition to an alternate anesthesia machine or approved backup ventilation method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is ACB EEPROM FAIL. Observe whether it occurs during startup, self-test, reboot, checkout, standby, clinical use, or intermittently. Check for related system malfunction messages, repeated resets, startup failures, or other ACB-related alarms.

steps.title: Understand What the Alarm Means
steps.instructions: Treat the alarm as a memory fault on the Anesthesia Control Board. The system is detecting a read/write failure or CRC failure involving EEPROM memory on the ACB.

steps.title: Check Power and Basic External Conditions First
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is connected to a known-good AC power source. Check that the power cord is fully seated, the outlet is powered, and there are no signs of unstable facility power.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Reboot
steps.instructions: Power the machine down according to facility procedure, allow it to fully shut down, restart the system, and observe whether the ACB EEPROM FAIL alarm returns. If it clears, complete checkout before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Check for Additional ACB or System Alarms
steps.instructions: Review the screen, alarm history, or service history for ACB software errors, unexpected reset alarms, ACB communication errors, power-related alarms, or repeated startup failures.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections and Physical Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect externally for loose power cord, damaged cord or plug, liquid intrusion, physical impact damage, contamination, or recent service activity. Verify external modules, cables, and accessories are properly connected.

steps.title: Repeat Startup Observation
steps.instructions: After confirming power and external condition, restart the system again if safe to do so. Observe whether the alarm clears, returns immediately, returns intermittently, or appears with other system malfunction alarms.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm does not return, complete the required anesthesia machine checkout and verify normal display, ventilation readiness, alarm function, and overall system operation. Return the machine to service only after successful verification.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed ACB EEPROM FAIL alarm during startup. Unit removed from patient use for evaluation.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed ACB EEPROM FAIL alarm. Verified stable AC power, inspected external power cord and machine condition, and performed controlled reboot. Alarm returned after startup, indicating persistent Anesthesia Control Board memory fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation or qualified repair due to recurring ACB EEPROM FAIL alarm.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: ACB FLASH FAIL
description: Troubleshooting guide for the GE Healthcare Aisys ACB FLASH FAIL alarm caused by code-space CRC failure, system malfunction, or unreliable anesthesia machine operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the ACB FLASH FAIL alarm occurs during setup, checkout, standby, or operation. If connected to a patient, have anesthesia staff transfer the patient to another anesthesia machine or approved backup ventilation and anesthesia support. Do not continue using the machine with an unresolved system malfunction alarm.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is ACB FLASH FAIL. Observe whether the alarm occurs during startup, system checkout, standby, active use, or intermittently after reboot. Look for associated symptoms such as system malfunction messages, failed startup, failed checkout, unresponsive controls, unexpected reset, or abnormal display behavior.

steps.title: Power Down and Reboot the System
steps.instructions: Safely power down the Aisys, wait briefly, and power the system back on. Allow the system to complete startup and observe whether the ACB FLASH FAIL alarm returns. If the alarm clears, run the required checkout and return the machine to service only if checkout passes and the alarm does not return.

steps.title: Check External Power and Basic Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is connected to a known-good AC outlet. Inspect the power cord for damage, looseness, or signs of stress. Verify the machine is not repeatedly switching between AC power and battery. Check for external damage, fluid intrusion, overheating, unusual odor, or signs of impact.

steps.title: Review Recent Events or Service History
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff or review the work order for recent power loss, unexpected shutdown, failed startup, software service activity, ACB-related service, machine movement, impact, fluid spill, or cleaning event. Document any event that may have occurred before the alarm appeared.

steps.title: Repeat Startup Observation After Reboot
steps.instructions: After confirming stable power, restart the machine again if appropriate. Observe whether the alarm returns consistently and confirm whether it appears immediately, during checkout, during standby, or only after a specific sequence.

steps.title: Do Not Attempt Clinical Use if Alarm Returns
steps.instructions: If ACB FLASH FAIL returns after reboot, stop troubleshooting at the user-accessible level. Do not bypass the alarm and do not release the machine for patient use. Remove the machine from service and escalate for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported ACB FLASH FAIL alarm during startup. System malfunction was present and machine was not cleared for patient use.
CCR.Cause: Alarm persisted after reboot and external checks. Stable AC power was verified. Condition indicates likely internal Anesthesia Control Board code-space or flash integrity failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed machine from service, labeled Out of Service, verified AC power and power cord condition, rebooted system, confirmed ACB FLASH FAIL returned, and escalated for bench evaluation and possible ACB replacement.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: ACB HW WATCHDOG
description: Troubleshooting guide for the ACB HW WATCHDOG alarm on a GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine, including patient safety steps, external checks, controlled reboot, checkout verification, and repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the ACB HW WATCHDOG alarm is active. Move the patient to a backup anesthesia machine or approved backup ventilation method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is ACB HW WATCHDOG. Observe whether it occurs during boot-up, self-test, checkout, restart, or intermittent use. Document any system malfunction, failed startup, frozen display, checkout failure, unexpected reset, or loss of normal operation.

steps.title: Check Power Source First
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is connected to a known-good AC outlet. Confirm the power cord is fully seated at the wall and machine. Check for unstable power, switched outlets, or emergency power issues.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious Physical or Environmental Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect the exterior for visible damage, fluid intrusion, impact damage, loose panels, overheating, unusual odor, abnormal fan noise, or blocked vents. Do not perform internal disassembly during basic troubleshooting.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Reboot
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is not in patient use. Power the Aisys down using the normal shutdown process when possible, wait briefly, restore power, and observe startup behavior. Do not repeatedly power cycle without purpose.

steps.title: Repeat Startup Observation
steps.instructions: After reboot, observe whether the alarm returns during boot-up, self-test, or checkout. Record whether the alarm clears temporarily or returns consistently.

steps.title: Run Required Checkout Before Returning to Service
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears, perform the required anesthesia machine checkout. Confirm ventilation, monitoring, gas delivery, alarm function, and system readiness are normal before returning the machine to service.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Review whether AC power, power cord condition, physical condition, environment, reboot results, and checkout results were verified. If the alarm returns or the system remains abnormal, treat the issue as an internal device fault and remove the machine from service.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported ACB HW WATCHDOG alarm during startup/checkout. Staff reported system malfunction and machine was removed from patient use.
CCR.Cause: ACB HW WATCHDOG alarm confirmed during startup. AC power source and power cord were checked. No obvious external damage or environmental issue found. Controlled reboot attempted, but alarm returned.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from service and labeled Out of Service. Sent for repair/bench evaluation. Anesthesia Control Board evaluation recommended due to persistent ACB HW WATCHDOG alarm.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: ACB MICROPROC ERROR
description: Troubleshooting guide for the GE Healthcare Aisys ACB MICROPROC ERROR alarm, a system malfunction related to an unexpected Anesthesia Control Board microcontroller exception that may affect safe anesthesia machine operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the alarm occurs during setup, checkout, standby, or clinical operation. Move the patient to another anesthesia machine or approved backup ventilation method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm is ACB MICROPROC ERROR. Note when it occurs and whether there are associated symptoms such as System Malfunction, failed startup, frozen controls, unexpected restart, or repeated alarm after cycling power.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Clinical Use Before Resetting
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is not supporting a patient before rebooting. Make sure anesthesia staff has another safe means of ventilation and anesthetic delivery available.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Reboot
steps.instructions: Power the Aisys down using normal shutdown methods when possible, wait briefly, and power it back on. Observe whether the ACB MICROPROC ERROR returns. If the alarm clears, complete required checkout before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Check External Power Stability
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is connected to a known-good AC outlet. Check that the power cord is fully seated and not damaged. Avoid questionable power strips or non-approved extension cords.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious External or Environmental Issues
steps.instructions: Check for liquid intrusion, physical damage, impact, burning smell, unusual heat, loose external modules, recent service activity, or reports of repeated shutdowns or resets.

steps.title: Repeat Startup Observation
steps.instructions: After confirming power and basic external conditions, restart the system again only if appropriate and safe. Note whether the alarm returns immediately, later in startup, consistently, or intermittently.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Fault Is Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the alarm returns after reboot with stable power and no external cause found, treat the issue as a likely internal Anesthesia Control Board fault and remove the machine from service.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed ACB MICROPROC ERROR with System Malfunction message during startup/system checkout and was not cleared for use.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed ACB MICROPROC ERROR. External power connection and basic external condition were checked. Alarm returned after controlled reboot, indicating likely internal Anesthesia Control Board microcontroller fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from service and labeled Out of Service. Reboot attempted and alarm persisted. Sent for repair/bench evaluation with suspected Anesthesia Control Board failure.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: ACB RAM ERROR
description: Troubleshooting ACB RAM ERROR on a GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine caused by a memory test failure that may create system malfunction, startup failure, failed checkout, or unreliable operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the alarm occurs during setup, checkout, startup, or clinical use. If the machine is connected to a patient, have anesthesia staff move ventilation and anesthesia support to another anesthesia machine or approved backup method. Do not continue using the machine if the system malfunction remains active.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error/Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed alarm is ACB RAM ERROR. Observe whether the alarm appears during power-up, machine startup, checkout, active use, after reboot, or intermittently. Look for associated symptoms such as system malfunction messages, failed startup, failed checkout, unexpected reset, loss of normal control response, or other ACB-related alarms.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Startup Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the anesthesia machine is connected to a known-good AC outlet. Confirm the power cord is fully seated at both the wall outlet and the machine connection. Check for loose power, damaged cord, tripped outlet, unstable outlet, or recent power interruption. Restart the unit on confirmed AC power only if safe and removed from patient use.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Reboot
steps.instructions: With the machine removed from patient use, reboot the system. Allow the machine to complete startup without interrupting power. Observe whether ACB RAM ERROR returns. If the alarm clears, complete the required checkout and verify normal operation before returning the machine to service.

steps.title: Check for Related Alarms or Abnormal Behavior
steps.instructions: Review the current alarm condition and any related ACB or system malfunction messages. Look for related issues such as ACB software error, ACB watchdog error, ACB unexpected reset, communication failures, or repeated failed startup behavior.

steps.title: Inspect External Condition
steps.instructions: Check the machine for obvious external issues such as liquid intrusion, physical damage, recent impact, loose external cables, loose external modules, overheating, odor, abnormal sounds, recent movement, recent cleaning, recent service, or a recent power event.

steps.title: Repeat Startup Only After Basic Checks
steps.instructions: After verifying power and external condition, restart the machine one more time if appropriate. Do not repeatedly cycle power if the alarm continues. Observe whether ACB RAM ERROR returns during startup or operation. If the machine starts normally, run the required checkout and return it to service only after successful testing.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed ACB RAM ERROR during startup and could not be trusted for patient use.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed ACB RAM ERROR after controlled reboot. AC power and external condition checked with no external cause found. Persistent alarm indicates likely internal Anesthesia Control Board memory failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed machine from service and labeled Out of Service. Reboot attempted per troubleshooting guidance. Alarm persisted, so unit was escalated for bench evaluation and ACB replacement as needed.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: ACB SW ERROR
description: Troubleshooting guide for ACB SW ERROR on the GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine, including patient safety steps, reboot, power checks, external inspection, software reload escalation, and CCR documentation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Aisys from patient use if the alarm appears during setup, startup, checkout, or clinical operation. If connected to a patient, have anesthesia staff transition to a safe backup ventilation and anesthesia plan.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is ACB SW ERROR. Observe when the alarm occurs and check for system malfunction messages, failed startup, failed checkout, freezing, rebooting, or ventilation-related symptoms.

steps.title: Power Down and Reboot the System
steps.instructions: Remove the machine from clinical use, power it down normally when possible, allow the system to fully shut down, restart the Aisys, and observe whether the alarm returns.

steps.title: Check AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the power cord is seated correctly and check for damaged plugs, loose connections, or signs of power interruption.

steps.title: Check Battery and Power Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is not unexpectedly running on battery. Check for battery-related alarms or low battery messages, allow the battery to charge on AC power if needed, and restart after stable power is confirmed.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections and Modules
steps.instructions: Check externally accessible cables, modules, and connectors for loose connections, damage, liquid contamination, impact damage, recently changed modules, or poorly seated accessories.

steps.title: Review Recent Service or Software Activity
steps.instructions: Ask whether the machine recently had software updates, service work, board replacement, a power event, failed checkout, unexpected shutdown, network changes, or configuration changes.

steps.title: Repeat Startup and Checkout
steps.instructions: After power, battery, and external checks are complete, restart the machine and run the appropriate anesthesia machine checkout. Watch for the alarm returning, system malfunction messages, freezing, restart behavior, or checkout failure.

steps.title: Reload ACB Software if the Alarm Persists
steps.instructions: If ACB SW ERROR returns after reboot and external checks, perform or arrange for ACB software reload according to approved service process and facility policy. Repeat startup and checkout afterward.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Fault Is Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the alarm returns after reboot and ACB software reload, treat the issue as a likely device-level ACB failure and remove the unit from service for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported ACB SW ERROR. Staff reported system malfunction during startup or checkout. Unit removed from patient use for evaluation.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed ACB SW ERROR on restart. External power, battery status, and visible external connections were checked. Alarm persisted after reboot, indicating likely ACB software or control board fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service and labeled Out of Service. Reboot performed and external checks completed. ACB software reload required. If alarm persists after reload, ACB replacement or repair evaluation required before return to service.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: ACB SW WATCHDOG
description: Troubleshooting guide for the ACB SW WATCHDOG alarm on a GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine, including patient safety, reboot verification, external checks, checkout, escalation, and CCR work order documentation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the ACB SW WATCHDOG alarm appears during setup, checkout, or active use. Do not rely on the machine for ventilation or anesthesia delivery while the alarm is unresolved. Provide another anesthesia machine or approved backup ventilation method if the room is in use.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is ACB SW WATCHDOG. Note whether it occurs during power-up, system checkout, after reboot, standby, or clinical use. Check for system malfunction messages, unexpected rebooting, failed startup, frozen display, loss of control response, or ventilation interruption.

steps.title: Remove the Device from Clinical Use Before Rebooting
steps.instructions: Coordinate with clinical staff before powering down or rebooting if the machine is connected to a patient or prepared for a case. Use another anesthesia machine if patient care is active or imminent.

steps.title: Reboot the System
steps.instructions: Power the Aisys system down using the normal shutdown process when possible. Wait briefly, power the system back on, and observe the full startup sequence. Confirm whether the alarm clears or returns.

steps.title: Repeat Startup Observation
steps.instructions: Allow the machine to complete startup and remain powered on long enough to confirm stable operation. Watch for alarm recurrence, unexpected reset, startup delay, system malfunction message, frozen display, or unresponsive controls.

steps.title: Check Basic External Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify stable AC power, confirm the power cord is fully seated, check for loose external modules, physical damage, liquid intrusion, impact damage, or external accessories that may affect startup behavior.

steps.title: Perform a System Checkout if the Alarm Clears
steps.instructions: If the reboot clears the alarm, perform the appropriate anesthesia machine checkout according to facility procedure. Confirm no active alarms, normal startup, normal display response, ventilator readiness, gas delivery readiness, and successful checkout results.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after reboot and the machine passes checkout, document the event and return the machine to service only after normal operation is confirmed. Monitor for repeat reports.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported ACB SW WATCHDOG alarm with system malfunction during startup. Staff reported machine was not reliable for use.
CCR.Cause: ACB SW WATCHDOG alarm confirmed. AC power and external connections checked. System rebooted and alarm cleared. Machine completed startup and checkout without recurrence.
CCR.Resolution: Removed machine from patient use during troubleshooting. Rebooted system, verified alarm did not return, completed system checkout, and returned unit to service.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: ACB UNEXPECTED RESET
description: Troubleshooting guide for the ACB UNEXPECTED RESET alarm on a GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine, including patient safety, power checks, reboot, checkout, escalation, and CCR documentation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the anesthesia machine is connected to an active patient. If the alarm occurs during clinical use, notify anesthesia staff immediately and move the patient to backup equipment if needed. Do not continue using the machine for patient care with an unresolved control board reset alarm.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is ACB UNEXPECTED RESET. Check whether it appears during startup, checkout, active use, after power interruption, or intermittently. Observe for system malfunction messages, unexpected reboot, screen issues, ventilator interruption, or repeated alarm after restart.

steps.title: Check AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is connected to a known-good hospital-grade AC outlet. Confirm the power cord is fully seated at the wall and at the machine. Check for recent power loss, unplugging, or battery operation.

steps.title: Check Power Cord and External Power Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord for loose connection, damaged insulation, bent plug blades, overheating, or intermittent connection when moved. Do not use the machine if cord or plug damage is present.

steps.title: Check for Recent Events Before the Alarm
steps.instructions: Ask staff what happened before the alarm appeared. Look for power outage, machine movement, accidental unplugging, startup after storage, alarm during checkout, accessory/module connection, or repeated resets with no user action.

steps.title: Reboot the System
steps.instructions: Power the Aisys system down using the proper shutdown process. Wait briefly, restart the system, allow startup to complete, and observe whether the ACB UNEXPECTED RESET alarm returns.

steps.title: Run or Repeat System Checkout
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after reboot, perform the required anesthesia machine checkout according to facility procedure. Watch for alarm recurrence, system malfunction messages, ventilator issues, failed checkout steps, or unexpected restart.

steps.title: Check Whether the Alarm Is Repeatable
steps.instructions: If the alarm returns, document exactly when it happens, such as immediately at startup, during checkout, when ventilation begins, after several minutes powered on, when moved or touched, or randomly with no clear trigger.

steps.title: Isolate External Causes Before Calling the ACB Failed
steps.instructions: Confirm AC power is stable, the power cord is secure, no obvious external damage is present, the alarm returns after reboot, the alarm is not tied to a known power interruption, and the system cannot reliably complete checkout if persistent.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after reboot, AC power is stable, and checkout passes, return the machine to service according to facility policy. Document the alarm, reboot, checkout result, and final status.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported ACB UNEXPECTED RESET alarm. Staff reported system malfunction and unexpected reset during startup or checkout.
CCR.Cause: Alarm confirmed. External AC power and power cord checked. System reboot performed. Alarm cleared after reboot, or alarm returned after external power issues were ruled out.
CCR.Resolution: Rebooted system, verified AC power connection, and repeated system checkout. If checkout passed and alarm did not return, device was returned to service. If alarm returned, device was removed from service and sent for repair or ACB evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: ACMains POWER FAIL
description: Troubleshooting guide for ACMains POWER FAIL on the GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine when the unit is running on battery due to lost, unstable, or undetected AC mains power.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the anesthesia machine is connected to an active patient. If the unit is in use, notify anesthesia staff immediately that the machine is operating on battery power. Verify alternate ventilation and monitoring options are available if power cannot be restored quickly.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is ACMains POWER FAIL. Check for displayed text such as Plug in power cable. On battery. Observe when the alarm occurs and check whether the unit is operating on battery.

steps.title: Confirm the Power Cord Is Plugged In
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord at the anesthesia machine and wall outlet. Verify the cord is fully seated at both ends and check for loose connection, damaged plug, damaged strain relief, or a cord pulled tight.

steps.title: Restore AC Mains Power
steps.instructions: Plug the machine into a known working hospital-grade outlet. If already plugged in, reseat the power cord if safe and appropriate. Confirm the machine returns to normal AC power operation.

steps.title: Check the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Test the outlet using an approved outlet tester or facility-approved process. Check whether other devices on the same circuit are without power and try a different known-good outlet if available.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord for cuts, crushing, exposed conductors, bent plug blades, burn marks, loose plug fit, or damaged hospital-grade plug. Observe whether cord movement causes AC power dropouts.

steps.title: Check for Facility Power or Circuit Issues
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether there was a recent power outage, generator transfer, breaker trip, or OR power issue. Determine whether the issue follows the machine or stays with the room, outlet, or circuit.

steps.title: Monitor Battery Status
steps.instructions: After AC power is restored, monitor the battery indicator and any related battery alarms. Confirm the system is no longer discharging on battery and allow the unit to recharge if needed.

steps.title: Check for Repeat or Intermittent Alarm Behavior
steps.instructions: Leave the unit plugged into a known-good outlet and observe for recurrence. Move the cord slightly at the plug and machine connection while watching for AC power dropouts.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If known-good AC power restores normal operation and the alarm clears, confirm no active battery alarms remain and return the device to service only after normal operational checks and facility policy requirements are met.

CCR.Complaint: GE Aisys anesthesia machine displayed ACMains POWER FAIL alarm with message Plug in power cable. On battery. Staff reported unit was operating on battery power.
CCR.Cause: AC mains power was not detected by the machine. Power cord, outlet, or facility power issue was found and corrected, or internal AC power detection issue was suspected after external checks were completed.
CCR.Resolution: Verified alarm condition, checked power cord connection, tested known-good AC outlet, restored AC mains power, monitored battery status, and confirmed alarm cleared. Unit was returned to service or removed for repair if the alarm persisted.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: BATTERY LOW
description: Troubleshooting BATTERY LOW alarm conditions on the GE Healthcare Aisys when the anesthesia machine is operating on battery power with limited backup time remaining.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the anesthesia machine is connected to an active patient. If the machine is in clinical use, notify anesthesia staff immediately, restore AC power if safe, and ensure an alternate ventilation and anesthesia plan is available if power cannot be restored.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is BATTERY LOW. Check whether the screen indicates the system is on battery power and instructs the user to plug in the power cable. Ask staff when the alarm appeared and whether the machine had been unplugged or moved.

steps.title: Check the AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the anesthesia machine power cord is plugged into a known working hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the outlet has power and check whether the machine recognizes AC mains power after being plugged in.

steps.title: Check the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord for loose connections, bent plug blades, cuts, crushing, exposed conductors, strain relief damage, or signs of heat. Confirm the cord is fully seated at the wall outlet and at the machine.

steps.title: Confirm the Machine Charges While Plugged In
steps.instructions: Leave the Aisys plugged into confirmed AC power, allow the battery to charge, and monitor whether the BATTERY LOW alarm clears after sufficient charging time.

steps.title: Check for Repeated or Rapid Battery Drain
steps.instructions: Ask staff how long the machine had been unplugged and whether the machine had recently been stored unplugged. Check for repeated BATTERY LOW alarms after short unplugged periods.

steps.title: Check for Other Battery or Power Alarms
steps.instructions: Look for additional battery or power-related messages such as No battery backup, Battery charge failure, Battery missing, Battery fail, or AC power loss.

steps.title: Isolate External Power vs Battery Issue
steps.instructions: After confirming the machine is plugged into a working outlet, observe whether it still behaves as though it is on battery. If AC power is recognized but the battery does not recover, suspect weak battery condition or charging issue.

steps.title: Perform Bench Evaluation if the Alarm Persists
steps.instructions: Remove the machine from patient care if the BATTERY LOW alarm does not clear after confirmed charging on AC power. Evaluate AC input behavior and battery charge status on the bench.

steps.title: Replace Battery if Needed
steps.instructions: If the battery does not hold charge, charges abnormally, or repeatedly triggers BATTERY LOW after proper charging, replace the battery according to approved service procedure and retest.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after restoring AC power or charging the battery, confirm the machine recognizes AC power, confirm no battery-related alarms remain active, and verify the machine passes required checkout before clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported BATTERY LOW alarm. Display indicated the unit was on battery power and instructed staff to plug in the power cable.
CCR.Cause: Machine was operating on battery power due to AC power loss or insufficient battery charge. External AC power source, power cord connection, and battery charge condition were checked. Persistent alarm after charging may indicate weak battery or battery charge circuit issue.
CCR.Resolution: Verified machine was removed from patient use for evaluation. Checked wall outlet, power cord, and AC power connection. Left machine plugged into confirmed AC power to charge battery. Monitored battery alarm status. If alarm cleared and checkout passed, returned device to service. If alarm persisted, labeled device Out of Service for battery replacement and battery charge circuit evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: BATTERY EMPTY
description: Troubleshooting guide for a GE Healthcare Aisys BATTERY EMPTY alarm indicating critically low battery power and possible shutdown in less than 5 minutes.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the BATTERY EMPTY alarm cannot be corrected immediately. If in active use, notify anesthesia staff and ensure the patient can be safely ventilated using another anesthesia machine, manual ventilation, or another approved backup method.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is BATTERY EMPTY and check for the message System shutdown in <5 min. Note when the alarm occurs and whether symptoms include low battery indication, loss of backup power, charging failure, or shutdown risk.

steps.title: Confirm AC Power Is Connected
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is plugged into a working hospital-grade outlet and that the power cord is fully seated at both the wall outlet and the machine.

steps.title: Test the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Test the outlet with a known-working device or outlet tester if allowed by facility procedure. Try a different verified outlet if the original outlet is questionable.

steps.title: Check the Power Cord Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord for damage, loose connections, bent pins, overheating, or intermittent power when moved. Swap with an approved known-good cord if available.

steps.title: Leave the System Plugged In to Charge
steps.instructions: Keep the machine connected to verified AC power and allow the battery time to charge. Monitor whether the BATTERY EMPTY alarm clears or changes to a less critical battery condition.

steps.title: Check Recent Use or Storage History
steps.instructions: Ask whether the machine was stored unplugged, moved between rooms, left off charge overnight, used during a power interruption, or previously had battery alarms.

steps.title: Inspect External Battery-Related Conditions
steps.instructions: Check for external signs of battery or power system issues, including loose battery compartment, fluid intrusion, burning smell, heat near the power entry area, or repeated alarms after charging.

steps.title: Recheck After Charging
steps.instructions: After charging on verified AC power, confirm whether the alarm clears, battery charge level increases, machine remains powered normally on AC, and backup status is restored.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Power Check When Safe
steps.instructions: When the machine is not in patient use and has charged sufficiently, verify normal AC power operation and, if allowed by department procedure, briefly confirm battery backup behavior without allowing the unit to reach critical shutdown.

steps.title: Check the Battery Charge Circuit if the Alarm Persists
steps.instructions: If the alarm remains after verified AC power and adequate charging, evaluate battery charging behavior using approved service procedures and Service Software if authorized.

steps.title: Replace the Battery if Needed
steps.instructions: If the battery does not recover, does not hold charge, or fails battery-related checks, replace the battery according to facility procedure and approved parts requirements. Charge and retest before returning the machine to service.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after charging, AC power is verified, and required checks pass, return the device to service. If the alarm returns or backup power cannot be trusted, remove the device from service.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed BATTERY EMPTY alarm with message indicating system shutdown in <5 min.
CCR.Cause: Machine battery was critically depleted. AC outlet and power cord connection were verified. Battery required charging. If alarm persists after charging, possible failed battery or battery charge circuit issue.
CCR.Resolution: Connected machine to verified AC power, allowed battery to charge, rechecked alarm status, and confirmed battery indication improved. Returned machine to service if checks passed, or removed from service for battery and charge circuit evaluation if alarm persisted.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: BATTERY FAIL
description: Troubleshooting guide for the GE Healthcare Aisys BATTERY FAIL alarm when the anesthesia machine reports no battery backup due to low voltage, failed charging, battery failure, or a charging circuit issue.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the BATTERY FAIL alarm on an active patient unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. Remove the machine from patient use if battery backup is required, if checkout cannot be completed, or if No Battery Backup is displayed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is BATTERY FAIL and check for No Battery Backup. Note whether the alarm appears during startup, checkout, after being unplugged, after storage, or during normal operation.

steps.title: Check AC Power Source First
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is plugged into a known-good hospital-grade AC outlet. Check that the power cord is fully seated and inspect for damage, looseness, overheating, or power source issues.

steps.title: Leave the System Plugged In to Charge
steps.instructions: Allow the machine to remain connected to AC power long enough for the battery to charge, especially if the unit was unplugged or stored. Restart the system and check whether the alarm clears.

steps.title: Check Recent Power or Storage History
steps.instructions: Ask whether the machine was recently unplugged, stored without AC power, moved between rooms, affected by a power outage, or recently had the battery replaced.

steps.title: Inspect External Battery-Related Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible external areas for obvious damage, loose covers, fluid intrusion, burning smell, heat, discoloration, or signs of unsafe conditions. Remove the unit from service if unsafe conditions are found.

steps.title: Repeat Startup or Checkout After Charging
steps.instructions: After charging, restart the system and repeat the appropriate checkout. Confirm the BATTERY FAIL alarm and No Battery Backup message clear and that no related battery alarms return.

steps.title: Review Battery and Charging Information Through Approved Service Procedures
steps.instructions: If the alarm remains, use approved Service Software according to facility policy to review battery charge circuit status, voltage, current, and charge behavior.

steps.title: Replace the Battery if Indicated
steps.instructions: If the battery does not recover after charging or service checks indicate failed battery behavior, replace the battery using the correct approved replacement part and facility procedure. Charge, restart, and repeat checkout.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after charging or battery replacement, document the corrective action, complete checkout, verify safe operation, and return the anesthesia machine to service.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported BATTERY FAIL alarm with No Battery Backup message during startup or checkout.
CCR.Cause: BATTERY FAIL alarm confirmed. AC power source and power cord were checked. Battery was allowed to charge, but alarm persisted, indicating failed battery or possible charging circuit issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from patient use. Connected to known-good AC power, allowed battery to charge, restarted system, reviewed battery charge status as needed, and replaced battery if indicated. Unit passed checkout and was returned to service, or remained Out of Service for further repair.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: BATTERY CHARGE FAIL
description: Troubleshooting guide for a GE Healthcare Aisys BATTERY CHARGE FAIL alarm with possible No battery backup message caused by abnormal battery charging, excessive charge current, battery failure, battery connection issues, or battery charge circuit faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the alarm is active, repeated, or cannot be cleared. If the unit is in use, notify anesthesia staff and move to another anesthesia machine or approved backup ventilation method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm is BATTERY CHARGE FAIL and check whether No battery backup is also displayed. Note when the alarm appears, such as startup, standby, AC power connection, after charging, or after battery replacement.

steps.title: Confirm AC Power Is Present and Stable
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is connected to a known-good hospital-grade AC outlet. Check that the power cord is fully seated and inspect it for damage, looseness, overheating, or outlet-related issues.

steps.title: Check External Power Indicators
steps.instructions: Observe AC power and battery indicators. Confirm the machine recognizes AC power and look for abnormal battery icon behavior, unavailable battery status, immediate alarm return after restart, or continued No battery backup messages.

steps.title: Inspect the Battery Area Externally
steps.instructions: With the machine removed from patient use, inspect the battery area externally for a loose cover, recent battery replacement, damage, fluid intrusion, corrosion, burning smell, or heat. Do not perform deep internal disassembly during basic troubleshooting.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Machine Only if Safe
steps.instructions: If the machine is not connected to a patient, perform a controlled restart and allow startup checks to complete. Observe whether BATTERY CHARGE FAIL returns during startup, standby, or powered-on operation.

steps.title: Allow Charging Time if Recently Depleted or Replaced
steps.instructions: If the battery was recently depleted, replaced, or stored unplugged, leave the machine connected to AC power for an appropriate charging period and recheck the alarm. Do not ignore the alarm if it persists.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge Information in Service Software
steps.instructions: If qualified and authorized, access Service Software and review battery charge circuit information, including abnormal battery charge current or charge circuit status. Compare the readings with the reported alarm condition.

steps.title: Isolate Battery-Related Failure
steps.instructions: If charge circuit values are abnormal, suspect battery failure, battery connection issue, or battery charge circuit fault. Stop repeated charging attempts if heat, odor, corrosion, leaking, swelling, or visible damage is present.

steps.title: Replace Battery if Indicated
steps.instructions: If Service Software findings and inspection support battery failure, replace the battery according to approved service procedures. Reconnect AC power, allow charging, restart the machine, and confirm the alarm and No battery backup message clear.

steps.title: Return to Service Only After Verification
steps.instructions: Confirm BATTERY CHARGE FAIL is no longer present, No battery backup is cleared, AC power is recognized, battery status appears normal, system checkout passes, and no related battery or power alarms return.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed BATTERY CHARGE FAIL with No battery backup message while connected to AC power.
CCR.Cause: Verified AC power and power cord connection. Alarm returned after restart. Battery charge information required review in Service Software and findings indicated suspected failed battery or battery charge circuit fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service. Checked battery charge status in Service Software. Battery replaced if indicated and system retested. If alarm persisted, unit remained Out of Service for further bench evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: BATTERY MISSING
description: Troubleshooting BATTERY MISSING alarm on a GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine when the system reports no battery backup due to a missing, disconnected, discharged, failed battery, or battery charge circuit issue.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the BATTERY MISSING alarm appears during setup, checkout, or before a case. If active patient use is involved, notify anesthesia staff and make sure alternate ventilation, oxygen delivery, and monitoring are available.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the system on and confirm the exact alarm is BATTERY MISSING. Confirm whether the displayed message states No battery backup and observe whether the alarm appears during POST, checkout, after power cycling, or continuously while connected to AC power.

steps.title: Confirm AC Power Is Stable
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is plugged into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Check the power cord, plug, strain relief, and machine power connection. Confirm the outlet is powered and the machine recognizes AC power.

steps.title: Confirm the Battery Is Installed and Connected
steps.instructions: Verify the battery is physically present and connected according to facility-approved service access procedures. Check for loose leads, disconnected plugs, damaged connectors, corrosion, or swollen battery condition.

steps.title: Leave the System Plugged In to Charge
steps.instructions: Connect the machine to stable AC power and allow time for charging. After charging, power cycle the machine and check whether the BATTERY MISSING alarm clears.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge Circuit Status
steps.instructions: If the alarm continues, use appropriate service diagnostics per facility policy to review battery voltage, charge status, and battery charge circuit indicators if available.

steps.title: Replace the Battery if Indicated
steps.instructions: Replace the battery if it is connected but will not charge, reads abnormally, fails diagnostics, or continues to trigger the alarm. After replacement, charge as needed, power cycle, verify the alarm clears, and complete required checkout.

steps.title: Escalate if the Alarm Persists
steps.instructions: If the alarm remains after AC power, battery installation, connection, charge time, and battery replacement have been checked, remove the machine from service and send it for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed BATTERY MISSING alarm during startup with No battery backup message.
CCR.Cause: Battery backup was not detected during startup. AC power, battery connection, charge condition, and battery charge circuit were checked.
CCR.Resolution: Connected battery and left unit plugged into AC power to charge. Rechecked startup condition. Replaced battery if indicated, completed required checkout, and verified whether BATTERY MISSING alarm cleared. Unit removed from service if alarm persisted.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: BATTERY REVERSED CONNECTIONS
description: Troubleshooting guide for the GE Healthcare Aisys BATTERY REVERSED CONNECTIONS alarm when the anesthesia machine reports no battery backup due to reversed polarity, incorrect battery connection, or battery voltage detection problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if battery backup cannot be confirmed. If the machine is in use, notify clinical staff and move patient care to a safe anesthesia setup according to facility procedure. Do not rely on the machine during a power interruption while this alarm is active.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is BATTERY REVERSED CONNECTIONS. Check whether the screen also displays No battery backup. Observe whether the alarm appears at startup, after battery replacement, after service, after transport, or intermittently during use.

steps.title: Connect to Known Good AC Power
steps.instructions: Verify the anesthesia machine is plugged into a working hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the power cord is fully seated and the unit powers normally from AC mains power while the battery backup issue is evaluated.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Service Before Battery Inspection
steps.instructions: Power the machine down according to facility procedure before inspecting battery connections. Label the device unavailable for patient use while battery backup is suspect and use appropriate precautions when handling battery wiring.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Connections
steps.instructions: Check the battery terminals and wiring connections for reversed positive and negative leads, loose terminals, disconnected leads, damaged wiring, corrosion, incorrect battery type, or recent installation error. Correct obvious issues only if within facility Clinical Engineering scope and procedure.

steps.title: Check for Recent Service or Battery Replacement
steps.instructions: Review whether the alarm started after battery replacement, power supply service, internal battery inspection, transport, relocation, or a previous repair attempt. Review recent work order notes if available.

steps.title: Reconnect and Restart the System
steps.instructions: After confirming the battery connections are correct and secure, reconnect power and restart the Aisys. Observe startup behavior and confirm whether the BATTERY REVERSED CONNECTIONS alarm clears.

steps.title: Confirm Battery Backup Status
steps.instructions: Verify the machine no longer reports no battery backup. Allow the unit to remain powered and observe for repeat alarms. Follow facility procedure for checking battery backup readiness before returning the unit to service.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed BATTERY REVERSED CONNECTIONS alarm with No battery backup message.
CCR.Cause: Battery backup alarm confirmed. AC power verified. Battery leads inspected for reversed polarity, loose terminals, damaged wiring, corrosion, and recent service-related connection error.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, inspected and secured battery connections, restarted system, confirmed BATTERY REVERSED CONNECTIONS alarm cleared, and verified battery backup status before return to service.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: COM ERROR VENT TO ACB
description: Troubleshooting guide for the COM ERROR VENT TO ACB alarm on a GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine, indicating lost communication between the ventilator side of the system and the Anesthesia Controller Board.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the alarm is active, recurring, or associated with ventilation failure. Support the patient with another anesthesia machine, manual ventilation, or an appropriate backup device before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is COM ERROR VENT TO ACB. Check whether System Malfunction is also displayed and document when the alarm occurs, such as during startup, POST, ventilation start, intermittent use, or after reboot.

steps.title: Understand What the Alarm Means
steps.instructions: Treat the alarm as a communication fault between the ventilator side of the system and the Anesthesia Controller Board. Focus troubleshooting on communication, power, cabling, VIB, ACB, and related harnessing after basic checks are complete.

steps.title: Reboot the System
steps.instructions: Remove the machine from clinical use, power it down normally, wait for full shutdown, restart it, and observe whether the alarm returns. If the alarm clears, complete required checkout and verify ventilator operation before returning to service.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Startup Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is connected to a known-good AC outlet. Verify the power cord is secure and check for signs of unstable power, loose plug connection, damaged cord, or failed outlet.

steps.title: Check for Obvious External or Physical Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect the machine for damage, fluid intrusion, impact, loose panels, or recent movement. Ask staff whether the alarm started after moving, cleaning, service, impact, or a power event.

steps.title: Review Alarm Timing and Event History
steps.instructions: Review the alarm history and note whether the alarm appears once, repeatedly, or after every startup. Look for related ventilator, SIB, VIB, ACB, communication, or system malfunction errors.

steps.title: Check Cabling and Connections
steps.instructions: With the machine removed from service and following facility procedures, inspect accessible cabling and connectors related to ventilator communication. Look for loose connectors, damaged harnesses, bent pins, poor seating, fluid contamination, or service disturbance.
