steps.title: Isolate Whether the Issue Appears Cable-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: After reboot and cabling checks, determine whether the alarm clears or returns. If it clears and does not return, the issue may have been temporary or connection-related. If it returns, suspect internal communication hardware.

steps.title: Escalate to Ventilator Interface Board Evaluation
steps.instructions: If the alarm continues after reboot and cabling checks, remove the machine from service for repair evaluation. Evaluate the Ventilator Interface Board communication path according to facility and manufacturer service procedures.

steps.title: Escalate to Anesthesia Controller Board Evaluation if Needed
steps.instructions: If VIB evaluation or replacement does not resolve the issue, escalate to Anesthesia Controller Board evaluation. Complete checkout and ventilator functional testing before returning the machine to service.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed COM ERROR VENT TO ACB with System Malfunction alarm. Staff reported ventilator communication fault during startup or use and machine was removed from service.
CCR.Cause: Verified COM ERROR VENT TO ACB alarm. Rebooted system and checked power source, external condition, alarm history, and accessible cabling. Alarm indicates loss of communication between ventilator side of system and Anesthesia Controller Board after communication had been established.
CCR.Resolution: Removed machine from patient use. Rebooted system, inspected power and cabling, and reviewed alarm behavior. If alarm cleared, completed checkout and verified ventilator operation before returning to service. If alarm persisted, labeled Out of Service and escalated for bench repair with VIB and ACB evaluation as needed.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: VENT SIB COMMUNICATION FAILURE
description: Troubleshooting guide for VENT SIB COMMUNICATION FAILURE on the GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine, indicating ventilator communication loss between the Anesthesia Controller Board and Ventilator Interface Board.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from active patient use if the alarm appears during a case or if ventilation is affected. Have anesthesia staff manually ventilate or move the patient to another anesthesia machine before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm is VENT SIB COMMUNICATION FAILURE. Note whether it occurs at startup, during checkout, after ventilation starts, intermittently, or after movement or service.

steps.title: Remove the Machine from Clinical Use
steps.instructions: If the alarm is active or repeatable, label the anesthesia machine unavailable for patient use and provide a backup anesthesia machine if needed.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Power Cycle
steps.instructions: With the machine removed from patient use, power the unit down normally, restart it, and observe whether the alarm returns. If it clears, run checkout and verify ventilation before return to service.

steps.title: Check External Power and Basic Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify connection to a known-good hospital-grade outlet, inspect the power cord, and check for unstable power, recent movement, impact, fluid intrusion, or physical damage.

steps.title: Check Loose or Damaged External Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible external cables and connectors for damage, stress, pinching, or disconnection. Check recently moved or serviced areas for disturbed connections.

steps.title: Run the Machine Checkout
steps.instructions: Run the anesthesia machine checkout if the unit powers up safely. Record whether the alarm occurs during checkout or ventilation testing and whether checkout passes or fails.

steps.title: Escalate for Bench Evaluation
steps.instructions: If the alarm returns, remove the machine from service for qualified service evaluation. Likely fault areas include internal cabling, Pan Connector Board, Ventilator Interface Board, and Anesthesia Controller Board.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed VENT SIB COMMUNICATION FAILURE. Ventilator communication reliability could not be confirmed.
CCR.Cause: Alarm verified during testing. External power and accessible connections checked. Alarm persisted, indicating likely internal communication fault involving internal cabling, Pan Connector Board, Ventilator Interface Board, or Anesthesia Controller Board.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from service and labeled Out of Service. Referred for bench evaluation and service-level troubleshooting. Machine not returned to service pending repair and successful checkout.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: VENT AIRWAY OVERPRESS SIGNAL FAIL
description: Troubleshooting guide for a GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reporting VENT AIRWAY OVERPRESS SIGNAL FAIL, which may cause Vent Fail, Monitoring Only operation, and loss of normal ventilator function.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not rely on the internal ventilator if the machine is displaying Vent Fail, Monitoring Only, or any active ventilator failure condition. Remove the anesthesia machine from active patient use if ventilation cannot be trusted. Have anesthesia staff ventilate the patient manually or transfer ventilation to a backup anesthesia machine or approved alternate device as clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm reported is VENT AIRWAY OVERPRESS SIGNAL FAIL. Confirm whether the screen also displays Vent Fail. Monitoring Only. Ask when the alarm occurred and record whether the ventilator stopped normal function or remained in monitoring-only operation.

steps.title: Understand What the Alarm Means
steps.instructions: Interpret this as a ventilator pressure signal disagreement rather than a simple high-pressure circuit event. The system may be detecting a high airway overpressure signal while measured airway pressure and manifold pressure are below the expected overpressure range.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Circuit and Breathing System
steps.instructions: Check for kinked breathing hoses, blocked inspiratory or expiratory limbs, blocked filter or HME, obstructed patient connection, incorrect breathing circuit assembly, condensation, damaged tubing, or pinched hoses.

steps.title: Check Airway Pressure Line and Sampling Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect externally accessible airway pressure sensing tubing and related connections. Look for loose pressure tubing, disconnected sensing lines, cracked tubing, blocked ports, or fluid contamination.

steps.title: Check for Recent Setup or Accessory Changes
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the alarm started after a circuit change, filter change, breathing system cleaning, reassembly, service, transport, or power interruption. Inspect any recently changed components.

steps.title: Reboot the System Only When Safe
steps.instructions: Only reboot after the patient is no longer dependent on the machine and clinical staff confirm it is safe. Power the Aisys down and restart it. Allow the system to complete startup checks and observe whether the alarm returns.

steps.title: Perform Checkout if the Alarm Clears
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after reboot, perform the normal anesthesia machine checkout required by your facility. Verify ventilator operation, leak test results, pressure readings, bag/vent switch operation, and alarm function.

steps.title: Test for Repeat Failure
steps.instructions: Run the machine long enough to confirm the alarm does not return. Use an approved test lung or facility-approved test method. Watch for repeat VENT AIRWAY OVERPRESS SIGNAL FAIL, Vent Fail messages, Monitoring Only status, abnormal pressure readings, or failure during ventilation mode changes.

steps.title: Escalate to Service Mode Evaluation if the Alarm Repeats
steps.instructions: If the alarm repeats after external checks and reboot, remove the unit from service. Request bench evaluation by qualified Clinical Engineering or authorized service personnel. The listed follow-up action is to check the airway pressure signal in Service Mode.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears, the machine passes checkout, and functional ventilation testing passes, return the unit to service according to facility policy. If the alarm repeats or ventilator function remains questionable, keep the unit out of service.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported VENT AIRWAY OVERPRESS SIGNAL FAIL with Vent Fail. Monitoring Only displayed. Staff reported ventilator fault and machine could not be trusted for normal ventilation.
CCR.Cause: External breathing circuit, filter, hose, and pressure-related connections inspected. Alarm indicates high airway overpressure signal disagreement while measured pressures were not consistent with a true overpressure condition.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from patient use. Verified alarm condition, inspected external circuit and pressure connections, rebooted system, and completed checkout and functional ventilation testing. Unit returned to service after passing testing, or labeled Out of Service for bench evaluation if alarm repeated.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: VENT SUSTAINED PAW SDOWN
description: Troubleshooting guide for VENT SUSTAINED PAW SDOWN on a GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine, including high airway pressure shutdown, monitoring-only operation, external circuit checks, functional testing, and escalation guidance.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from active patient use if ventilation is not reliable. Have anesthesia staff manually ventilate the patient or move the patient to another functional anesthesia machine or ventilator as clinically appropriate before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm is VENT SUSTAINED PAW SDOWN and check whether Vent Fail. Monitoring Only. appeared. Determine when the alarm occurred and whether airway pressure was reported above 100 cmH2O for an extended period.

steps.title: Check for Obvious External High-Pressure Causes
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing circuit, hoses, filters, airway adapter, expiratory path, and manual ventilation setup for restriction, blockage, or incorrect setup.

steps.title: Check the Breathing System Setup
steps.instructions: Verify inspiratory and expiratory limb connections, absorber canister seating, flow sensor seating, bag arm connection, breathing bag setup, scavenging connection, and recently replaced circuit components.

steps.title: Review Ventilation Settings and Alarm Context
steps.instructions: Review pressure settings, PEEP, inspiratory pressure, tidal volume, flow settings, mode selection, and patient size configuration for settings that could contribute to excessive airway pressure.

steps.title: Power Cycle the System After Patient Is Safe
steps.instructions: After the machine is removed from patient use, reboot the anesthesia system and observe whether the alarm returns during startup, self-test, or checkout.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Check
steps.instructions: Run the appropriate machine checkout and ventilation test using a test lung or approved setup. Confirm normal ventilator cycling, reasonable Paw readings, and no repeated Vent Fail or Monitoring Only messages.

steps.title: Inspect Airway Pressure Reading Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe Paw readings during testing for stuck high readings, unstable readings, unexpected jumps, failure to return to baseline, or readings inconsistent with test lung behavior.

steps.title: Isolate External Setup Issue vs Device Fault
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting an external issue, document the cause. If the alarm repeats with a known-good circuit or Paw remains abnormal, remove the device from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Return to Service Only If Fully Resolved
steps.instructions: Return the unit to service only if the alarm does not return, checkout passes, test lung ventilation passes, Paw readings behave normally, and no unresolved Vent Fail or Monitoring Only condition remains.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported VENT SUSTAINED PAW SDOWN with Vent Fail. Monitoring Only. during use. Staff reported high airway pressure alarm and ventilator shutdown.
CCR.Cause: Found breathing circuit restriction from kinked hose causing excessive airway pressure. No internal device fault found during checkout.
CCR.Resolution: Removed kinked circuit, installed known-good circuit, rebooted system, completed anesthesia machine checkout and test lung ventilation. Paw readings normal. Alarm did not return. Returned unit to service.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: VENT FLOW VALVE FAIL CURRENT
description: Troubleshooting guide for VENT FLOW VALVE FAIL CURRENT on a GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine, including Vent Fail. Monitoring Only behavior, loss of automatic ventilation capability, external checks, service software evaluation, removal from service, and CCR documentation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not rely on the anesthesia machine for automatic ventilation if Vent Fail. Monitoring Only is displayed. Remove the machine from active patient use if automatic ventilation is not available or reliable. Have anesthesia staff manually ventilate the patient or move the patient to another anesthesia machine or ventilator as clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed message is Vent Fail. Monitoring Only and the associated alarm condition is VENT FLOW VALVE FAIL CURRENT. Determine whether the alarm occurred during startup, checkout, switching into ventilation, active ventilation, or intermittent use.

steps.title: Check Power and Basic System Condition
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is connected to a proper AC power source. Check the power cord at the wall and machine. Confirm there are no obvious power interruptions, loose connections, or signs of electrical damage. If safe and not connected to a patient, power cycle the system.

steps.title: Confirm Whether the Alarm Returns After Reboot
steps.instructions: Allow the system to complete startup after rebooting. Run the normal clinical checkout or pre-use test process according to hospital policy. Watch for return of Vent Fail. Monitoring Only or VENT FLOW VALVE FAIL CURRENT.

steps.title: Check for External or Setup-Related Contributors
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing circuit, absorber, accessible ventilator components, external hoses, filters, and breathing system setup. Look for disconnected or kinked hoses, blocked filters, damaged accessories, improper seating, contamination, fluid intrusion, heat, odor, or debris.

steps.title: Remove the Machine from Service if the Alarm Persists
steps.instructions: If the alarm returns after reboot or during checkout, do not continue attempting clinical use. Label the machine unavailable for patient use and notify the clinical area that automatic ventilation should not be trusted.

steps.title: Perform Bench Evaluation Using Service Software
steps.instructions: On the bench, use the appropriate GE service software pathway if authorized. Use Vent Flow and Pressure Diagnosis. Increase Flow Valve counts and observe Flow Valve Current mA and Flow Valve Counts to determine whether current feedback changes appropriately.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Connections Within Service Scope
steps.instructions: If authorized, inspect accessible ventilator-related connections for loose connectors, damaged harnesses, contamination, overheating, or poor seating after recent service. Do not perform deep internal repair beyond your training, tools, or service authorization.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after reboot, service checks are normal, and the machine passes required checkout, return the device to service according to facility policy. If the alarm returns at any point, remove the machine from service again.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed Vent Fail. Monitoring Only with alarm condition VENT FLOW VALVE FAIL CURRENT during startup, checkout, or attempted ventilation.
CCR.Cause: Incorrect current feedback condition reported from ventilator flow valve. Reboot and external checks performed. Breathing circuit, filters, hoses, power connection, and visible setup issues checked. Persistent fault indicates possible internal ventilator flow valve feedback, valve control, wiring, or ventilator system issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from patient use. Rebooted system and checked for alarm recurrence. Inspected external setup, breathing circuit, filters, hoses, and accessible connections. Machine labeled Out of Service and escalated for bench evaluation using Vent Flow and Pressure Diagnosis to evaluate Flow Valve Current mA and Flow Valve Counts.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: VENT VALVE POWER FAIL
description: Troubleshooting guide for the GE Healthcare Aisys VENT VALVE POWER FAIL alarm when ventilator valve power is outside range and the machine may be limited to monitoring only.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from active patient use if ventilation support is affected or the machine displays Vent Fail. Monitoring Only. Have anesthesia staff ventilate manually or move the patient to another functioning anesthesia machine or ventilator as clinically appropriate. Do not continue using the machine for patient ventilation with an unresolved ventilator power failure.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is VENT VALVE POWER FAIL. Check whether the displayed message includes Vent Fail. Monitoring Only. Note whether the alarm occurs at startup, during checkout, when entering ventilation mode, or intermittently during operation.

steps.title: Translate the Alarm Into the Real-World Problem
steps.instructions: Treat this as a ventilator power/control issue. The nominal 12.5 V ventilator valve supply may be detected below 11.3 VDC or above 13.13 VDC. Possible affected areas include the Vent Interface Board, ACB-to-VIB cabling, VIB-to-pan harness, or downstream ventilator valve load.

steps.title: Check External and Simple Causes First
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is connected to a known good AC outlet. Check that the power cord is fully seated and not damaged. Verify the machine powers on normally and does not show other power-related alarms. Inspect visible external cables, rear connections, and accessible areas for loose connections, damage, impact, or fluid exposure.

steps.title: Run a Machine Checkout if Safe to Do So
steps.instructions: With the machine removed from patient use, attempt the appropriate pre-use checkout or system test. Watch for when the alarm appears. Note whether the alarm occurs immediately, only during ventilator checks, or only when ventilation is attempted.

steps.title: Access Service Software Diagnosis
steps.instructions: Use GE service software according to your facility’s service process. Access Vent Interface Board Power Diagnosis. View Vent Int Bd 10VA Voltage and Vent Valve 10VA status. Use the diagnostic data to isolate the fault instead of guessing at parts.

steps.title: Check Vent Interface Board 10VA Voltage
steps.instructions: If Vent Int Bd 10VA reads OK, continue checking the Vent Valve 10VA status. If Vent Int Bd 10VA reads Fail from the VIB but reads OK from the ACB, suspect the connection path between the ACB and VIB.

steps.title: Compare VIB Voltage Against Vent Valve 10VA Status
steps.instructions: If the VIB voltage reads OK but the Vent Valve 10VA reads Fail, disconnect the VIB-to-pan connector harness if appropriate for your service level. Recheck whether the failure continues. If the failure clears, suspect the harness, pan connection, or downstream valve-related load. If the failure continues with the harness disconnected, suspect the Vent Interface Board.

steps.title: Inspect and Reseat Related Cabling
steps.instructions: Power the device down and keep it removed from service before inspecting internal-access cabling. Inspect accessible cabling between the ACB and VIB, the VIB-to-pan connector harness, and ventilator-related connector points. Look for loose connectors, bent pins, damaged wiring, pinched harnesses, fluid intrusion, overheating, odor, or discoloration.

steps.title: Recheck the Alarm After Cable Inspection
steps.instructions: Reconnect any inspected harnesses securely. Power the machine back on. Run the same Vent Interface Board Power Diagnosis again. Confirm whether the VENT VALVE POWER FAIL alarm returns. If the alarm clears, run checkout successfully, verify ventilation operation, document the repair, and return the machine to service only after all required testing passes.

steps.title: Isolate Likely Device-Level Failure
steps.instructions: If Vent Int Bd 10VA reads Fail from the VIB but OK from the ACB, focus on cabling between the ACB and VIB. If VIB voltage reads OK but Vent Valve 10VA remains Fail with the VIB-to-pan connector harness disconnected, replace or escalate replacement of the VIB according to your service process.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported VENT VALVE POWER FAIL alarm with displayed message Vent Fail. Monitoring Only. Machine unavailable for normal mechanical ventilation.
CCR.Cause: Service software Vent Interface Board Power Diagnosis indicated ventilator valve 10VA power fault. External power checks completed. Further isolation pointed toward VIB, ACB-to-VIB cabling, VIB-to-pan harness, or downstream ventilator valve power path.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from service. Verified reported alarm and monitoring-only condition. Checked external power and visible connections. Ran Vent Interface Board Power Diagnosis and documented Vent Int Bd 10VA / Vent Valve 10VA findings. Inspected accessible cabling. Unit remained out of service for VIB, cabling, or ventilator power path repair evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: VENT VALVE 10VA OVER CURRENT
description: Troubleshooting guide for the GE Healthcare Aisys VENT VALVE 10VA OVER CURRENT alarm caused by abnormal inspiratory flow valve current feedback that may affect ventilator control.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the machine from patient use if the alarm is active, recurring, or associated with abnormal ventilation. Ensure the patient is ventilated using an alternate safe method if needed. Do not continue clinical use if the ventilator cannot control inspiratory flow reliably.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is VENT VALVE 10VA OVER CURRENT. Identify whether the alarm occurs during power-up, checkout, ventilation startup, active ventilation, or intermittently during use. Record any associated ventilator symptoms or checkout failures.

steps.title: Check Power and Basic External Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is connected to a known-good AC outlet, verify the power cord is secure, and check for recent power interruption, breaker trip, or unusual startup behavior.

steps.title: Check Breathing System Setup
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing circuit, absorber, bag arm, patient circuit connections, filters, and accessories for incorrect setup, blockage, kinks, saturation, or restriction.

steps.title: Perform or Review Machine Checkout
steps.instructions: Run or review the anesthesia machine checkout if the machine is not connected to a patient. Record whether the alarm occurs during checkout and note any additional failed checkout items or ventilator messages.

steps.title: Use Service Software for Vent Flow and Pressure Diagnosis
steps.instructions: If qualified and permitted by facility procedure, use Service Software / Vent Flow & Pressure Diagnosis. Increase Flow Valve counts while observing Flow Valve Current mA and Flow Valve Counts to determine whether current feedback tracks normally.

steps.title: Remove From Service if Unresolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm repeats or flow valve current feedback remains abnormal after external checks, remove the machine from service, label it Out of Service, and escalate for qualified repair or GE service evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported VENT VALVE 10VA OVER CURRENT alarm. Machine removed from patient use for evaluation.
CCR.Cause: Alarm reproduced during evaluation. External breathing circuit setup, power connection, filters, and obvious restrictions checked. Service Software / Vent Flow & Pressure Diagnosis used to observe Flow Valve Current mA and Counts. Findings indicate likely internal inspiratory flow valve feedback, valve control, wiring, or ventilator hardware issue.
CCR.Resolution: Machine removed from service and labeled Out of Service. Failed condition documented. Unit referred for qualified anesthesia machine repair or GE service evaluation. Machine not returned to clinical use due to unresolved ventilator valve current alarm.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: VENT SIB 10VA OVER CURRENT
description: Troubleshooting VENT SIB 10VA OVER CURRENT on the GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine, including patient safety, external checks, alarm log review, VIB power harness isolation, VIB replacement, ACB escalation, and final checkout.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if the alarm occurs during setup, checkout, or active operation. Have anesthesia staff move to a backup anesthesia machine or alternate ventilation method as clinically appropriate. Do not continue using the machine for patient ventilation with an unresolved ventilator electronics alarm.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the alarm or logged event is exactly VENT SIB 10VA OVER CURRENT. Check whether it appears during startup, system checkout, ventilation, intermittent use, or only in the error log. Observe for ventilator failure, startup failure, checkout failure, repeated alarm after reboot, or loss of normal ventilation function.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Check for Simple External Issues
steps.instructions: Power the anesthesia machine down using the normal shutdown process, confirm the system is removed from patient use, verify proper AC power, inspect the power cord and outlet, restart the system, and observe whether the alarm returns.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Physical or Environmental Causes
steps.instructions: Inspect externally for fluid intrusion, recent cleaning saturation, physical impact, loose panels, burning smell, unusual heat, recent service activity, or visible cable and connector damage. If fluid intrusion, burning smell, or overheating is found, stop troubleshooting and remove the machine from service.

steps.title: Review the Alarm Log
steps.instructions: Access the error or alarm log through normal Clinical Engineering access. Confirm whether VENT SIB 10VA OVER CURRENT is current, repeated, or historic, and look for related ventilator, VIB, SIB, power, or ACB events.

steps.title: Isolate the VIB Power Harness
steps.instructions: With the machine powered off and removed from service, disconnect the VIB power harness according to approved service access procedures. Restart the system and check whether the VENT SIB 10VA OVER CURRENT error reappears in the log.

steps.title: Replace or Escalate Based on the Isolation Result
steps.instructions: If the error clears with the VIB power harness disconnected, suspect and replace the VIB, then retest. If the error persists with the VIB isolated, suspect the ACB or related internal control circuitry and remove the machine from service for board replacement or bench evaluation.

steps.title: Retest Before Returning to Service
steps.instructions: After corrective action, restart the machine, confirm the alarm no longer appears, run required anesthesia machine checkout and ventilator functional testing, verify normal ventilation, and review the error log for repeat entries before returning the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine reported VENT SIB 10VA OVER CURRENT alarm. Device removed from service due to ventilator electronics fault concern.
CCR.Cause: Alarm confirmed in system log. External power and visible condition checked. VIB power harness isolation performed. Alarm cleared with VIB disconnected, indicating likely VIB fault, or persisted with VIB disconnected, indicating likely ACB/internal control board fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from patient use. Verified reported alarm behavior and checked external power and physical condition. Disconnected VIB power harness and restarted system for isolation. Replaced VIB if error cleared during isolation, or escalated for ACB replacement or bench repair if error persisted. Retested system and verified alarm status before final disposition.

title: GE Healthcare Aisys Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: Circuit check failed
description: Troubleshooting Circuit check failed during GE Healthcare Aisys system checkout when the machine cannot complete the circuit test due to possible setup, leak, obstruction, accessory, gas supply, or device-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use until the checkout issue is corrected. Do not use the machine for a patient case if the circuit check cannot be completed successfully. Provide another anesthesia machine or alternate approved setup if the room needs to remain operational.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed message is Circuit check failed. Identify whether the failure occurs during full system checkout, breathing circuit checkout, after circuit changes, or after replacing accessories.

steps.title: Follow the On-Screen Suggested Action First
steps.instructions: Read the specific suggested action displayed with the checkout failure. Correct the listed issue if provided, then repeat the system checkout.

steps.title: Check the Breathing Circuit Setup
steps.instructions: Inspect the inspiratory and expiratory limbs, circuit connections, patient Y-piece, hoses, filters, HMEs, and adapters. Confirm all parts are correctly routed, fully seated, and free of leaks or restrictions.

steps.title: Check the Manual Bag and Bag Arm Setup
steps.instructions: Inspect the manual ventilation bag, bag connection, bag arm, bag port, and APL valve. Confirm the bag is secure, intact, and correctly configured for checkout.

steps.title: Check the Absorber Canister and Breathing System Seating
steps.instructions: Verify the absorber canister is installed, seated, locked, and not cracked. Check gaskets, seals, latches, and seating surfaces for damage, debris, or absorbent dust.

steps.title: Check Disposable Accessories and Filters
steps.instructions: Inspect recently changed filters, HME filters, sampling adapters, circuit adapters, checkout plugs, and caps. Replace questionable parts with known-good accessories and repeat checkout.

steps.title: Check for Obstruction or Flow Restriction
steps.instructions: Look for kinked hoses, crushed tubing, blocked filters, occluded patient connections, incorrectly installed valves or adapters, and foreign material in ports.

steps.title: Check Fresh Gas and Gas Supply Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm wall gas connections, pipeline pressures, O2 supply, cylinder backup status, and fresh gas setup if prompted by checkout.

steps.title: Repeat Checkout With a Known-Good Circuit
steps.instructions: Install a known-good circuit, known-good bag, and correct checkout plug or test setup. Run the system checkout again to separate accessory/setup issues from machine-related failure.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Visually inspect connection points and the breathing system area for broken ports, damaged latches, cracked plastic, loose fittings, missing seals, liquid intrusion, absorbent dust, debris, or visible flow sensor area damage.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Accessory, Setup, Environmental, or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Use the results of the previous checks to isolate the failure source. Escalate as device-related only if the failure repeats with known-good accessories and proper setup.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the circuit check passes after correction, complete the full required checkout, confirm no related alarms remain, document the correction, and return the machine to service.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Aisys anesthesia machine displayed Circuit check failed during system checkout. Machine could not complete checkout and was removed from patient use.
CCR.Cause: Circuit check failure confirmed during checkout. Breathing circuit setup, bag connection, absorber seating, filters, gas supply, and accessory condition were inspected. Failure was caused by a loose breathing circuit connection.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated breathing circuit connections, verified bag and absorber installation, repeated system checkout, and confirmed checkout passed without further alarms. Returned machine to service.

title: GE Healthcare Giraffe Warmer Infant Warmer - Radiant Heater Output Failure
description: Troubleshooting inadequate or absent radiant heat output caused by power, operating mode, probe, obstruction, alarm, or external equipment conditions.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If an infant is occupying the warmer and heat output is inadequate, notify clinical staff immediately and transfer the patient to an approved functioning warmer or alternate thermal support device. Do not perform troubleshooting while an infant remains dependent on a warmer with questionable heater output. Expected outcome: The patient receives reliable thermal support before equipment evaluation begins. Why it matters: The Giraffe Warmer provides controlled radiant heat for infants who may be unable to maintain body temperature independently.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: With the unit removed from patient use, confirm whether the complaint is: No apparent radiant heat output. Heater output remains unexpectedly low. Heater output bar indicates power, but no warming is detected. Unit displays an alarm, system failure, or heater-related message. Record the exact alarm message, displayed heater power percentage, operating mode, and when the failure occurs. Expected outcome: The reported problem is verified and accurately documented before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: 3. Inspect the Unit for Obvious Safety Concerns
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the heater head, reflector area, bed surface, side panels, power cord, plug, and control panel for damage, contamination, loose components, unusual discoloration, burning odor, or evidence of overheating. Confirm the heater path above the mattress is not blocked by covers, equipment, stored items, or accessories. Expected outcome: No physical damage, obstruction, odor, or heat-related concern is present. Stop if abnormal: If the unit has damage, unusual odor, visible overheating, or loose heater components, remove it from service without attempting operation.

steps.title: 4. Verify AC Power and Startup Operation
steps.instructions: Confirm the power cord is fully seated at the warmer and plugged into a known-working hospital-grade outlet. Check that the mains power switch is on and that the display completes startup normally. Test the outlet using an approved method or move the warmer to a verified working receptacle. Expected outcome: The warmer powers on normally without power-related alarms or interruptions. If resolved: If proper power restores expected operation, complete an operational verification before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: 5. Verify the Warmer Is in an Appropriate Heating Mode
steps.instructions: Confirm the warmer is operating in Manual Mode or Baby Mode, not in a standby, interrupted, or alarm-limited state. For troubleshooting off-patient, place the unit in Manual Mode according to facility practice and set a moderate heater power level sufficient to verify whether heater output responds. In Manual Mode, heater output is controlled by the selected heater power percentage; in Baby Mode, output is regulated using the connected skin temperature probe and selected temperature setting. Expected outcome: The heater power indication responds to the selected Manual Mode setting and the heater begins producing detectable warmth. If resolved: If the issue was caused by an incorrect mode or setting, document the finding and complete safety verification before release.

steps.title: 6. Check the Skin Temperature Probe Condition When Failure Occurs in Baby Mode
steps.instructions: If the complaint occurred in Baby Mode, inspect the skin temperature probe and cable for damaged insulation, contamination, bent connector contacts, intermittent connection, or an incorrect/unrecognized probe. Disconnect and reconnect the approved probe securely. When available, substitute a known-good compatible skin temperature probe to determine whether the original probe or cable caused the heating complaint. Expected outcome: The warmer recognizes the skin probe without probe alarms and regulates heater output appropriately in Baby Mode. If resolved: Replace the defective probe or accessory, document the cause, and verify normal operation.

steps.title: 7. Observe Heater Output and Alarm Behavior Off-Patient
steps.instructions: With no patient in the warmer, observe whether the heater power display increases when heat is requested and whether warmth is produced at the mattress area without alarms or abnormal odor. Do not touch the heater element or reflector area. Do not leave the warmer operating unattended during testing. Expected outcome: The unit produces appropriate radiant warmth when commanded and does not generate heater, power, or system-failure alarms. Stop if abnormal: If the display calls for heat but no warmth is produced, output is intermittent, or any heater/system fault appears, discontinue testing and remove the unit from service.

steps.title: 8. Perform the Approved Preoperational or Functional Checkout
steps.instructions: After correcting any external issue, complete the applicable preoperational or functional checkout required by your department and the manufacturer’s instructions before returning the warmer to clinical use. GE service information states that preoperational checkout must not be performed with a patient occupying the unit, and equipment that fails any portion of checkout must be removed from use and repaired. Expected outcome: The warmer passes the required checkout and provides reliable heater output without active faults.

CCR.Complaint: NICU staff reported that the GE Giraffe Warmer powered on but did not provide expected radiant heat during patient preparation.
CCR.Cause: Unit displayed commanded heater output in Manual Mode, but no detectable radiant warming occurred after confirming proper AC power, clear heater path, and normal external connections.
CCR.Resolution: Removed Giraffe Warmer from service, labeled unit Out of Service, and routed it for bench evaluation of the radiant heater and associated control circuitry.

helpfulDetails: Skin temperature probe connected, reseated, or substituted.
Presence of unusual odor, discoloration, sound, or heat.

title: GE Healthcare Giraffe Warmer Infant Warmer - SKIN TEMPERATURE PROBE ERROR
description: Troubleshooting probe-related alarms, missing or unstable skin temperature readings, and incorrect Baby Mode operation caused by external probe or connection problems.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the warmer is in use and the skin temperature probe error occurs, have clinical staff move the infant to a safe alternate warming method or another verified warmer before troubleshooting. Do not rely on Baby Mode while the device has an unresolved skin temperature probe issue, because Baby Mode uses the skin temperature probe reading to regulate heater output. Expected outcome: The infant is safely supported without dependence on the affected warmer.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Power on the warmer while it is not being used on a patient and verify the reported behavior. Document whether the unit displays: A skin temperature probe-related message or alarm; Confirm Probe Jack Connection when the probe is disconnected or not recognized; No baby temperature reading; An intermittent, implausible, or fluctuating temperature value; Inability to operate correctly in Baby Mode. GE training material identifies Confirm Probe Jack Connection as an alarm associated with an unplugged skin temperature probe jack. Expected outcome: The exact alarm message and operating condition are confirmed before parts are exchanged.

steps.title: 3. Confirm the Correct Probe Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Verify that the connected skin temperature probe is intended for the Giraffe Warmer and is approved by facility policy. GE HealthCare lists its Giraffe Panda disposable infant skin temperature probe as compatible with the Giraffe Warmer and Panda Warmers. Expected outcome: An incompatible or incorrect accessory is ruled out. If the error resolves after installing the correct probe, stop troubleshooting and document the correction.

steps.title: 4. Inspect the Skin Temperature Probe Externally
steps.instructions: Disconnect the probe and inspect the accessible portions of the probe, cable, and connector for: Bent, recessed, loose, or contaminated connector contacts; Cracked connector housing; Cuts, pinches, kinks, stretching, or exposed wiring; Moisture, residue, or signs of improper cleaning; Damage at the probe tip or cable strain relief. Do not use a damaged reusable probe or a suspect disposable probe for clinical operation. Expected outcome: The probe is visibly intact, clean, and free of external damage. If damage is found, replace the probe with a known-good compatible probe and retest. If the error clears, stop.

steps.title: 5. Reseat the Probe Connection
steps.instructions: Insert the skin temperature probe fully into the appropriate probe jack. Confirm the connector is seated securely and is not loose or easily disturbed. Gently observe whether the displayed reading or alarm changes when the connector is stationary. Do not manipulate the connection aggressively. Expected outcome: A properly seated probe produces a stable recognized connection without a probe-jack alarm. If reseating clears the error and the reading remains stable, stop troubleshooting after completing functional verification.

steps.title: 6. Check for Probe Attachment or Setup Issues
steps.instructions: If the original complaint occurred during use, ask whether the probe was: Dislodged from the infant; Pulled by linens, tubing, or repositioning; Wet, soiled, or improperly secured; Connected during a cleaning or patient turnover process; Used without the appropriate heat-reflective probe cover when required by clinical protocol. Probe attachment issues may cause inaccurate or unstable readings even when the warmer recognizes the probe. Expected outcome: A clinical setup issue is identified or ruled out without troubleshooting on an active patient.

steps.title: 7. Test With a Known-Good Compatible Probe
steps.instructions: With the warmer removed from patient use, connect a known-good compatible skin temperature probe. Observe whether: The probe-related alarm clears; A reasonable and stable temperature value appears; Baby Mode accepts the probe input without immediately generating the same error. Expected outcome: The test separates a failed accessory from a device-side probe input problem. If the known-good probe works properly: Remove the original probe from use and document it as defective or suspect. If the same error remains with a known-good probe: Continue to the next step.

steps.title: 8. Inspect the Warmer Probe Jack Externally
steps.instructions: Inspect the accessible probe jack on the warmer for: Debris, fluid residue, or cleaning solution contamination; Loose mounting or movement of the jack; Bent or damaged internal contact appearance visible without disassembly; Evidence that the connector cannot seat fully. Do not insert tools into the jack or attempt internal repair during this troubleshooting process. Expected outcome: The external jack condition is verified. If contamination or visible damage prevents a reliable connection, remove the warmer from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: 9. Verify Stable Temperature Display Off Patient
steps.instructions: Using the known-good probe and an approved facility method for functional verification, confirm that the displayed skin temperature is stable and plausible. Do not return the warmer to service solely because an alarm clears; the probe input must also display reliably. Expected outcome: The warmer consistently recognizes the probe and displays a stable temperature input. If the value is missing, erratic, or unreasonable with a known-good probe, remove the warmer from service.

steps.title: 10. Complete Functional Checkout Before Return to Service
steps.instructions: After correcting an external issue, perform the applicable pre-use or post-repair functional checkout according to hospital procedure and available manufacturer documentation. Confirm: No active skin temperature probe alarm remains; The probe connection is stable; The displayed baby temperature is plausible; The warmer is safe for clinical return. Expected outcome: The warmer passes functional verification before being released for use.

CCR.Complaint: NICU reported the GE Healthcare Giraffe Warmer displayed a skin temperature probe error and would not provide a stable baby temperature reading in Baby Mode.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the connected skin temperature probe cable damaged near the connector, and the alarm cleared when a known-good compatible probe was installed.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the damaged probe from use, verified stable skin temperature display with a replacement probe, completed functional checkout, and returned the warmer to service.

helpfulDetails: Alarm or message displayed, including Confirm Probe Jack Connection
Operating mode when the issue occurred
Whether the unit was in use on a patient

title: GE Healthcare Giraffe Warmer Infant Warmer - Alarm Silence Sensor or Alarm Fault
description: Troubleshooting hands-free alarm silence failure, alarm acknowledgement problems, abnormal alarm operation, or alarm-related system fault indications.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the warmer is in use with an infant, notify clinical staff and transfer the patient to another appropriate warming device before troubleshooting. Do not continue using a warmer with unreliable alarm behavior, an active system failure, or an alarm that cannot be properly acknowledged. Expected outcome: The infant is safely supported on alternate equipment before testing begins. Why it matters: The Giraffe Warmer uses audible and visual alarms to notify staff of unsafe conditions. Alarm failure can delay response to a clinically significant event.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Alarm Problem
steps.instructions: Power on the warmer while it is not in patient use and identify the reported symptom: Hands-free alarm silence sensor does not respond. Alarm will not silence when expected. Alarm silences intermittently or without intentional activation. Audible alarm is weak, absent, abnormal, or continuous. A system failure message or alarm-related error is displayed. Record the exact alarm message, displayed fault, and whether the issue occurs at startup or during operation. Expected outcome: The complaint is verified and clearly distinguished as a sensor-response issue, alarm-output issue, or system fault.

steps.title: 3. Inspect the Hands-Free Alarm Silence Sensor Area
steps.instructions: Inspect the alarm light/sensor area for tape, stickers, residue, dust, dried cleaning solution, cracks, or physical damage. Clean the external sensor surface according to approved cleaning procedures, then allow it to dry completely. Expected outcome: The sensor area is unobstructed, clean, dry, and free of obvious damage. Why it matters: The Giraffe Warmer uses a hands-free alarm silence sensor near the alarm light; contamination or obstruction can interfere with infrared sensing. GE describes alarm silencing by waving a hand in front of the sensor.

steps.title: 4. Test the Hands-Free Alarm Silence Response
steps.instructions: Create or observe a safe test alarm condition only according to facility practice and approved equipment testing methods. Pass a hand slowly within approximately six inches in front of the alarm light/sensor area. Observe whether the alarm acknowledges or silences as intended. Expected outcome: The hands-free sensor consistently responds when a hand is passed in front of the sensor area. If the sensor works normally: The issue may have been caused by obstruction or contamination. Complete required operational verification and document the finding before returning the warmer to service. If the sensor does not respond: Continue troubleshooting. Why it matters: GE training material identifies the hands-free alarm silence function as operating when a hand is passed within six inches in front of the alarm light.

steps.title: 5. Check for Unintentional Sensor Activation
steps.instructions: Verify that blankets, supply bags, cables, equipment covers, hanging accessories, or staff movement are not repeatedly passing in front of the sensor area. Observe the unit during an alarm test to determine whether alarms silence unexpectedly without intentional hand movement. Expected outcome: No nearby object or workflow condition is unintentionally activating the hands-free sensor. If the problem is resolved: Reposition or remove the interfering item, complete operational verification, and document the correction.

steps.title: 6. Verify Alarm Audio and Visual Indication
steps.instructions: During a safe test alarm condition, confirm that: The audible alarm is clearly heard. The alarm light activates appropriately. The displayed alarm message corresponds to the condition. Alarm acknowledgement does not prevent recognition of a continuing alarm condition. Expected outcome: Audible, visual, and displayed alarm indications operate consistently. If alarm audio is absent, distorted, unusually quiet, continuous, or inconsistent: Remove the warmer from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: 7. Check Basic Power Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord, plug, strain relief, and outlet connection for looseness or damage. Confirm the warmer is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet and powers on normally without intermittent resets. Expected outcome: Stable power is present and the warmer completes normal startup without alarm-system symptoms caused by interrupted power. If the issue resolves after correcting an external power problem: Complete functional testing and document the cause and resolution.

steps.title: 8. Review for System Failure or Startup Fault Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe the display during startup and alarm testing for any system failure message, blank display with high-priority alarm, repeated restart, or non-silenceable alarm. The Giraffe/Panda warmer service documentation describes abnormal system operation as capable of generating a high-priority alarm and system failure indication; a detected startup failure is recorded and initiates a high-priority alarm. Expected outcome: No system failure indication or unresolved high-priority alarm is present. If a system failure or unresolved high-priority alarm occurs: Stop troubleshooting, record the displayed information, and remove the device from service.

steps.title: 9. Determine Whether the Issue Is External or Requires Repair
steps.instructions: If cleaning the sensor area, removing nearby obstructions, verifying power, and performing a safe alarm-function test do not restore reliable operation, do not proceed with internal component replacement at the clinical troubleshooting level. Expected outcome: A device with unresolved alarm silence sensor or alarm-function failure is identified for repair evaluation rather than returned to patient use.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the GE Healthcare Giraffe Warmer alarm would not silence when a hand was passed in front of the hands-free alarm silence sensor.
CCR.Cause: Verified that the alarm activated normally, but the hands-free alarm silence sensor did not respond after the sensor window was cleaned and external obstructions were removed.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Giraffe Warmer from service, applied an Out of Service label, documented the failed alarm-silence test, and routed the unit for bench evaluation of the alarm sensor/alarm system.

helpfulDetails: Patient removed from device before troubleshooting
Exact alarm message or system failure indication
Whether failure occurred at startup or during operation
Alarm audio present, absent, weak, or continuous
Hands-free sensor area cleaned and inspected
Sensor response when hand passed within approximately six inches
Outlet tested or known-good outlet used
Any unusual sounds, heat, odor, or intermittent resets

title: GE Healthcare Giraffe Warmer Infant Warmer - Integrated Scale Failure
description: Troubleshooting an integrated in-bed scale that will not weigh, displays incorrect weight, drifts, or reports a scale-related fault.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Do not troubleshoot or verify scale accuracy with an infant on the warmer.

If the scale reading is clinically needed, have clinical staff use another approved weighing method or transfer the patient to appropriate equipment before troubleshooting.

Expected outcome: The patient is safely managed without relying on an inaccurate or failed scale.

Why it matters: Weight data may influence clinical decisions, and GE identifies the integrated in-bed scale as the warmer’s bedside patient weighing option.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Failure
steps.instructions:
Obtain the exact complaint from staff and attempt to reproduce the issue with the warmer unoccupied.

Determine whether the reported problem is:

Scale option unavailable or not recognized.
Weight cycle will not start or complete.
Displayed weight is obviously inaccurate.
Weight reading drifts or fluctuates while the bed is undisturbed.
Scale-related message or fault appears on the display.

Document any displayed message, intermittent behavior, or conditions under which the failure occurs.

Expected outcome: The scale issue is clearly identified and reproducible before parts or accessories are considered.

If the issue cannot be reproduced: Document the reported symptom, perform a safe functional check, and return the device only if scale function is verified acceptable under facility procedure.

steps.title: 3. Inspect the Scale Platform, Mattress Area, and Bed Surface
steps.instructions:
Remove linens and accessories as needed to inspect the in-bed scale area.

Check for:

Debris or fluid beneath the mattress or scale platform.
Damaged, cracked, warped, or incorrectly seated scale components.
Objects contacting the mattress, scale platform, bed frame, side panels, or accessory trays.
Any accessory or cable applying force to the bed surface.

Reinstall the mattress and applicable bed components correctly after inspection.

Expected outcome: The scale platform is clean, seated correctly, undamaged, and free to measure weight without mechanical interference.

Why it matters: Physical contact or interference can cause inaccurate, unstable, or failed weight readings.

steps.title: 4. Verify the Bed Is Level and Undisturbed
steps.instructions:
Position the warmer on a stable floor surface and confirm the bed is level using the warmer’s level indicator.

Ensure no one is leaning on the warmer, touching the bed, moving attached cables, or contacting accessories during a weight attempt.

Expected outcome: The scale produces a stable reading without movement-related variation.

Why it matters: The Giraffe Warmer includes continuous bed tilt with a bubble level indicator, and an out-of-level or disturbed bed can affect accurate weight verification.

steps.title: 5. Inspect the Scale Cable and External Connection
steps.instructions:
With the warmer removed from patient use, inspect the accessible in-bed scale cable and connector area.

Check for:

Loose or partially seated scale connection.
Bent pins, damaged connector housing, or contamination.
Pinched, pulled, cut, or strained cable.
Scale cable routed across or against a corner tray or other bed component.

Reseat an accessible external connection only when permitted by department procedure and the connector is undamaged.

Expected outcome: The scale cable is intact, properly routed, and securely connected without mechanical strain.

Why it matters: GE service documentation identifies loose/damaged scale connectors, bent pins, and scale-cable interference with bed components as scale inspection points.

steps.title: 6. Power Cycle and Recheck Scale Recognition
steps.instructions:
After completing external inspection, power the warmer off according to facility procedure, wait briefly, then restart it.

Observe startup for scale-related faults and determine whether the scale function becomes available.

Expected outcome: The warmer recognizes the integrated scale and no scale fault remains present.

If the scale is restored: Perform an accuracy verification before returning the device to clinical use.

steps.title: 7. Perform a Safe Functional Weight Check
steps.instructions:
With the bed empty, correctly assembled, level, and free of interference, perform a scale check using an approved known test weight according to Clinical Engineering procedure.

Observe whether the displayed weight:

Appears stable.
Corresponds appropriately to the known test weight.
Repeats consistently when the test is performed again.

Expected outcome: The scale displays a stable and accurate value within the acceptance criteria established by approved GE documentation or facility procedure.

Why it matters: GE service material includes center and off-center scale verification using a known test weight; failed verification indicates the scale is not suitable for clinical use.

steps.title: 8. Determine Whether Calibration or Repair Evaluation Is Required
steps.instructions:
If the scale is recognized but does not pass an approved accuracy verification, do not attempt to compensate for the error clinically.

If authorized by your department and appropriate for the device configuration, proceed only with the approved scale calibration or performance-verification process from current GE service documentation.

Do not perform internal load-cell, circuit-board, or wiring repairs as part of frontline troubleshooting.

Expected outcome: A scale that passes approved verification may be returned to service; a scale that fails remains out of service for repair evaluation.

If the scale does not calibrate or repeatedly fails testing: Stop troubleshooting and escalate for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the GE Giraffe Warmer integrated scale would not provide a stable infant weight and readings varied during repeated attempts.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the in-bed scale cable routed against the corner tray, creating mechanical interference with the scale platform.
CCR.Resolution: Rerouted the scale cable away from the bed tray, leveled the bed, completed known-weight verification successfully, and returned the warmer to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the integrated scale option was visible on the display.
Any scale-related fault message or abnormal screen behavior.
Condition of the mattress, clear plate, and scale platform.
Whether fluid, debris, or mechanical interference was found.
Scale cable condition and connector inspection results.
Approved test weight used and verification result.
Whether readings were stable, drifting, or intermittent.
Whether calibration or repair evaluation was required.
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service.

title: GE Healthcare Giraffe Warmer Infant Warmer - Resuscitation module, blender, or SpO₂ integration issue
description: Troubleshooting unavailable resuscitation support, incorrect blended oxygen, absent SpO₂ function, or accessory and supply issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the warmer is supporting an infant and the resuscitation module, blender, or SpO₂ function is unavailable or unreliable, notify clinical staff immediately. Provide alternate approved resuscitation support, oxygen delivery, and pulse oximetry monitoring before beginning troubleshooting. Do not troubleshoot neonatal resuscitation functions while they may be needed for active patient care. Expected outcome: The infant is supported by known-working alternate equipment before the affected Giraffe Warmer is evaluated. Why it matters: The Giraffe Warmer may be equipped with integrated T-piece or bag-and-mask resuscitation, an integrated blender, and SpO₂ monitoring options.

steps.title: Confirm the Installed Configuration and Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Verify which optional components are installed on the affected warmer: Resuscitation module present or absent; T-piece or bag-and-mask configuration; Integrated oxygen blender present or absent; Integrated SpO₂ monitoring present or absent. Ask staff what occurred: No gas flow; Incorrect or unstable oxygen concentration; Inability to obtain expected pressure or ventilation support; SpO₂ option unavailable, sensor not detected, or unreliable reading; Error message, alarm, or intermittent operation. Expected outcome: The complaint is matched to an installed option rather than assuming a failure of equipment not configured on the unit.

steps.title: Inspect the Device and Accessories Externally
steps.instructions: With the warmer removed from service, inspect the resuscitation and SpO₂ areas for: Damaged, loose, cracked, or missing connectors; Disconnected gas hoses; Damaged patient circuit, T-piece, mask, tubing, or suction accessories; Cracked flowmeter tube, damaged gauge, or loose control knobs; Damaged SpO₂ patient cable, sensor, extension cable, or connector; Evidence of liquid intrusion, unusual smell, impact damage, or contamination. Do not continue testing if physical damage or contamination could affect safe operation. Expected outcome: Easily visible damage or disconnection is identified before functional testing. If the problem is resolved by securely reconnecting an undamaged accessory, verify operation off-patient and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Warmer Power and Option Recognition
steps.instructions: Power the warmer on while it is not in patient use. Confirm that the display initializes normally and that no system failure or option-related fault is present. For an SpO₂ complaint, verify that the SpO₂ monitoring option appears available on the display when equipped. The warmer’s service checkout identifies oximetry as an installed option that should be recognized by the system. Expected outcome: The warmer powers on normally and recognizes the installed monitoring option. If the SpO₂ option is installed but does not appear, or a system fault remains active, remove the device from service and escalate for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Check Medical Gas Supply Connections
steps.instructions: For resuscitation or blender concerns, verify that the required medical gas hoses are properly connected to the correct wall outlets or approved gas source. Inspect the external gas hoses and fittings for: Loose connections; Damaged fittings; Kinks or compression; Incorrect outlet connection; Absent air or oxygen supply where both are required for blended output. The optional integrated blender is designed to provide adjustable oxygen concentration from 21% to 100% when properly supplied with medical gas inputs. Expected outcome: Correct external gas supplies are present, securely connected, and free of obvious damage. If reconnecting the correct supply restores expected function, complete an off-patient verification and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Evaluate Resuscitation Accessory Setup
steps.instructions: If the complaint involves no flow, low flow, unstable pressure, or ineffective resuscitation output, inspect the attached patient circuit and accessories. Check for: Kinked or occluded tubing; Loose circuit connection; Damaged or incorrectly assembled T-piece or bag-and-mask accessories; Blocked outlet fitting; Incorrect accessory attached to the configured resuscitation system; Disposable component that requires replacement. Use an approved test setup according to department procedure to determine whether flow or pressure responds normally after replacing suspect disposable accessories. Expected outcome: External circuit or accessory problems are corrected, or the problem remains isolated to the integrated resuscitation system. If a known-good approved accessory restores expected performance, document the failed accessory and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Blender Control and Output Concern
steps.instructions: If the complaint involves oxygen concentration, inspect the blender externally. Confirm that: The blender control knob is present and mechanically secure; The knob moves normally without looseness, binding, or excessive play; Required air and oxygen supplies are connected; Staff are not reporting inconsistent oxygen concentration despite correct external setup. Do not attempt internal blender adjustment or calibration during initial troubleshooting. Expected outcome: No visible control damage or external supply problem is present. If the blender knob is loose, damaged, or output remains questionable, remove the device from service for resuscitation-system bench evaluation.

steps.title: Troubleshoot the SpO₂ Connection Path
steps.instructions: For an SpO₂ integration complaint, check the monitoring path externally: Verify the SpO₂ patient cable is fully seated; Inspect the cable and sensor for cuts, crushed areas, bent pins, contamination, or damaged connector housings; Confirm the sensor type is compatible with the installed SpO₂ technology and approved for the warmer; Replace the sensor or patient cable with a known-good approved accessory, when available; Observe whether the display recognizes the connected sensor and clears the reported fault. Do not evaluate sensor performance on a patient while the monitoring function is considered unreliable. Expected outcome: A defective or disconnected external SpO₂ accessory is identified, or the failure remains with the installed monitoring system. If a known-good approved sensor or cable restores normal recognition, document the accessory replacement and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Perform an Off-Patient Operational Verification
steps.instructions: After correcting an external connection, supply, or accessory issue, verify the affected function while the unit remains out of clinical use: Resuscitation function responds as expected with the approved test setup; Blender control is secure and no concern remains regarding output selection; SpO₂ option recognizes approved accessories without an active fault; No system failure, alarm abnormality, gas leak concern, or intermittent behavior occurs. Return the warmer to service only when the affected option operates reliably and all required departmental checks are completed. Expected outcome: The repaired external issue is verified before clinical return.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Giraffe Warmer’s integrated resuscitation system was available, but the blender output was questionable and the SpO₂ sensor would not register.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the oxygen supply connected correctly, but the SpO₂ patient cable was damaged and the blender control remained loose during external inspection.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the damaged SpO₂ cable for verification, removed the warmer from service due to unresolved blender concern, labeled it Out of Service, and routed it for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Patient removed from affected warmer before troubleshooting
Installed option confirmed: resuscitation module, blender, and/or SpO₂
Medical air and oxygen sources checked
Flow, pressure, or oxygen concentration complaint reported
SpO₂ sensor and cable swapped with approved known-good accessories
Any unusual sound, leak indication, heat, odor, or physical damage
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: GE Healthcare Panda Warmer Infant Warmer - Alarm: Temp Probe Failure
description: Troubleshooting the Temp Probe Failure alarm when patient temperature input is unavailable or inconsistent, preventing reliable temperature monitoring or servo-controlled warming.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the warmer is being used for an infant, notify clinical staff immediately and move the patient to an appropriate alternate warming and temperature-monitoring method before troubleshooting. Do not rely on patient temperature readings or servo-controlled warming while Temp Probe Failure remains active.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: With the warmer removed from patient use, confirm that the display shows Temp Probe Failure. Document whether the alarm occurs at startup, when the patient probe is connected, when servo control is selected, during operation, or when the probe cable is moved. Observe for abnormal temperature display or repeated alarm activation.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Probe and External Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm that the correct compatible patient temperature probe is being used and fully seated. Inspect the probe, accessible cable, and connector for damage, contamination, fluid intrusion, bent contacts, or intermittent behavior. Disconnect and reconnect the probe securely, then recheck for the alarm.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Compatible Patient Probe
steps.instructions: Replace the suspected patient probe with a known-good compatible probe, when available, and observe whether Temp Probe Failure returns. If the alarm clears, remove the failed probe from use and document the replacement. If the alarm remains, do not return the warmer to clinical use.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible External Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm that the connector area is clean and dry and that the probe cable is not trapped, stretched, sharply bent, or exposed to recent fluid or transport damage. Document whether the alarm is constant or intermittent after external checks are completed.

steps.title: Verify Alarm Status After External Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: After reseating the probe or installing a known-good compatible probe, confirm whether Temp Probe Failure remains present. If the alarm has cleared, complete required operational verification according to facility procedure. If the alarm remains or returns, remove the warmer from service and escalate for repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Panda Warmer displayed Temp Probe Failure alarm during attempted servo-controlled warming. Patient temperature input was unavailable or inconsistent.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed Temp Probe Failure alarm. Original patient temperature probe showed visible cable damage and alarm remained associated with the probe until replaced with a known-good compatible probe.
CCR.Resolution: Removed warmer from patient use, replaced defective patient temperature probe with a known-good compatible probe, verified alarm cleared and temperature input operated normally, completed operational verification, and returned unit to service.

title: GE Healthcare Panda Warmer Infant Warmer - Alarm: Confirm Probe Jack Connection
description: Troubleshooting the Confirm Probe Jack Connection alarm when unreliable patient probe connection or temperature conditions may prevent dependable patient temperature monitoring and safe servo-controlled warming.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Notify clinical staff and move the infant to an approved backup warming device or alternate temperature monitoring method before troubleshooting. Do not rely on servo-controlled warming or displayed patient temperature while the alarm remains unresolved.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that the display shows Confirm Probe Jack Connection and document whether the alarm occurs at startup, after probe connection, when entering a patient-temperature-controlled mode, or intermittently during cable movement.

steps.title: Inspect and Reseat the Patient Probe Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the probe connector, cable, and jack for incomplete insertion, damage, looseness, contamination, moisture, debris, pinching, stretching, or cuts. Reseat the compatible probe and retest for stable temperature indication.

steps.title: Consider a Cold Patient Probe Condition
steps.instructions: Determine whether the probe may have been below 24°C. Allow a cold probe to warm to room temperature, reconnect it securely, and retest the warmer.

steps.title: Substitute a Known-Good Patient Probe
steps.instructions: Connect a compatible known-good patient probe and repeat the operating check. If the alarm clears with stable temperature indication, replace the defective probe and complete functional verification.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible External Connection Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm that the probe jack area is clean, dry, undamaged, and free of external strain or movement-related interruption. If the alarm persists after external causes are ruled out, remove the warmer from service for qualified bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Panda Warmer displayed Confirm Probe Jack Connection alarm during setup for infant warming. Staff reported patient temperature monitoring could not be confirmed.
CCR.Cause: Patient temperature probe was not fully seated in the probe jack. Alarm cleared after reseating the probe and confirming stable temperature indication.
CCR.Resolution: Removed warmer from patient use during evaluation, reseated the patient probe, confirmed alarm cleared, completed functional verification, and returned the warmer to service.

title: GE Healthcare Panda Warmer Infant Warmer - Error: Oximetry System Failure
description: Troubleshooting Oximetry System Failure when optional SpO2 monitoring is unavailable or unreliable because the oximetry communication path is not functioning normally.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the warmer is supporting an infant and SpO2 monitoring is required, notify clinical staff immediately and provide an alternate approved pulse oximetry monitoring method before troubleshooting. Do not rely on the Panda Warmer optional SpO2 function while Oximetry System Failure remains active.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that Oximetry System Failure is displayed. Document whether it occurs at startup, when an SpO2 accessory is connected, during attempted monitoring, or intermittently during operation. Note absent readings, intermittent function, unrecognized accessories, or repeated failure messages.

steps.title: Inspect External SpO2 Accessories and Connections
steps.instructions: Examine the sensor, patient cable, extension cable, and accessible external connector area for damage, contamination, fluid intrusion, loose connections, or bent contacts. Secure connections and substitute compatible known-good external accessories when available.

steps.title: Confirm Whether the Failure Is Isolated to SpO2
steps.instructions: In a safe non-patient test condition, determine whether the warmer otherwise functions normally while the SpO2 option remains unavailable. Document any additional abnormal behavior or whether the failure changes with external accessory connection or movement.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible External Connector Areas and Cable Routing
steps.instructions: Check visible SpO2 cable routing and accessible connector areas for strain, pinching, chafing, contamination, or intermittent contact. Do not perform internal disassembly during bedside troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm Resolution Before Return to Service
steps.instructions: After correcting an external accessory or connection issue, confirm the error no longer appears and verify normal SpO2 initialization and function using approved testing methods. Document the corrective action and final device status.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Panda Warmer displayed Oximetry System Failure. Optional SpO2 monitoring was unavailable or intermittent during startup and functional verification.
CCR.Cause: Inspection identified an external SpO2 cable connection that was not fully seated, resulting in loss of reliable optional SpO2 operation.
CCR.Resolution: Removed warmer from patient use, secured the external SpO2 connection, inspected associated accessories and visible cabling, completed functional verification, and confirmed Oximetry System Failure no longer returned. Device returned to service.

title: GE Healthcare Panda Warmer Infant Warmer - Error: Sys Fail 06 [0B XX 00 00] Over-Temperature Thermostat Open
description: Troubleshooting Sys Fail 06 [0B XX 00 00] when an open over-temperature thermostat prevents reliable radiant heat operation and requires the warmer to be removed from patient use until resolved.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately remove the warmer from patient use if Sys Fail 06 [0B XX 00 00] is displayed or radiant heat operation is interrupted or unreliable. Transfer the infant to another approved warming device according to clinical procedures before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: With the warmer removed from patient use, confirm that the display shows Sys Fail 06 [0B XX 00 00]. Document whether the failure occurs at startup, after heater operation, after prolonged use, or intermittently during warming.

steps.title: Inspect the Heater Head and Airflow Openings
steps.instructions: Power the warmer off and allow the heater head to cool. Inspect the heater duct and heater head vents for blankets, linens, covers, dust, debris, contamination, or visible damage. Remove only external obstructions that can be cleared without disassembly.

steps.title: Check the Operating Environment and Recent Use History
